bullets: An excess (deductible) requires the policyholder to meet the first tranche of any claim cost personally. This lowers the premium and creates an incentive for policyholders to seek treatment through the public system for minor conditions.

A co-insurance arrangement shares the claim cost in fixed proportions between insurer and policyholder (eg insurer pays 75%, policyholder pays 25%). Advantages: reduces moral hazard and claim exaggeration, lowers premiums. Disadvantages: insurer is involved in every claim (higher admin cost); no cap on policyholder liability.
NCD scheme: encourages policyholders to absorb small claims, reducing claims frequency and admin costs. Risk of being viewed as discouraging policyholders from seeking needed treatment.
Policy limit (maximum claim per year): limits insurer's liability and lowers premiums. Risk of bad publicity if cover runs out during serious illness, particularly for longer-serving policyholders.
Preferred provider arrangements: insurer directs policyholders to a restricted list of hospitals, buying in bulk to control costs.

key_terms.definition: The amount of each claim met by the policyholder before the insurer pays. Reduces premium and incentivises use of the public system for minor conditions.
A PMI condition where the policyholder pays a fixed proportion of each claim (eg 20%), with the insurer paying the remainder.
An arrangement where the insurer negotiates discounted rates with a network of hospitals; policyholders must use these hospitals to receive full coverage.

exam_qa.q: Why is A&E treatment usually not covered under PMI?
exam_qa.a: A&E is typically provided by the State and is free at the point of use. Private hospitals cannot reliably provide fast emergency infrastructure. The usual claims assessment and pre-authorisation process is impractical in emergencies. Patients may be incapacitated and unable to contact the insurer. Patients in emergency situations are generally less concerned about accommodation quality or provider choice.

subsections.title: Major Medical Expenses and Waiting List Plans
bullets: Major Medical Expenses (MME): pays a lump sum when the policyholder undergoes surgery from a specified list. The lump sum is sized to cover in-patient costs and incidentals. There is no cover for out-patient episodes. Benefit is fixed — simpler claims administration — but may not cover extreme surgical complications. Can be written as short-term (annually renewable) or long-term (reviewable premium) business.

MME vs CI: MME triggers on undergoing a specified operation (not necessarily life-threatening, eg hip replacement); CI triggers on diagnosis of a specified serious illness (not necessarily requiring an operation). Some overlap exists for conditions requiring major surgery (eg heart bypass), but they are fundamentally different in structure.

Waiting list plans: provide standard PMI benefits only if the State health service cannot provide treatment within a specified period (typically 6 weeks). If the State can provide timely treatment, no benefit is payable. Designed for policyholders whose primary motivation is to avoid waiting for treatment.

Budget plans: cheaper PMI products achieved by restricting out-patient cover (monetary limits, time restrictions, initial consultation only) or limiting the range of hospitals (preferred provider arrangements). These target lower socioeconomic groups priced out of comprehensive PMI.

key_terms.definition: A product paying a fixed lump sum when the policyholder undergoes one of a specified list of surgical procedures. Covers in-patient costs but not out-patient treatment.
A PMI product that only pays benefits if the State health service is unable to provide the required treatment within a specified period (typically 6 weeks).
A lower-cost PMI product achieved by restricting out-patient cover or limiting the range of hospitals, aimed at widening distribution beyond higher socioeconomic groups.

subsections.title: Health Cash Plans
bullets: Health cash plans provide defined cash benefits for a range of healthcare-related events (dental, optical, physiotherapy, maternity, hospitalisation, recuperation, hearing aids, consultations) for low premiums (as little as $2/week).
Benefits may be limited to a percentage of the medical bill (eg 50%) — a form of coinsurance. Benefits are bought in 'units' with proportionally higher premiums for higher benefit levels.

Health cash plans are typically community-rated — no individual underwriting. Full medical underwriting is impractical because: premiums are small (underwriting costs would represent too large a loading), the process delays acceptance, and simplicity is a key selling point.

A waiting period (often 6 months) applies before benefit eligibility to reduce anti-selection in the absence of full underwriting. No waiting period usually applies for accidents. A moratorium may exclude pre-existing conditions.
Profitability depends on: long-term policyholder loyalty (so initial expenses can be spread over many years), cost-efficient claims administration, and large volumes in force to spread fixed costs.

Community rating: premiums do not vary by individual characteristics (age, gender, medical history). This creates cross-subsidy between policyholders — older/sicker policyholders pay less than their expected cost, subsidised by younger/healthier ones. This creates scope for selection against the insurer by older/sicker lives.

Lifetime community rating: allows premiums to vary by age at entry but remain level thereafter (if renewed). Reduces anti-selection by giving all individuals an incentive to join early (rather than waiting until older).

key_terms.definition: A low-premium product paying defined cash benefits for a range of healthcare events. Community-rated, typically with a waiting period. Success depends on high volumes and renewal rates.
A rating system where all policyholders (or a defined subset) are charged the same premium, regardless of individual characteristics such as age, gender or health status.
An initial period during which claims related to pre-existing conditions are excluded. If no related treatment occurs during the moratorium, the exclusion is removed.
An initial policy period during which premiums are paid but no benefit is payable. Designed to reduce anti-selection where full medical underwriting is not performed.

exam_qa.q: Why is full underwriting not feasible for health cash plans?
exam_qa.a: Cash plans have small premiums, so the cost of full medical underwriting would represent a disproportionate loading. This would reduce sales and make it harder to cover fixed costs. Cash plans also appeal through their simplicity and speed of acceptance — underwriting delays act as an additional disincentive to applicants.

exam_qa.q: How does community rating lead to cross-subsidy and potential selection against the insurer?
exam_qa.a: Under community rating, all policyholders pay the same premium regardless of risk. Higher-risk individuals (older, sicker) pay less than their expected claims cost, subsidised by lower-risk individuals. Better risks may therefore switch to individually-rated insurers (which charge them lower premiums), leaving the community-rated insurer with a deteriorating risk pool — a classic adverse selection spiral.

subsections.title: Dental and Optical Plans
bullets: Dental plans can be included within comprehensive PMI, within health cash plans, or purchased as standalone cover. Two principal methods: (1) capitation basis — insurer and dentist agree a fixed sum per insured mouth per year (dentist bears the claims risk, insurer handles administration and covers emergency treatment only); (2) indemnity basis — insurer bears the claims risk and pays actual treatment costs, often subject to limits, excesses or coinsurance.

Optical plans provide cover for spectacles, contact lenses, eye tests and optical treatments. May include waiting periods and exclusions for pre-existing conditions. Most often found within cash plans or comprehensive PMI, but available standalone.

key_terms.definition: A dental insurance structure where the insurer pays the dentist a fixed annual amount per insured mouth. The dentist bears the risk of providing more treatment than the capitation covers.

subsections.title: Benefits and Exposure
bullets: Personal accident policies are short-term, annually renewable products providing lump sum benefits for bodily injury from an accident. Benefits are fixed amounts for specified injuries (loss of limb, sight, hearing, permanent total disability, accidental death) — not indemnity products because the loss of a limb cannot be financially quantified.
Benefits can cover the policyholder's family. Rider benefits for children are common, usually at a lower level.

The standard exposure measure is the person-year. Where family details are not available, the member-year (or employee-year) is used — the insurer charges an average family rate and expects to be right on average.
Rating factors are usually simple: occupation is the primary factor (demolition vs clerical work); age and gender are generally not used. Detailed medical history questions are not usually asked.
For group schemes, the insurer may only know the member's age and industry. The industry serves as a proxy for the underlying risk factor (occupation).

key_terms.definition: A short-term product providing fixed lump sum benefits for bodily injury resulting from an accident. Not an indemnity product as the financial value of physical loss cannot be objectively determined.

A measure of the amount of risk underlying each policy. For personal accident, this is the person-year (or member-year). Should be a good indicator of expected claim frequency and severity, and be objectively measurable.
A personal accident benefit payable if the policyholder is permanently and totally incapacitated as a result of an accident. May take months or years to confirm.

exam_qa.q: What are the main differences between MME and CI policy benefits?
exam_qa.a: MME triggers on undergoing a specified operation (which may or may not be for a serious illness). CI triggers on diagnosis of a specified serious illness (which may or may not require an operation). Some overlap exists where CI lists specific operations (eg heart bypass), but CI also covers conditions not requiring surgery, while MME covers non-life-threatening operations (eg hip replacement) that CI would not cover.

subsections.title: Claim Characteristics
bullets: Personal accident claims are usually reported quickly and settled without much delay — the incidence of an event is generally clear, reducing reporting delays, and claim costs are predetermined by the benefit schedule.
Claims can be very large (often much higher than typical health insurance claims). Claim frequency tends to be reasonably stable.
Exception: permanent and total disability claims may take months or years to confirm, adding uncertainty.

4.summary:
Chapter 4 Summary
Private medical insurance (PMI)
PMI, whether individual or group, is usually an indemnity‐based product that seeks to provide compensation for the cost of private medical treatment. It provides cover for care and treatment for acute rather than chronic or incurable conditions.

A policy may typically cover treatment for short‐term acute medical conditions, in‐patient tests, surgery, hospital accommodation and nursing. It may include out‐patient tests and consultations, overseas cover and cash payments while in a State‐funded hospital.

Policies do not usually include treatment for pre‐existing conditions, GP services, cover for long‐term incurable (ie chronic) conditions and accident and emergency treatment. There are also typically a number of exclusions from cover, eg self‐inflicted injuries, cosmetic surgery and experimental drugs.

Policyholders are usually required to pay an excess on claims. They may be able to choose the level of excess to suit their income level. Usually the insurer pays the balance of the policyholder’s treatment cost directly to the provider.

Premiums
PMI is short‐term and usually a one‐year renewable contract. Premiums usually increase at each renewal with increasing age and due to medical cost inflation, however, some policies have premiums that are based on age at entry. Some PMI insurers operate NCD systems to reflect claims experience in premiums.

Product design
Policies should be designed to complement any health services provided by the State. This could mean providing an alternative to State treatment (eg in a hospital chosen by the policyholder, at a time chosen by the policyholder), rather than providing treatments that are not offered by the State health services.

Cheaper options may be available to widen the potential target market, eg policies:
• that only provide fixed cash sums for specified surgical procedures (MME)
• that will only accept a claim if the required treatment is unavailable from the State health service within a certain specified period (waiting list plans)
• that restrict cover to choice of hospitals (eg to preferred provider arrangements).

Health cash plans
These are policies that provide cash benefits designed to meet the unexpected costs of healthcare‐related events, including dental, optical, physiotherapy, maternity, hospitalisation, recuperation, hearing aids and consultation. Benefits may be limited to a certain percentage of the medical bill.

The products are typically community‐rated and a waiting period often applies. Pre‐existing conditions may be excluded. These policies have relatively small benefits and premiums, and so their success depends on large volumes, high renewal rates and low cost administration.

Dental and optical plans
Dental and optical benefits can be part of PMI or cash plan policies or can be stand‐alone plans.

The two principal methods of providing dental cover are the capitation basis, where the dentist is paid an agreed fixed sum per insured mouth, and an indemnity basis, where the insurer covers the actual cost of treatment delivered.

Optical plans provide cover for spectacles, contact lenses, eye tests and optical treatments.
Waiting periods and pre‐existing condition exclusions may apply.

Personal accident
Personal accident policies are short‐term contracts that provide lump sum benefits to compensate for bodily injury suffered as the result of an accident. Benefit amounts can be large.

The measure of exposure is usually the person‐year (or member‐ / employee‐year). The rating factors used are usually very simple (eg occupation, but not age or gender).

Claims are usually reported and settled quickly because the incidence of an event is usually very clear and claim costs are known, although they are sometimes subject to dispute. Claim frequency tends to be reasonably stable.

5.title: Group Health and Care Insurance Products

subsections.title: Definition and Key Differences from Individual Business
bullets: Group business is any collection of individuals who combine to make a single proposal for uniform insurance cover. The employer is typically the policyholder; the employees are the insured.

Group business is written over a short period (typically one or two years), covering claims that occur during that period. This makes group policies short-term, regularly renewable products even when written by a long-term insurer.
Key difference from individual business: premiums for each period depend on the number and characteristics of covered individuals. LTCI group products are not common.

Flexible (cafeteria-style) benefit schemes allow employees to choose from a menu of benefits. These increase anti-selection risk (members choose benefits where they expect to claim) and administrative complexity for the insurer.
Group covers may have tax advantages over individual insurances, making the employer-provided benefit worth more to the employee than its cost to the employer.

key_terms.definition: Any collection of individuals who combine to make a single proposal for uniform insurance cover. Usually employer-sponsored, with the employer as policyholder and employees as the insured.
An employer scheme offering a menu of benefit options from which employees can choose. Increases anti-selection risk and administrative complexity.

subsections.title: Purposes of Group Insurance
bullets: Group arrangements may be: a legal requirement (eg IP in some territories); a positive recruitment/retention message to employees; a mechanism to smooth early retirement (group IP) or promote return to work after illness (group PMI); or an employee benefit with tax advantages.
Group IP can act as a mechanism for early retirement when an employee is in continuing poor health. Group PMI can promote health and ensure speedy return to work following operations.

Group CI is used as part of the overall employee reward package — particularly valuable when TPD cover is included — and can cover occupations (eg some blue-collar) for which individual IP is not available.

subsections.title: Underwriting and Acceptance
bullets: Group underwriting uses 'average' assumptions: the underwriter assesses whether the group's overall experience will approximate that in the premium basis. Some unhealthy lives can be accepted because they are balanced by healthy lives.
Anti-selection is generally lower for group business than individual, but remains a risk. Extent depends on eligibility rules and whether a flexible benefit scheme is used.

Measures to restrict anti-selection: applying exclusions; setting free cover limits; requiring members to be 'actively at work' when cover begins; setting minimum take-up rates on voluntary schemes; imposing take-over terms for schemes transferring from another insurer.

Schemes with compulsory membership, determined-by-formula benefits (linked to salary), and a large number of members present less anti-selection risk — these can have higher free cover limits and smaller premium loadings.

Free cover: a certain level of benefit is automatically available to all group members without individual underwriting. Cover above the free cover limit requires individual medical evidence. Free cover limits are lower for smaller schemes (to avoid one individual carrying a large proportion of total risk).

Free cover does not mean the cover is free of charge — it means standard premiums apply without individual underwriting. The aim is to reduce underwriting costs and avoid inconveniencing most members.
Free cover applies to group IP and CI (where benefit levels vary by salary), but not group PMI (where benefits are indemnity-based and the benefit amount is unknown in advance).

Actively at work criterion: members must have been continuously at work (or unrestricted in daily activities, for the non-employed) during the past 2-3 months to qualify for free cover. Cheap and verifiable from employer records.

key_terms.definition: The level of benefit automatically granted to group scheme members without individual underwriting. Standard premiums apply. Only members seeking benefit above this limit need medical evidence.

A requirement that group members must have been at work (or unrestricted in daily activities) for a minimum recent period (typically 2-3 months) to qualify for free cover. Reduces the risk of anti-selection at scheme inception.

exam_qa.q: Why does compulsory membership, a minimum number of members, an actively at work criterion, and a prescribed benefit formula each enable the insurer to offer a higher free cover limit?
exam_qa.a: Compulsory membership: ensures good and bad risks join, allowing cross-subsidy. Minimum number of members: larger groups have a better mix of risks and cross-subsidy is more reliable. Actively at work criterion: excludes those currently sick, cheaply verified from employer records. Prescribed benefit formula (salary-linked): prevents worse risks from over-insuring relative to better risks, reducing the adverse impact on claims experience.

subsections.title: Benefits and Key Features
bullets: Group IP is used by employers to provide sick pay to employees who are unable to work due to illness or injury. Benefits are often based on salary gross of tax, though net pay schemes exist.

Additional benefits may include: employer and employee State welfare contributions; pension contributions during incapacity; continuation options (allowing a leaving employee to continue cover individually without medical evidence).
Group IP is effectively a short-term, annually renewable product even though written by a long-term insurer — cover and premiums are renegotiated each year based on current membership.
Group IP can be used to smooth the process of early retirement when an employee is in continuing poor health.

key_terms.definition: A feature of group schemes allowing a departing employee to continue their cover individually, without individual underwriting. Valuable for lives who may be impaired at departure.

subsections.title: Benefits, Purposes and Underwriting
bullets: Group CI operates on the same basic principles as group life insurance. It is part of the overall employee reward package and can attract and retain staff.
Group CI is particularly valuable for employees in occupations where individual IP is not available (eg some blue-collar occupations), especially when TPD cover is included.
The free cover limit for group CI depends on: size of the scheme, whether membership is compulsory or voluntary, take-up proportion if voluntary, and the total and average sum insured.

subsections.title: Benefits and Key Features
bullets: Group PMI benefits and exclusions are broadly similar to individual PMI, but pre-existing conditions are more likely to be covered under group business due to the lower anti-selection risk.

There is greater scope for benefit customisation for larger groups: choosing specific excess or limit levels; adding dental or prescription cover not on standard products; excluding benefits the group does not require (eg psychiatric treatment, complementary medicine).

For experience-rated large groups, the insurer may offer bespoke benefit structures even where it has limited experience data for those specific benefits — the group bears much of the claims risk via the high credibility factor.

Free cover limits are not used for group PMI because benefits are indemnity-based: the eventual claim amount is unknown in advance, making it impossible to determine which members would exceed a free cover limit.

exam_qa.q: Describe the underwriting procedures appropriate for a group CI scheme offered to parents of school pupils, where membership is voluntary and premiums are collected by the school.
exam_qa.a: The intermediary (school) has limited contact with parents, so procedures must be simple. No financial underwriting is needed — the benefit is formula-based (school fees) and earmarked for a specific purpose. If voluntary, offer free cover to those joining at inception. Require subsequent joiners to complete simple individual underwriting (DOB, smoking status, declaration of medical treatment, actively at work/unrestricted activities declaration). Consider a moratorium excluding pre-existing conditions in year 1. A free cover limit based on the number of children at the school could be applied. If large take-up, reduced underwriting is appropriate.

5.summary:
Chapter 5 Summary
Group business
Group business is defined as any collection of individuals who combine to make a single proposal for uniform insurance cover.

Group schemes are most commonly set up by employers for their employees, in which case the employer is the policyholder even though those insured are the employees.

Group products:
• may be a legal requirement (eg IP in some territories)
• may be used as a positive message to employees at the time of recruitment / promotion (eg group CI or group PMI)
• can promote health and ensure a speedy return to work (group PMI)
• can smooth the process of early retirement (group IP)
• may have tax advantages over individual products.

Flexible benefit schemes
Flexible (or cafeteria‐style) benefit schemes are schemes offered by employers that give employees the choice of which benefits to select from a list of benefits offered by the employer. Flexible benefit schemes give employees greater flexibility to match benefits to their needs.

Anti‐selection
Anti‐selection is generally less of a risk for group schemes. As a result, underwriting and pricing can be more relaxed compared to individual products, especially for groups where the take‐up rate is close to 100%. For example, some lives in poor health may be accepted because they are balanced by lives in good health within the group.

However, the underwriting of group schemes must still be considered in order to prevent selection against the insurer. This is usually done by the use of eligibility rules. The insurer would usually ensure that members are ‘actively at work’ when cover begins. It may also require a minimum take‐up rate for members joining the scheme.

Flexible benefits can increase the potential for anti‐selection through increased choice.

Free cover
Free cover, whereby a certain level of benefits is available without individual underwriting, may be offered through group schemes because of reduced anti‐selection risk. This has the benefit of reduced underwriting costs and a faster application process for most members in the group. Individual underwriting may still be required for the largest cases.

Group IP insurance
Employers can use such schemes to provide sick pay to employees.

Benefits are often based on salary gross of tax, but net pay schemes are sometimes offered.
Additional benefits may include employee and employer State welfare and pension contributions. Continuation options may also be offered.

Group CI insurance
Employers can use group critical illness insurance policies as part of the reward package for staff.

The benefit can be provided to those employees and individuals for whom income protection insurance is not available, including some blue‐collar occupations, and is of particular value when TPD cover is included.

Group PMI
Benefits and exclusions are generally similar to those of individual products, although pre‐existing conditions are more likely to be covered.

There is often greater scope for customising benefits for larger groups.

6.title: Product Design and Stakeholder Interests (1) – Customer Perspective

subsections.title: General Needs and Microinsurance
bullets: From a customer viewpoint, a good product should: meet needs; be clear in purpose (risks covered and benefits payable); have an appropriate charging structure; and above all have an affordable premium that offers value for money.
Health and care insurance products meet three specific needs: providing a lump sum to repay loans; replacing income; and financing medical treatment bills.

IP insurance: meets the need for replacement income when policyholders are unable to work. Benefits are typically limited to 60–75% of gross salary to maintain an incentive to return to work. Main buyers: self-employed individuals, employers (group IP), and small professional practices (eg Locum Protection insurance).

CI insurance: meets a variety of needs — repaying a mortgage or loan; funding medical costs; providing income via annuity; funding business partner buyout (Keyperson cover); lifestyle change after illness. Tiered benefit CI is a closer fit to medical distress and financial need, and is deemed fairer, but is more complex.

LTCI: meets the need for financial protection against costs of care when unable to look after oneself. Care needs progress from domestic support → live-in care → residential care → medical care. Demand depends on adequacy of State and informal (family/friends) provision.

PMI: assists the policyholder in covering the cost of medical care — complementing or replacing State provision. In markets with State provision, PMI is bought for shorter waiting times, better accommodation, choice of doctor, and local hospital access.

Microinsurance: targeted at those with low incomes (especially in developing world). Characterised by limited benefits and very low premiums. Main products: versions of PMI (hospitalisation, primary care) and IP. Often sold alongside microfinance.

key_terms.definition: A CI or life insurance policy taken out by business partners on each other's lives, where the benefit funds the buyout of the sick partner's stake in the business.
Insurance targeted at those with low incomes, characterised by very low premiums and limited benefits. Common in developing markets such as India and parts of Africa.
A CI product design where the sum insured varies according to the severity of the diagnosed illness, providing a closer match to the financial need and allowing cover for less severe conditions.

exam_qa.q: In what sense could you argue that PMI is more focused, and IP more comprehensive, than CI?
exam_qa.a: PMI is more focused: it is an indemnity product that pays for actual medical treatment expenses incurred — the payment is directly related to the actual loss. CI is less focused as it pays a fixed lump sum regardless of the actual loss suffered. IP is more comprehensive: it pays whenever the policyholder cannot work in their own occupation due to any illness or injury, whereas CI only covers specific pre-defined critical illnesses. For example, a broken arm preventing a carpenter from working would trigger an IP benefit but not a CI or PMI payment.

subsections.title: Cash vs Indemnity by Product
bullets: Cash benefits give the policyholder choice — they can be applied to any personal need, converted to income or used for any purpose. However, the customer must understand the likely costs to set an appropriate benefit level.

Indemnity benefits are directly related to the actual loss. An advantage is that the insurer may negotiate cheaper prices from healthcare providers due to volume, making treatment cheaper than if funded from a CI cash benefit.

IP: cash benefit, paid as income stream. May include a rehabilitation or proportional benefit to encourage return to work — benefiting both insurer (lower claim costs) and policyholder (faster recovery).

CI: cash benefit, paid as lump sum. The lump sum cannot be subsequently withdrawn by the insurer once paid. Can be more than the immediate cost of care — surplus available for recuperation or lifestyle change. Risk of 'windfall gains' where benefit greatly exceeds loss (eg angioplasty now a minor procedure) — can cause anti-selection and moral hazard.

LTCI: typically cash benefits (income stream). Few insurers offer indemnity benefits due to the difficulty of forecasting future care costs, longevity risk, and benefit inflation uncertainty. Immediate needs plans convert a single premium into a lifetime income at point of need.

PMI: normally an indemnity product — pays actual medical treatment costs, often directly to the provider. Exceptions include: health cash plans (partial cash contribution); daily benefit for use of State hospital; major medical expense plans (lump sum for surgery); personal accident plans.

key_terms.definition: A benefit that reimburses the actual costs incurred (eg treatment expenses), returning the policyholder to the financial position they were in before the insured event. Contrasts with a cash benefit of fixed amount.
A type of PMI variant that provides a partial cash contribution when certain medical events occur (eg physiotherapy, new spectacles), rather than full indemnification. Reduces anti-selection risk.

subsections.title: Peace of Mind by Product
bullets: Customers are only concerned about risks they recognise — many are unaware of the true likelihood of long-term sickness vs critical illness, leading CI to outsell IP in some markets.
IP: concern about being unable to provide for dependants or repay loans. State sickness benefits are often inadequate and employer benefits are usually short-term.
CI: provides financial peace of mind, but is not comprehensive — it only covers specific headline illnesses. A combination of IP and PMI may be better targeted and more comprehensive.

LTCI: concerns about insufficient funds for care in old age and inadequacy of State/informal provision. Key issue — policyholders may believe care is being bought, when only cash is provided. Some insurers address this with a care management advisory service.
PMI: concern about unaffordable treatment costs, waiting times, or quality of State provision. Employer may also share the concern about employees being unable to work.

subsections.title: Simplicity and Clarity
bullets: IP: simple concept but complications arise from: benefit limits (replacement ratio cap); payouts not always linked to current salary; complex underwriting (occupation, medical history); exclusions (alcohol, drug abuse, self-inflicted injury, war); difficulty defining incapacity objectively. ADLs, ADWs, FATs and PCAs are objective tests used.

CI: theoretically simple (fixed lump sum on diagnosis). Attractions: lump sum cannot be withdrawn; claims trigger (diagnosis or procedure) is easily explained. But: exclusions and point-of-claim underwriting complicate matters; claim definitions may differ between insurers or require evidence of severity beyond the layperson's understanding. Tiered CI is more complex — greater potential for claims dispute.

LTCI: concept is straightforward but confusion arises over: whether cash benefits will meet actual care costs; benefit caps or maximum payment periods; trigger points (ADLs, cognitive impairment) need explanation.

PMI: indemnification is clear in concept but complications arise from: underwriting/acceptance process; exclusions (chronic and pre-existing conditions, cosmetic surgery); cost-sharing (coinsurance, excesses/deductibles); pre-authorisation requirements; restrictions to hospital bands or networks.

key_terms.definition: A set of defined personal care activities (eg bathing, dressing, eating, mobility) used as objective trigger criteria for LTCI and some IP claims. Inability to perform a specified number of ADLs without assistance triggers the claim.
A PMI requirement for the insurer to approve a planned procedure before treatment begins, ensuring it is covered by the policy and performed in an appropriate facility. Reduces disputed claims.
A cost-sharing arrangement under which the policyholder pays a fixed percentage of every claim cost, with the insurer covering the remainder.

sections.title: Guaranteed vs Reviewable Premiums and Benefits

subsections.title: Guarantees and Reviewability by Product
bullets: Policyholders prefer guaranteed premiums (certainty of future outgo) but guarantees are expensive to price and reserve for, especially in health and care insurance where there are many factors outside the insurer's control.

IP: premiums may be guaranteed or periodically reviewable. Claims experience is affected by economic and social influences (eg unemployment trends). Selective lapsing limits the size of any premium increase under reviewable policies — capping increases at new business rates helps retention.

CI: originally premiums were reviewable due to unknown future risks. Competition pushed some insurers to offer guaranteed premiums at little extra cost, but the true reserving cost is now better understood. With many illness trends moving in different directions, future premium increases under reviews are difficult to predict.

LTCI — immediate needs: no premium guarantee issues — single premium buys a lifetime income stream. LTCI — pre-funded: reviewable premiums predominate. Degree of variation depends on claims experience and trend in care cost index.

PMI: almost invariably a one-year product, annually renewable. Premiums (and sometimes policy terms) can change at each renewal. Guaranteed premium products for limited periods (3–5 years) are rare and only viable when medical inflation is stable.

key_terms.definition: Premiums that cannot be changed by the insurer throughout the policy term. Provide certainty to the policyholder but are expensive to price, as the insurer must hold sufficient reserves to cover all future adverse scenarios.
Premiums that the insurer may change at defined review dates, based on the emerging experience of the whole portfolio. The policyholder retains the option to accept or decline the new terms.

exam_qa.q: Outline the factors that influence the suitability of offering guaranteed vs reviewable premiums on long-term health and care insurance business.
exam_qa.a: Factors favouring guaranteed premiums: customer preference for certainty; competitive pressure (if competitors offer guarantees); simpler product (easier to sell); less risk of selective lapsing on review. Factors favouring reviewable premiums: less need for prudent reserves upfront (cheaper premium); greater ability to respond to adverse experience (eg new diseases, medical advances for CI); economic and social influences on IP claims are hard to predict long-term; for PMI, claims inflation is inherently unpredictable over multiple years; if the insurer has limited capital, smaller reserves required for reviewable products; where data are scarce, pricing is uncertain and review allows correction. In practice, the insurer must balance cost competitiveness, policyholder expectations, regulatory requirements, and its own risk appetite.

6.summary:
Chapter 6 Summary
Customers are among those who have a stake or interest in the design of health and care insurance products. Their primary interests are to have products that meet their needs, are clear in purpose, have an appropriate charging structure (eg guarantees and options), and are affordable.

Meeting specific needs
IP insurance may be used to cover day‐to‐day expenses, loan servicing costs and other insurance premiums. The need will be greatest where employer and State benefits are low.

The main customers for IP are individuals (in particular the selcf‐employed), employers and small professional practices.

CI insurance meets a variety of needs:
• the benefit can be used to repay a mortgage or other loan
• medical costs can be funded when the CI requires surgery or treatment
• income can be provided when the individuals cannot work as a result of their CIs
• business partners can purchase CI policies on the lives of each other such that the benefits will fund the buyout of the stake in the partnership
• it can fund a change of lifestyle in order to improve the claimant’s health
• other needs include recuperation or rehabilitation treatment after illness, taxation planning and medical aids.

The needs met by tiered CI benefits are that they:
• may be a closer fit to medical distress and financial needs
• may be deemed more comprehensive and more fair.

LTCI meets the need of financial protection against the costs of care and assistance when a person becomes unable to look after him/herself. The care needs in old age may include domestic support, live‐in care, residential care and medical care.

Demand for LTCI will depend on the level of State benefits and also the extent to which care may be provided informally (by family and friends).

PMI aims to assist the policyholder in covering the costs of medical care. Products should be designed to complement or augment the care offered by the State. This means that different designs will be needed in different markets, eg if no State‐funded healthcare is provided, PMI may also cover primary care.

PMI will be purchased by those who want better standards of treatment or care, more choice or shorter waiting times.

Microinsurance targeted towards those with low incomes (eg in the developing world), and has a greater emphasis on the more basic components of cover and on affordability.

Providing cash
Cash gives the policyholder choice, both in terms of:
• the level of benefit
• the use of the benefit.

However, this choice may not be positive if the policyholder makes a poor choice. An advantage of indemnity benefits is that the insurer may be able to obtain cheaper prices from healthcare providers due to the volume of business given to providers.

IP insurance provides a cash (income) benefit. The policyholder chooses the amount of benefit, subject to limits. A proportional or rehabilitation benefit may be provided so that the policyholder has an incentive to return to work.

CI insurance provides a lump sum benefit that is not related to the actual loss incurred by the policyholder and it can be used for any purpose. Benefits can be more than the cost of immediate care; the surplus may be used for recuperation / rehabilitation. Sometimes the benefit is a genuine windfall to the policyholder (which can cause anti‐selection).

LTCI is likely to provide cash benefits: while consumers may prefer indemnity benefits, insurers have found it increasingly difficult to assess the main risks (longevity (annuity) risk and the risk of benefit inflation being greater than expected).

PMI policies are normally written on an indemnity basis, although there may be a cap on the benefits paid for certain procedures. The insurer usually pays the provider of treatment directly. Exceptions to this include cash benefits that are paid when a State‐alternative is used and health cash plans, which provide cash benefits.

Providing peace of mind
For many individuals there is a positive psychological effect of knowing they are protected should they suffer disability / sickness.

IP insurance – The main concern is being unable to provide for self / dependants and/or to pay off loans if unable to work due to ill‐health. State disability allowances are often insufficient and employment benefits are often only short term.

CI insurance – The main concern is being diagnosed with certain headline diseases or the need to undergo serious surgical procedures.

LTCI – The main concerns are having insufficient funds to pay for care in old age and that care available from the State (or informally) is inadequate. The concern may also come from a family member or friend.

PMI – The main concerns are that the costs of treatment are unaffordable or that the quality of State provision is inadequate.

Simplicity and clarity
IP insurance – The concept of IP is simple and appealing, but, there are certain (potentially confusing) issues:
• it may be necessary to apply benefit limits
• payouts are not always linked to current salary
• underwriting can be complex (due to occupation or past medical history)
• there may be exclusions, eg claims resulting from alcohol and drug abuse
• the definition of incapacity and the measures of fitness to work are not always open to objective assessment.

CI insurance – These policies appeal to consumers as they are readily understood, provide a lump sum benefit that cannot subsequently be withdrawn and cover a risk that customers can recognise readily. However confusion can arise due to exclusions, point‐of‐claim underwriting and non‐standard claim definitions.

Tiered benefits CI:
• is more complex than a standard CI contract, making it hard to compare
• has potential for a higher degree of claims dispute.

LTCI – While the concept is straightforward, confusion may arise over the extent of the benefits. Cash benefits may not meet the costs of care, and may be subject to maximum amounts or payment period. Also, trigger events are usually defined in terms of ADLs, which need to be explained to an applicant.

PMI – Again, the concept is clear, but policy conditions can complicate the product and lead to misunderstandings by policyholders. In particular:
• the underwriting and acceptance process can be misunderstood
• there may be limits on the benefits (eg excesses and upper limits)
• there may be exclusions (eg for pre‐existing and non‐acute conditions)
• there may be complicated cost‐sharing arrangements for sharing the risk between the policyholder and the insurer
• claims may be required to be pre‐authorised
• the choice of hospitals or other establishments in which treatment can be provided may be restricted.

Guarantees and reviewability of premiums and benefits
Policyholders will prefer a guaranteed premium, however, high levels of uncertainty for health and care insurance means that guaranteed premium business can be expensive.

IP insurance – Premiums may be guaranteed or periodically reviewable, however, claims experience is uncertain and subject to many influences outside the insurer’s control (eg unemployment trends) so guaranteed premiums may be expensive. If premiums are reviewable then selective lapsing will act to limit the size of any premium increase.

CI insurance – What are serious illnesses, and how they are treated, changes rapidly with medical advance. This has made it difficult for insurers to assess the risks they face. This uncertainty makes reviewable premiums more suitable, however, competitive pressures have led to guaranteed premium business being sold.

LTCI – Immediate needs products present no problems for premium guarantees. For pre‐funded products, reviewable premiums predominate.

PMI – PMI is short term and usually annually renewable, and so premiums (and sometimes policy terms and conditions) can be changed each year.

7.title: Product Design and Stakeholder Interests (2) – Insurer, Regulator and Other Stakeholders

subsections.title: Insurer Product Design Considerations
bullets: Product design and pricing is an iterative procedure balancing: customer acceptability; regulator requirements; needs of distributors; price competitiveness; adequate profitability/return on capital; company culture in product style and price; systems and other internal constraints; and underwriting methodology.

Customer acceptability: the product must be clear about both the benefits provided (claims triggers, cash values) and the amounts and variability of premiums. Innovative features and options increase marketability but must be understood by the target customer base — simplicity matters for the chosen distribution channel.
Adequate profitability: premiums must cover benefits, expenses, and leave a surplus to reward shareholders. The balance between competitiveness and profitability is a fundamental management task.

Company culture: new product structures should be similar to existing products to avoid systems development costs, staff retraining, and the risk of dissatisfying existing policyholders who may feel unfairly treated.

Systems constraints: IT must record all processes, provide profitability information, and be capable of handling the product design. Systems limitations may restrict product features. Data capture is critical for pricing, monitoring, and repricing.

Underwriting methodology: medical underwriting is a fundamental element of product design. For long-term guaranteed premium policies, stringent underwriting is most important as there is no opportunity to revise terms. For one-year renewable policies (PMI), moratorium underwriting (collecting evidence only at claim) reduces expense but increases anti-selection risk.
Claims procedures must be consistent with underwriting criteria and pricing data. They should match policyholder expectations and broadly match competitor practices to avoid adverse anti-selection.

Risk pricing: a clear set of conditions and policy rules enables statistical costing and controls anti-selection and moral hazard. Reinsurer assistance is often vital for data, contract design, and pricing, especially for new products.

Financing requirement: the insurer will want to minimise its capital strain on new business. Guarantees have a big influence on up-front reserve requirements. This is particularly important for relatively new health and care insurers with limited free assets.

Cross-subsidies: where premium averaging is needed, the actuary must monitor the mix of business to ensure cross-subsidies do not create systematic losses. The rights of different policyholder classes must not be diminished.

key_terms.definition: The tendency for those with a higher-than-average risk to seek insurance cover, while lower-risk individuals decline. Arises when the insurer does not capture all relevant risk information. Also known as adverse selection.
The risk that a policyholder's behaviour changes adversely as a result of having insurance — eg taking greater risks or using more healthcare services than they otherwise would.

A PMI underwriting approach where medical evidence of existing conditions is only collected at the point of claim, rather than at inception. Reduces initial expense but increases the risk of accepting sub-standard risks.

The initial negative cashflow arising when a new policy is written, equal to the initial supervisory reserve plus solvency capital plus initial expenses minus the first premium. Must be financed from free assets.

exam_qa.q: Why is reinsurer expertise more important for health and care insurance products than for life insurance protection products?
exam_qa.a: Health and care insurance involves greater complexity — incidence rates are harder to predict, claim definitions are more subjective, and experience data are scarcer. When launching a new health and care product (eg CI in a new market), a reinsurer can provide: statistics from other markets; relevant population data; actuarial and pricing expertise; assistance with contract wording; regulatory knowledge; and training for internal staff. Reinsurers accumulate experience across many insurers and territories, making their data particularly valuable in markets with limited historical data. For standard life insurance protection (term assurance), mortality data are well established and standard tables are widely available, making external reinsurer expertise less critical.

sections.title: Product-Specific Control Procedures for the Insurer

subsections.title: Control by Product
bullets: IP: initial underwriting/acceptance with premiums reflecting risk factors (occupation, age, gender where permitted); claims underwriting (checking benefit against current salary); use of claims counsellors; monitoring by specialist nurses throughout claims.

CI: effective underwriting and claims control to guard against anti-selection and non-disclosure (especially important for tiered benefit CI); survival period on stand-alone policies (avoids 'death' claims); adequate IBNR reserves for late-notified claims; managing policyholder expectations as new diseases emerge; careful policy wording (especially for tiered benefits); large margins and/or reinsurer co-operation where data are scarce.
LTCI: policy designs must be robust to changes in State provision of care and regulatory interpretation of benefits promised.
PMI: control of claim costs by agreeing and monitoring provider prices; pre-authorisation of benefits; ensuring premium increases can be justified to policyholders at renewal.

key_terms.definition: Claims that have been incurred (the insured event has occurred) but have not yet been formally notified to the insurer. Reserves must be held to cover expected IBNR claims.

subsections.title: Regulatory and Other Stakeholder Interests
bullets: Regulators' priorities: (1) the insurer remains solvent; (2) consumers are considered in all decisions and disadvantages to consumers in sales, administration, and claims are minimised.

Regulatory requirements may include: adequate and accurate reporting to the regulator; approval of premium increases; clear and unambiguous policy wording; a range of suitable benefits representing reasonable value for money; monitoring of sales processes and target market appropriateness.

Distributors (intermediaries, tied agents, own salesforce): interested in having products that are easy to explain, competitively priced, and carry sufficient commission. The sales incentive must be sufficient to encourage distribution but appropriate from a regulatory perspective.

Actuarial profession: professional guidance gives actuaries a framework for maintaining professional standards and may provide protection against pressure from management. Guidance typically covers: policy conditions; adequacy of premium rates; value of the liabilities. Professional guidance must not unduly restrict actuaries' judgement.

Good product design rule of thumb: ensure there is a customer need; price accurately, projecting a range of outcomes; charge the cost of capital to the product; obtain sound reinsurance; ensure policy literature clearly describes guarantees; ensure adequate reserves are in place.

7.summary:
Chapter 7 Summary
Insurers
Product design and pricing will often be an iterative procedure as each is adjusted in turn so that all the various targets can be met:
• customer acceptability – the product must be clear about both the benefits provided and the amounts and variability of premiums; it must meet customer needs and/or provide some element of customer gain
• regulator requirements – product designs (and possibly also premiums) must comply with the relevant regulation
• needs of distributors – products need to be distributed through appropriate sales channels; the structure and level of premiums or charges should not depart too far from those of competitors; input of sales and marketing teams (and other distributors) is essential
• adequate profitability / return on capital – the premiums charged must be sufficient to cover benefits and expenses, and meet the required profit criteria
• company culture in product style and price – the charging and benefit structures should be similar to other products of the company, and the company’s culture
• systems and other internal constraints – computer / administration systems must be able to cope with the design of products; data must be captured adequately; reinsurers may be able to assist in the provision of statistics
• underwriting methodology – medical underwriting and policy acceptance will affect product design and pricing; claims procedures should be consistent with the underwriting criteria, the data underpinning the pricing calculation, policyholder expectations and competitors’ practices.

The insurer will wish to ensure it has efficient control of the risk management process. This includes:
• ensuring there is a clear set of conditions and policy rules
• having sufficient and relevant data for pricing and subsequent monitoring
• appropriate reinsurance
• minimising financing requirements
• consideration of equity between policyholders
• consideration of cross‐subsidies between contracts
• being aware of the guarantees offered
• premium reviews for reviewable or renewable policies.

Consideration will need to be given to the acceptability of the level of risk associated with a proposed contract design. The level of risk acceptable will depend on the company’s ability or willingness to either absorb risk internally or to reinsure it.

There may be conflicts between the above product design factors.

In summary the rules are broadly:
• ensure there is a customer need
• price as accurately as possible, projecting a range of potential outcomes
• charge the cost of capital to the product, if possible
• obtain sound reinsurance, building this cost into the product if necessary
• ensure that marketing and other policy literature is clear in its description of the guarantees
• ensure that the sales process explains clearly any guarantees and their implications for premiums and benefits
• ensure that adequate reserves are in place when the business is written.

Product‐specific control procedures
IP insurance:
• initial underwriting / acceptance and charging appropriate premiums based on risk factors (such as occupation, location, age, gender – if permitted)
• claims underwriting, including checking the level of benefit against current salary
• use of a claims counsellor to provide advice to claimants and advice to the insurer
• monitoring of claimants by specialist nurses throughout claims.

CI insurance:
• effective underwriting and claims control to protect against anti‐selection and nondisclosure – this is even more important for tiered benefit contracts
• imposing a survival period on stand‐alone policies (to avoid ‘death’ claims)
• adequate reserves to cover late notified claims
• managing policyholder expectations in the light of new diseases and market changes, eg to extend / restrict cover
• careful policy wording, especially for tiered benefit contracts
• data may be scarce when offering tiered benefits, so large margins and/or co‐operation of a reinsurer might be needed.

LTCI:
• policy designs that are robust to changes in the provision of care by the State and the regulator’s interpretation of the benefits promised.

PMI:
• control of claim costs by agreeing and monitoring provider prices
• control of claim costs through pre‐authorisation of benefits
• ensuring that price increases can be justified to the policyholder on renewal.

Regulators
The regulators’ priorities are:
• the insurer remains solvent
• that consumers are considered in all decisions and that any disadvantages to consumers in terms of sales, administration and claim payment are minimised if not totally eradicated.

Insurers will be required to submit adequate and accurate reports to the regulator. There may also be a requirement that premium increases are approved by the regulator.

The policy will be required to have clear and unambiguous wording, offer a range of benefits and represent reasonable value for money.

Sales processes and the appropriateness of the target market will also be monitored.

Other considerations
A good product design will be compatible with the interests of all stakeholders involved.

Product design and pricing should meet any relevant professional guidance.

8.title: Distribution Channels

subsections.title: Four Channels and Worksite Marketing
bullets: Insurance intermediaries (brokers): act independently of any particular insurer; find the contract that best meets the client's needs in terms of benefits and premiums; remunerated by commission from insurers or fees from clients. Clients usually initiate the sale. Risk of mis-selling if intermediaries are motivated by commission levels rather than client suitability.

Tied agents: tied to one (or sometimes a few) insurer(s); offer only those insurers' products (ranges are usually mutually exclusive). Typically employees of a bank or other financial institution — bancassurance. Remunerated by commission from the insurer. Active selling is common. Product ranges of multiple companies are usually mutually exclusive to avoid conflicts.

Own salesforce: employees of the insurer, selling only that insurer's products. Remunerated by salary, commission, or a mixture. Sales are usually initiated by the salesperson. Benefits from having a 'warm' client base once established.

Direct marketing: four forms — mailshots (insurer initiates); telephone selling (either party initiates); press advertising (debatable); internet/comparison websites (usually client initiates). Internet enables basic underwriting at quotation stage. Simple products (cash plans, basic PMI) are most suitable for internet distribution.

Worksite marketing: a broker or insurer representative obtains permission from an employer to address the entire workforce. Intended to sell simple products to individuals who lack cover; these are individual (not group) policies and continue independently of employment. LTCI is unsuitable for worksite marketing (too complex, post-employment need, expensive). CI, IP, and health cash plans are suitable.

key_terms.definition: The distribution of insurance products through a bank's distribution network. The bank acts as a tied agent for its associated insurance company, marketing products to its existing customer base.

A distribution approach where an insurer or broker obtains permission from an employer to address the workforce and sell individual health and care insurance products, using the workplace as a convenient point of access.

subsections.title: Types of Commission
bullets: Initial and renewal commission: high initial commission for a defined initial period (eg 24 months), then a lower renewal commission thereafter — both expressed as percentages of premiums in the same period. PMI equivalents: higher commission for new policies vs renewals.

Indemnity commission: a form of initial commission — a lump sum paid immediately when the policy is sold (typically as a percentage of the first premium). Involves new business strain as commission is paid before premiums are fully received. A credit check on the distributor is required.

Clawback: the repayment of commission by the intermediary if a policy lapses before the commission is fully earned. The insurer bears a credit risk that the intermediary may be unable to repay (eg through bankruptcy). The proportion clawed back typically varies with the premiums actually paid relative to those expected.

Renewal commission: a lesser ongoing amount paid after the initial period to encourage persistency; paid annually on long-term policies after the earnings period, or annually on renewal for short-term policies.

Level commission: a fixed proportion of every premium paid, throughout the policy term. Does not involve new business strain; matches commission outgo to premium contributions and profitability. Less popular with intermediaries (lower initial payment) but more suitable for the insurer's cashflow.
Commission may also be expressed as a proportion of the sum insured (rather than the premium), which better reflects the insurer's preference for higher sums at risk.

key_terms.definition: A lump sum commission paid to the distributor immediately on new business being written, representing an advance against future premiums. Involves new business strain and a credit risk on the distributor.
The recovery by the insurer of part of the initial commission paid to a salesperson when a policy lapses before the commission is fully earned. Subject to credit risk if the intermediary defaults.
A commission structure where a fixed percentage of every premium is paid to the distributor throughout the policy term. No new business strain; better aligns commission outgo with profitability.

subsections.title: Impact on Experience and Risk
bullets: Different channels appeal to different customers based on financial sophistication and income. The sophistication of the client base influences the complexity of products that can be sold.

Claims and persistency experience will vary by channel, depending on: financial sophistication; income levels; level of underwriting applied; and who initiated the sale (customer-initiated sales tend to have better experience as the policyholder has a genuine perceived need).
Greatest competition — and hence pressure for low premiums — exists in business sold through insurance brokers and internet comparison websites.
Worksite marketing: benefits from the prospect of engaging a large number of similar risks (keeping risk premiums low) and possible expense savings; limited by small average policy sizes.

Group risks via brokers: the broker handles communication, data gathering, and transfers. The insurer benefits from assured quality of selection and minimal additional administrative expense, but has limited ability to: build a relationship with the employer; influence retention of the business; or influence the employer's risk management.

8.summary:
Chapter 8 Summary
Distribution channels
The main distribution channels are:
• insurance intermediaries (brokers), who act independently of any particular insurer and who select products for their clients from all those available on the market
• tied agents, who are ‘tied’ to one or more insurer and who offer the products of these insurers (usually the product ranges of the companies are mutually exclusive)
• own salesforce, usually employed by a particular insurer to sell its products direct to the public
• direct marketing via mailshots, telephone selling, press advertising or internet advertising / comparison websites.

For insurance intermediaries and tied agents, it will usually be the client who initiates the sale, however:
• insurance intermediaries might also promote themselves actively to existing clients (eg instigating a periodic review of finances)
• tied agents might actively engage in selling (eg to existing customers).

Sales made by an own salesforce will usually be initiated by the salesperson, however once a rapport has been built up with a particular client, the client often initiates further sales.

For direct marketing, either the client or the seller might initiate the sale (and sometimes it is debatable who has done so).

Worksite marketing is where a broker or member of the own salesforce addresses the entire workforce of an employer.

Remuneration
Insurance intermediaries may be rewarded by commission from the insurers or by fees from their clients. Tied agents are usually rewarded through commission. The company’s own salesforce may be rewarded by salary or commission or a mixture of both.

Commission
Indemnity commission is a form of initial commission under which a lump sum is paid in respect of new business written. Therefore the majority of the commission is paid in advance of the premiums that earn it and so it will generally involve the insurer in some form of new business strain.

Clawback is the repayment of commission on policy lapse if it has not been fully earned (eg during the earnings period). Clawback may be calculated as a proportion of the initial commission that the number of premiums actually paid bears to the number expected during the earnings period.

Alternatively the initial commission paid may be spread over a number of years.

Renewal commission is a lesser amount of commission paid for the balance of the policy term to encourage persistency. It may be paid:
• annually after the earnings period for contracts paying indemnity commission
• annually (on renewal) for renewable contracts.

Level commission is where a set proportion of every premium paid by the policyholder is paid to the distributor. It does not involve the insurer in new business strain, and matches commission outgo to premium contributions and profitability more appropriately, but it is less popular with intermediaries.

Commission may be proportionate to the sum insured (rather than the premium), which better reflects an insurer’s preference for higher sums at risk.

The commissions paid may be determined by market norms or accepted codes of practice.

Effects of distribution channels
Different distribution channels are likely to appeal to different people according to their level of financial sophistication and level of income. The level of financial sophistication of the client base will also affect the complexity of the products being sold.

In particular, claims and persistency experience will vary depending on the level of financial sophistication, levels of income, the level of underwriting and who initiated the sale.

The need for competitive terms varies by sales channel, with the greatest competition being in business sold through insurance brokers and the internet via price comparison websites.

For worksite marketing, the insurer benefits from the prospect of engaging with a large number of similar risks, which may keep risk premiums low. There may also be expense savings, however, this may be limited by small average sizes.

Group risks
Employers (particularly large ones) usually buy their group insurance policies from brokers.
The broker will be responsible for the communication and data gathering and also transfer of money receipts / payments. The employer should benefit from a good level of service.

The broker should give assurance to the insurer over the quality of the selection process, and it normally means achievement of a legal contract with minimum additional administrative expenses. However, on the downside, the insurer will have limited opportunities to:
• build a relationship with the employer
• influence the retention of the business
• influence the risk attitude and risk management of the employer.

9.title: The General Business Environment

sections.title: Propensity to Buy vs Need to Sell

subsections.title: Bought vs Sold Products
bullets: Insurance that is legally compulsory is 'bought'. Insurance that is neither legally required nor obviously needed must be 'sold' — the intermediary must first make the customer aware of the need.

IP: should be a need for most, but is often not perceived as such. Many people are unaware of the inadequacy of State sickness benefits. Primarily a 'sold' product. Short-term IP may be legally binding on employers.

CI: not directly matched to a specific financial need; provides a lump sum at time of crisis. The 'lottery syndrome' (attraction of a large payout) means it is sometimes 'bought'. Provides peace of mind even if the policyholder can't specify what the money would be used for.

LTCI: need depends on perceived government attitude to care provision and individual priorities. Elements of legal compulsion exist in some countries. The State may incentivise purchase by providing low State provision or means-testing assets before providing care.

PMI: if all medical costs are individual responsibility, PMI is a need. If the State provides free healthcare, PMI must be sold — purchased for shorter waiting times, choice, and better accommodation.

Growing awareness that the State cannot maintain current welfare benefit levels (due to ageing populations, rising medical costs, and fiscal pressures) is increasing the underlying demand for health and care insurance.
Increasing sales regulation and disclosure requirements are reducing insurers' ability to 'create needs' through the sales process — products must genuinely meet customer needs to succeed.

subsections.title: Types of Regulatory Restriction
bullets: The primary aim of regulation is policyholder protection, ensuring public confidence in the insurance industry so that it can continue to attract business.

Regulatory restrictions may include: (1) restrictions on the types of contract an insurer can offer; (2) restrictions on premium rates or charges (eg maximum premium rates); (3) restrictions on rating factors (eg gender, genetic test results); (4) requirements on terms and conditions of contracts (eg claim definitions, maximum frequency and size of premium increases for reviewable products); (5) restrictions on sales channels and procedures (eg cooling-off periods, benefit illustrations, minimum salesperson training requirements); (6) restrictions on underwriting (eg prohibition on use of genetic test results or past claims history); (7) indirect capital constraint via minimum reserving and solvency capital requirements; (8) restrictions on types or amounts of assets used for solvency demonstration.
Regulation may discourage innovation or restrict benefits that could otherwise be provided to policyholders — a trade-off against policyholder protection.

The wider regulatory environment also matters — other institutions (eg hospital networks, care homes) may offer competing products under different regulatory constraints, creating an uneven playing field.

Genetic test results: insurers are generally prohibited from requiring genetic tests (an ethical infringement of personal liberty) or using results in underwriting, though they may continue to collect gender data.

key_terms.definition: A regulatory requirement giving policyholders the right to cancel a new insurance policy within an initial period (eg 30 days) without any financial loss.
The supervisory value of assets minus the supervisory value of liabilities (including SCR). Represents the capital available to write new business. Also called free capital or free reserves.

subsections.title: Taxation Approaches and Contract Design
bullets: Two main approaches to taxing insurance business: (1) tax on annual profits (increase in free assets over the year); (2) tax on investment income/gains less operating expenses (I–E basis).

The I–E basis is less suitable for health and care products than for life insurance, as most health and care products are primarily protection-based with small reserves and little investment income — little or no tax would be payable, which may not be acceptable for proprietary companies.
LTCI is the exception — it has a significant investment component and would generate more tax under an I–E basis.
Premium taxes exist in some territories — the insurer collects these on behalf of the State. Higher premium taxes reduce the attractiveness of insurance, increasing lapse rates.

Different types of business (short-term vs long-term, protection vs savings, conventional vs unit-linked) may be taxed differently within a country. Savings business may be exempt if policyholders receive gross investment returns, with benefits taxed instead.

Tax treatment of premiums (deductibility), investment return, and benefits all affect the overall attractiveness of a product. The State may use tax concessions to incentivise purchase of certain health products (eg tax relief on PMI or LTCI premiums).
Product design aims to make the best use of tax opportunities and must work within any tax-driven constraints (eg minimum insurance cover required to qualify for concessions).

key_terms.definition: A method of taxing insurance companies on investment income and capital gains less operating expenses, rather than on total profits. Common in some long-term insurance markets.

subsections.title: Professional Guidance
bullets: Actuarial associations issue professional guidance to give actuaries a framework for maintaining professional standards in advising health and care insurers.
Guidance typically covers: policy conditions; the adequacy of premium rates for new business; the value of the liabilities.
Guidance may also interpret government regulations where the government does not wish to be overly prescriptive — the profession effectively sets acceptable limits on behaviour.
Professional guidance provides protection to actuaries against pressure from management to agree to courses of action not in the best interests of policyholders.

subsections.title: Economic and Other Influences
bullets: Inflation: benefits must meet both current and future policyholder needs. Fixed level benefits risk becoming inadequate over time. Fixed escalation rates may be insufficient or excessive relative to actual inflation. Index-linked benefits may not match the specific inflation of health/care costs. Indemnity contracts transfer inflation risk to the insurer — especially risky for long-term guaranteed premium indemnity products.
Business cycle: demand for employer-sponsored insurances is higher in business upturns (used as a staff retention tool). In downturns, employers cut benefits or reduce coverage to save costs.

Employment security: high job security increases demand. Low job security increases some claim inceptions — eg employees claim under PMI before cover is lost; employers may encourage IP claims as a way to reduce the salary bill in downturns; work-related stress and depression increase IP claims.

Political stability: some political regimes are more favourable to health and care insurance than others (eg willingness to reduce State provision). Political stability generally indicates: economic wellbeing; stability of regulatory and tax rules; stability of the future economic value of insurance policies.

exam_qa.q: If premium regulation specifies minimum premium rates for CI insurance, what is the regulator's concern, and is this appropriate?
exam_qa.a: The regulator's concern is that excessively low premium rates may threaten the financial security of the insurer and hence the security of its policyholders. Minimum premium rates could prevent insurers from under-pricing and becoming insolvent. However, the regulator might also consider maximum premium rates to prevent overcharging, particularly in markets where competition is limited and consumers may not have access to easily comparable pricing information. Both upper and lower limits are theoretically possible — the appropriate choice depends on market structure and whether effective competition can be relied upon to control excessive pricing.

9.summary:
Chapter 9 Summary
Propensity to buy versus need to sell
Insurance can be considered as bought or sold:
• it will be bought if it is a legal requirement
• it will be sold where it is not legally necessary and where consumers are unaware that they have a need for the product.

Healthcare insurance products may be bought or sold. Protection products are generally designed to meet needs but may be somewhere between a need and a luxury. In particular:
• IP insurance should be a need but may not be perceived as such; limited term IP insurance may be legally binging on employers and is a need for the self‐employed
• CI insurance provides a lump sum that can be used to meet financial needs / compensate the policyholder, and the attraction of a big payout sometimes results in it being bought rather than sold
• the need for LTCI depends directly on the perceived attitude of governments to the provision of care and individuals’ priorities on long‐term funding for this purpose; there may be a legal compulsion for individuals to make some level of provision
• the need for PMI depends on level of State provision.

The perceived need for health and care insurance should increase with the awareness that the State cannot continue to afford to provide comprehensive welfare benefits. However, insurance companies may now be finding it more difficult to ‘create needs’ due to:
• increasing sales regulation and disclosure requirements
• mis‐selling scandals.

The regulatory regime
Governments may impose restrictions on insurance companies, including:
• a restriction on the types of contract that an insurance company can offer
• restrictions on the premium rates or charges used for some types of contract
• restrictions on rating factors that could be used to calculate premiums
• requirements relating to the terms and conditions of the contracts offered
• restrictions on the channels through which insurance can be sold, on sales procedures or on information given at the point of sale
• restrictions on the ability to underwrite
• an indirect constraint on the amount of business that may be written, via minimum reserving or solvency capital requirements
• restrictions on the types of asset or the amount of any particular asset in which the insurance company may invest for the purpose of demonstrating solvency.

Regulatory restrictions should help protect the policyholder, however, they may also discourage innovation and/or restrict the benefits offered.

The wider regulation of different financial institutions (eg insurers vs hospital networks and care homes) also has an impact on insurance companies, the products that they sell and the terms and conditions offered, by creating a non‐level playing field.

The taxation regime
The most common approaches to health and care insurance company taxation are:
• a tax on the annual profits of the business
• tax payable on investment income / gains less some or all of the operating expenses of the company.

Different tax treatment of different types of insurance contract can make it cheaper to provide some benefits as one form of business rather than another. The fiscal environment can also influence product design.

In comparing the tax advantages of different products one must consider:
