subsections.title: Consistency Between Assumptions and Across Products
bullets: Consistency is a guiding principle: assumptions must be considered in totality, not in isolation. Where correlations exist between variables, realistic allowance must be made for how they behave together — the correlation can be more important than the individual absolute values.

Cross-product consistency: the basis for a closely related product should form the starting point for a new product's basis. For example, the basis for a new stand-alone CI product should take as its starting point the insurer's existing accelerated CI product basis — using different assumptions for two similar products without justification is inconsistent.

Inconsistency between related products creates commercial risk: if two similar products are priced on different bases, one will be cheaper than the other. Policyholders holding the expensive product may lapse and take out the cheaper one, causing additional commission costs, new underwriting costs, and potentially generating overall losses. The insurer's profits become more sensitive to the mix of new business sold.

exam_qa.q: What might happen if an insurer uses an inconsistent basis between two closely related products?
exam_qa.a: The insurer ends up selling two similar products at the same time, one of which is cheaper relative to the other due to the inconsistency. Consequences: (1) Holders of the more expensive product may lapse and take out the cheaper one — the insurer incurs fresh underwriting and commission costs and makes a loss on the lapse. (2) A greater proportion of the cheaper product is sold, distorting the business mix. (3) Policyholders who discover the pricing inequity generate adverse publicity. (4) Profits become more sensitive to the mix of new business sold, increasing business risk.

subsections.title: Basis for Pricing — Best Estimate Plus Margins
bullets: Pricing focuses on book-rated business (premiums set from standard rate tables, not dependent on individual past experience) for initial derivation of rates. Experience-rated approaches — used mainly for group business and no-claims discounts on PMI — come later as own experience emerges and market information reveals relevant trends.

The starting point is a best estimate of future experience, but margins are necessary to guard against adverse outcomes. The pricing basis inclusive of margins fixes the level of risk the insurer accepts once the product is issued at that price — once policies are sold at a guaranteed premium, the risk cannot be undone.

Time horizon determines required margin size: a fully guaranteed IP policy ceasing at retirement requires long-term judgements about all parameters with corresponding large margins; a reviewable CI or annual PMI product only needs assessment over 1–5 years, after which premiums can be revised — shorter horizons where adverse experience can be corrected via renewal or review permit smaller margins.

key_terms.definition: A standard premium rate applied uniformly to all lives in a risk group, set from the insurer's standard rate tables without reference to the individual policyholder's past claims experience — the default for new individual business.

An approach where premiums are based wholly or partially on the past claims experience of the specific policyholder or group — used mainly for group insurance where the scheme's own past record is available and credible.

exam_qa.q: For what sort of health and care business is experience rating most likely to be used?
exam_qa.a: Experience rating is most used for group insurance — the past experience of the specific scheme plays a key part in determining the following year's premium. For individual business, no-claims discounts (a specific form of experience rating) may be used for PMI and possibly IP. Long-term individual products are almost always book-rated since there is insufficient individual history to rely on at the point of pricing.

subsections.title: Margins — Why They Are Needed and How Large
bullets: Health and care insurance has a wider 'funnel of doubt' than pure life insurance: fewer credible statistics; claims outcomes are more subjective (medical claim definitions involve interpretation); outcomes are more influenced by factors outside the insurer's control (economic conditions, State provision changes, social attitudes to sickness). All sources of risk must be considered when sizing margins.

Two determinants of margin size: (a) the degree of risk/uncertainty associated with each parameter — driven by data credibility, subjectivity of claim definitions, and the predictability of the variable; (b) the financial significance of adverse experience from each parameter — high uncertainty on a financially immaterial assumption (eg PMI investment return) requires less margin than low uncertainty on a key driver (eg CI incidence rates on a 20-year guaranteed product).

Three mechanisms for allowing for risk in a cashflow pricing model: (i) Apply margins directly to individual parameter assumptions — increase incidence rates above best estimate, reduce interest rate below best estimate. (ii) Use a stochastic approach — model parameter probability distributions and simulate the range of outcomes; margin = premium loading needed to achieve the target profit level across the simulated distribution. (iii) Include the risk premium in the risk discount rate — use best estimate parameters but increase the RDR to compensate shareholders for risk. All three can produce the same net premium but express the risk differently.

At one extreme, all prudence can be built into individual assumptions with a risk-free discount rate; at the other, best estimates are used everywhere with an elevated RDR. For pure formula models (no cashflow projection), only parameter margins are available — stochastic modelling and elevated RDR are not directly applicable.

It is worse to under-estimate morbidity than to over-estimate it: selling high volumes of under-priced business creates large losses before the error is identified (the insurer sells a lot before realising); over-priced business will quickly be revealed by low sales volumes and competitor rate comparisons, allowing fast corrective action.

key_terms.definition: The range of uncertainty around a best estimate assumption — wider for health and care than for life insurance because of greater subjectivity in claim definitions and stronger sensitivity to social, economic, and political factors outside the insurer's control.

The rate used to discount projected future profits in a profit test, incorporating a risk premium above the risk-free rate to compensate shareholders for the variability and uncertainty of those profits.

exam_qa.q: Discuss the extent to which margins are required when pricing a 20-year CI product, and where in the basis those margins should be applied.
exam_qa.a: Substantial margins are needed because: (1) 20-year term creates high parameter uncertainty — CI incidence rates are sensitive to medical advances (new diagnostic techniques accelerating claim rates, or new treatments reducing them), disease prevalence trends, and loose policy wording interpretation — all highly uncertain over 20 years. (2) If premiums are guaranteed and non-reviewable, the insurer cannot adjust for adverse experience, making the financial significance of any error very large. (3) CI data is less voluminous and credible than life mortality data, increasing estimation risk. Where to apply margins: primary margin is an upward loading to CI incidence rates. Expense assumptions should also be loaded upward for inflation uncertainty. An interest rate margin matters less (reserves for regular premium CI are relatively small so investment return has limited impact). If premiums are reviewable, smaller margins suffice because adverse experience can trigger a review, limiting the financial exposure.

subsections.title: Competitive Constraints on Margins — Five Factors
bullets: Five factors determine the final pricing margins: (1) Competitiveness of the market — more competitive markets compress margins to retain volume; (2) Company's USP — a distinctive product with no close substitute can sustain higher margins without losing sales; (3) Company's risk appetite — more risk-averse shareholders require larger margins to compensate for the uncertainty; (4) Credibility, accuracy, and relevance of data — better data reduces uncertainty and thus the required margin; (5) Size of free assets or parental guarantee — more capital buffer means the company can absorb adverse experience without immediate solvency risk, allowing lower margins.

Direction of effects: increasing competitiveness → reduce margins; increasing USP → can increase margins; increasing risk aversion → increase margins; improving data quality → reduce margins; increasing free assets or capital backing → reduce margins.

Trade-off between under- and over-pricing: too high a margin prices the product out of the market; too low a margin exposes the insurer to losses. The price charged and the margins incorporated are a satisfactory compromise between the need to reduce risk on one hand and the need to maintain sufficient business volume on the other.

key_terms.definition: The distinctive feature(s) of a product that differentiate it from competitors — a company with a high USP can charge a higher premium without losing sales because policyholders cannot easily find an equivalent alternative.

exam_qa.q: Discuss the extent to which margins against adverse contingencies are required when pricing an immediate needs long-term care annuity.
exam_qa.a: Significant margins are required because: (1) Mortality uncertainty — the policyholder is already claiming; how long they will live is highly uncertain and mortality rates for sick/elderly lives are less predictable than for healthy lives. Assumed mortality rates must be reduced (ie assume longer survival). (2) Transition to higher care categories — if higher benefit levels are triggered by deterioration in care needs, the transition rates and their timing are highly uncertain; transition rates should be increased. (3) Benefit inflation — if benefits are linked to long-term care cost indices (nursing/residential home fees), and care cost inflation may significantly exceed general price inflation due to staffing costs and demand for care. Assume a higher benefit inflation rate. (4) Investment return — cashflows are long-term and uncertain in duration, creating asset-liability mismatch risk; a reduced investment return should be assumed. (5) Expenses — administering claims for variable care levels and monitoring ongoing eligibility is expensive; assume higher expenses and expense inflation.

subsections.title: General Principles — Types of Reserve Basis
bullets: The starting point for reserving assumptions is always expected future experience (best estimate). Margins are then applied so that the probability of reserves being inadequate falls below an acceptably low level — what is 'acceptably low' depends on the purpose of the valuation.

Published accounts: assumptions must be consistent with accounting legislation and principles; key questions are whether accounts are on a going concern or break-up basis, whether a true and fair view is required, and whether reserves must be best estimate or prudent.

Solvency accounts: may have specific constraints (eg the valuation interest rate must equal the pricing basis rate) or general constraints (reserves must not be lower than a prescribed regulatory floor). Two broad approaches: (a) assumptions incorporate margins for prudence directly; (b) base reserves are calculated on a best estimate basis with a separate risk margin and/or minimum solvency capital requirement added.

Going concern vs break-up vs run-off: going concern assumes continued normal operation and new business writing; break-up assumes the insurer is wound up and unexpired cover cancelled; run-off assumes no new business but full servicing of in-force policies to expiry. The appropriate liability framework and expense loadings differ materially across these three bases.

Decreasing the discount rate increases prudence for a positive reserve (higher present value of future outgo); increasing the discount rate would paradoxically increase prudence for a negative reserve, where these are permitted.

key_terms.definition: A valuation basis that assumes the insurer continues to operate normally, writing new business and servicing existing policies — the standard basis for most published accounts.
A valuation basis that assumes the insurer is wound up — unexpired cover is cancelled, typically with a return of the unexpired pro-rata premium to the policyholder.
A valuation basis that assumes the insurer closes to new business but continues to service all existing in-force policies to expiry — an intermediate position between going concern and break-up.

subsections.title: Best Estimate Reserving and When It Is Used
bullets: Best estimate reserves use a cashflow method: project expected future claims, expenses, and tax outgo against expected future premium and investment income; the present value of any shortfall = reserve required. All experience items (morbidity, mortality, expenses, persistency, investment return) are allowed for explicitly on a realistic basis.

Under best estimate reserving there is no elimination of negative reserves — eliminating negative reserves is a prudent adjustment, but the purpose here is a realistic picture of financial performance. Negative reserves arise when future expected premium income exceeds future expected claims and expenses on a specific policy.

Uses for best estimate reserves: internal management accounts (monitoring profitability, investment strategy, reinsurance assessment, capital management); mergers and acquisitions (realistic sale/purchase price); incentive compensation (management bonuses linked to realistic company value); regulatory regimes requiring best estimate foundations (eg Solvency II gross best estimate liabilities).

key_terms.definition: A reserve that is negative because future expected premium income on a policy exceeds future expected claims and expenses — common in early policy years for long-term products. Elimination of negatives (setting them to zero) is prudent but not appropriate for best estimate calculations.

exam_qa.q: Why would there be no elimination of negative reserve values when setting best estimate reserves?
exam_qa.a: Eliminating negative reserves increases the total reserve held above the true best estimate — it is a prudent adjustment that distorts the picture. When the purpose is to obtain a realistic view of financial performance (management accounts, M&A pricing, bonus schemes, or Solvency II gross best estimate liabilities), management needs unbiased reserves. Setting negative reserves to zero would overstate the true liability and give a misleadingly conservative view of company value and profitability.

subsections.title: Pricing vs Reserving Assumptions — When They Diverge
bullets: In some countries, premiums are priced using prudent assumptions and the same basis is used for supervisory reserves — these are consistent because the pricing margins are already adequate for reserving. In other countries, pricing uses realistic best estimates (risk loaded into the RDR); here the supervisory reserving basis must add explicit prudential margins beyond the pricing assumptions since the pricing basis alone is not sufficiently conservative.

Under Solvency II-style regimes, base reserves are required to be on a best estimate basis; the total supervisory balance sheet = best estimate liabilities + risk margin + solvency capital requirement (SCR). Here pricing and reserving can share the same underlying best estimate, but the supervisory reserve is augmented by the separately calculated risk margin.

exam_qa.q: A proprietary company issues conventional long-term health insurance products. Describe the factors the actuary would consider when determining the extent of margins to incorporate when establishing the level of supervisory reserve.
exam_qa.a: The primary factors are: (1) Meeting supervisory regulatory requirements — the assumptions must achieve the necessary degree of prudence mandated by the relevant regime; this overrides commercial considerations that apply to pricing. (2) The necessary level of prudence may be achieved via margins in individual assumptions or via a separate risk margin/solvency capital requirement — regulations specify which approach is required. (3) Commercial considerations (competition, USP, free assets, risk appetite) which influence pricing margins are NOT relevant for reserving — the reserve must be adequate regardless of competitive pressure. (4) Modelling investigations for reserves focus on ensuring that reserves plus solvency capital are sufficient to cover future net outgo with a very high probability (eg 97.5%), using cashflow modelling of in-force business, stochastic analysis, and sensitivity testing.

subsections.title: Embedded Value — Definition, Components, and Purposes
bullets: Embedded value (EV) = shareholders' share of net assets + present value of future shareholder profits from in-force business, discounted at the RDR. Two separate bases are required: (a) the supervisory reserving basis — used to calculate current net assets and future changes in reserves; (b) the projection basis — used to project future experience to calculate the present value of future shareholder profits.

EV projection basis is typically a realistic best estimate — the aim is to give a genuine assessment of company value rather than a conservative regulatory picture. However, regulations may require a more prudent EV basis for published accounts.

EV is measured regularly (eg quarterly) to track how management is increasing company worth. It can be broken down by distribution channel or subsidiary to measure management contributions. Projected EVs are used as a criterion for strategic and financial decisions — eg choosing between two product strategies.

Appraisal value = EV + present value of future new business profits. Used as a sale/purchase price indicator for mergers and acquisitions. The seller uses realistic assumptions (producing a higher appraisal value); the buyer uses cautious assumptions (producing a lower offer); the final price reflects relative bargaining power.

EV financing: a reinsurer makes a loan to the insurer, repaid from future profits — the reinsurer uses prudent assumptions to protect itself against the risk that profits do not emerge as projected. The loan amount is limited by the reinsurer's assessment of the discounted future profits using those prudent assumptions.

key_terms.definition: A measure of the value of an insurer's in-force business = shareholders' net assets (assets minus current supervisory reserves) + present value of future shareholder profits from existing policies, discounted at the risk discount rate.
EV plus the present value of future new business profits — represents the total value of the insurer including the value expected from future sales; used as a sale/purchase price benchmark.

exam_qa.q: Why is the risk discount rate typically lower when calculating EV of existing business than when pricing a new product?
exam_qa.a: For pricing a new product, the RDR must incorporate a premium for the uncertainty of future sales volume and mix — the insurer does not know how many policies will be sold or what the distribution of risks will be. For existing business, volume and mix are already known (the policies are in force), so this source of uncertainty is eliminated. The remaining risks (morbidity, lapses, expenses, investment) still exist, but overall uncertainty is lower, justifying a lower RDR for the EV projection basis.

subsections.title: Allowing for Risk in EV — RDR, Stochastic, and Market-Consistent Approaches
bullets: Risk allowance in EV: a best estimate EV needs to be reduced to reflect that risky future profits are worth less to shareholders than risk-free cashflows of the same expected amount. Three methods: (a) Elevated RDR — traditional approach; increase the discount rate above the risk-free rate; higher product risk → higher RDR; simple but risk may be difficult to quantify precisely. (b) Stochastic projection — model parameter probability distributions; 1,000+ simulation runs directly measure the variance of future profits; more precise but requires defensible probability distributions. (c) Market-consistent approach — discount at the risk-free rate (swap curve); exclude investment risk from the liability; include an explicit risk margin for non-investment risks (eg using the cost of capital method).

Market-consistent / Solvency II framework: EV = net assets + gross best estimate liability + risk margin. Risk margin = present value of future SCRs × cost of capital rate (eg 6%). Advantage: directly comparable across companies; disadvantage: may not reflect the insurer's actual cost of capital or its actual investment strategy.

Even when not used as the primary EV methodology, a market-consistent valuation provides a useful reasonableness check and ensures that risk margins embedded in traditional approaches are appropriate relative to a market benchmark.

key_terms.definition: A method of calculating the risk margin by projecting the solvency capital requirement (SCR) over future years and discounting the annual cost of holding that capital at the prescribed cost of capital rate (eg 6% under Solvency II) — the total represents the compensation required by a third party to hold the residual non-hedgeable risk.

exam_qa.q: Describe the factors the actuary would consider when using a cashflow approach to price a long-term health insurance product, including what modelling investigations would be carried out.
exam_qa.a: Factors: (1) Parameter uncertainty for each assumption — degree of uncertainty (driven by data credibility and subjectivity of claim definitions) and financial significance of adverse outcomes; (2) Statistical/random fluctuations inherent in future experience — separate from parameter uncertainty; (3) Financial significance of the adverse outcomes — including whether premiums, charges, or policy terms are reviewable, which reduces the financial onerousness of guarantees; (4) Company risk appetite; (5) Proprietors' required return on capital (sets the base RDR); (6) Free assets or parental protection; (7) Competitive nature of the market and company USP — determine the acceptable ceiling on margins. Modelling investigations: (a) Single policy profit test — project profits for a representative model point; perform deterministic sensitivity analysis, varying each parameter through plausible adverse ranges (eg 95th percentile) to calculate revised return on capital; optionally run stochastically to directly model return variance; (b) Model office — incorporate future new business sales into a full company projection model; sensitivity test at the whole-office level to assess the financial impact on solvency and EV; this may change the actuary's view of the financial significance of specific assumptions and lead to different margin choices.

13.summary:
Chapter 13 Summary
General considerations when setting assumptions
When setting assumptions, it is important to:
• consider the use to which the assumptions will be put
• take particular care over assumptions with the most financial significance
• achieve consistency between the assumptions
• consider any legislative or regulatory constraints
• consider the needs of the client
• ensure that the parameters are produced accurately from the data
• ensure that the data used to derive assumptions are relevant to the insured lives
• ensure that bases used are flexible to reflect changing risk circumstances.

Deriving assumptions for pricing purposes
The starting point is a best estimate basis for the assumptions, however, margins will be necessary to guard against adverse future experience.

Where a cashflow model is being used, the risk from adverse future experience may be allowed for through the:
• assessment of what margins to apply to the expected values
• use of a stochastic approach
• risk element of the risk discount rate.

The margins incorporated and the price charged will depend on:
• the competitive nature of the market
• the company’s USP
• the company’s attitude to risk
• the credibility, accuracy, relevance and ‘up‐to‐date‐ness’ of the data
• the size of the company’s free assets or parental guarantee.

Deriving assumptions for reserving purposes
Again, the starting point is a best estimate basis for the assumptions.

For some purposes – including the published accounts and the statutory solvency accounts – margins may be incorporated. Consideration will need to be given to whether the accounts are being produced on a going concern or break‐up basis, whether they are required to give a true and fair view and whether there is any legislation or guidance on the basis or assumptions to use.

For other purposes – including the internal management accounts – margins may not be needed.

It is common in some countries to price prudently and then to define the reserving basis as the pricing basis. In other countries, premiums are calculated using a realistic basis, with risks to the insurer being allowed for through the risk discount rate assumption.

Deriving assumptions for determining profitability
The basis used is likely to be fairly realistic, although it will depend on the specific purpose.

14.title: Assumptions (2) – Demographic Assumptions

subsections.title: Four-Step Process: Data, Grouping, Calculating, Adjusting
bullets: Four-step framework applies to all demographic assumptions: (1) Data selection — choose the most suitable database; data should cover an appropriate period — long enough for credibility but short enough to avoid excessive heterogeneity from generational morbidity differences (typically 3–4 years for morbidity investigations). (2) Grouping — divide into relevant homogeneous risk cells while retaining adequate data volumes in each cell. (3) Calculating rates — usually as a multiplicative/additive adjustment to a standard table (eg UK CMI CI or IP tables), rather than fully independent graduation. (4) Adjustments — modify for differences between the historical data cohort and the expected future policyholders (target market, distribution channel, underwriting standards, calendar-year trends).

Adjustment formula approach: insurer defines e.g. CI incidence rates as i_x = a·i_x(S) + b, where i_x(S) is the standard table rate at age x and a, b are constants calibrated to own experience. Using standard tables rather than independent graduation: (a) reduces error from scarce own data — standard tables based on much larger pooled datasets give more confidence in the rate shape across all ages; (b) reduces risk of significant error; (c) is simpler to implement; (d) aids monitoring — comparing own actual/expected ratios against a common industry standard makes it easier to identify whether deviations are company-specific or market-wide.

Projecting to the future: the pricing basis will be used for a period of time, so all derived rates must be projected forward from the investigation period to the mid-point of expected insurance usage, allowing for both age-related trends (eg worsening morbidity with age) and calendar-year trends (eg medical advances improving CI survival).

If own data is insufficient, external sources are used: reinsurers' data (provided in exchange for accepting a share of the business); industry/market data (eg CMI publications); population data (high volume but lower relevance — requires adjustment for the insured vs population morbidity differential). No universal rule exists for adjusting population data to insured lives — each case requires specific actuarial judgement.

key_terms.definition: A group of insured lives sharing the same values for all factors deemed to affect morbidity experience — eg male, aged 45, smoker, professional occupation, based in London. Too fine a cell subdivision reduces data credibility; too coarse a cell creates heterogeneity and cross-subsidy.

An industry-published set of morbidity or mortality rates based on a large pooled dataset (eg UK CMI IP or CI tables) — used as a benchmark against which individual insurer experience is measured as a ratio or additive adjustment.

exam_qa.q: Why might an insurer prefer to use a standard table as the basis for its CI claim incidence rates rather than independently graduating its own data?
exam_qa.a: (1) Own data is likely to be scarce — independent graduation would be unreliable over large parts of the age range; using a standard table (based on much larger pooled data) gives more confidence in the shape of rates. (2) The risk of significant error is lower when anchored to a standard. (3) It is simpler to implement and explain. (4) When monitoring experience, comparing own actual-to-expected ratios against a common standard makes it straightforward to identify whether deviations are company-specific or indicative of a wider market trend requiring basis change.

subsections.title: Product-Specific Incidence Approaches
bullets: Product-specific structures: IP and LTCI use single claim incidence rates (transition from healthy to claiming state). PMI derives rates separately for each benefit category (in-patient, out-patient, specialist). CI derives rates separately for each main illness category (cancer, heart attack, stroke, CABG, TPD, and others) — each illness is projected forward independently before combination because different diseases have fundamentally different trend trajectories.

CI combination of individual illness rates: simple summation may double-count where a claimant could be diagnosed with multiple conditions that would generate a single CI claim — an adjustment must be made for this overlap.

Key risk factors for subdividing incidence data — long-term products: age; gender (now prohibited for pricing in the EU following ECJ ruling, but retained for internal analysis); smoker status; occupation; benefit size; policy class (stand-alone, accelerated, rider); individual vs group; distribution channel; region. Additional post-entry factors: duration since policy entry; underwriting status (standard vs special terms).

EU gender ruling implications: although insurers within the EU cannot charge different rates by gender, morbidity risk differs materially between men and women. Internal monitoring must continue to track gender-based experience differentials in order to understand portfolio risk and set accurate unisex rates — if the assumed gender mix deviates from actual, over- or under-recovery of claims costs results.

Sophisticated models for IP and PMI may allow for higher incidence rates among lives with a prior claims history (previous claims used as an additional risk factor), as past claiming behaviour is a predictor of future claiming propensity.

key_terms.definition: The rate at which new claims begin — for IP the transition rate from healthy to claiming; for CI the occurrence rate of each insured condition per year; for PMI the rate of claims per benefit category. Measured as claims commencing per life-year exposed.
The total time (in policy-years or life-years) during which a group of lives was at risk of making a claim — the denominator when calculating raw incidence or recovery rates from experience data.

exam_qa.q: Why are individual CI illness incidence rates projected forward separately before being combined into a single overall CI rate?
exam_qa.a: Different illnesses have fundamentally different future trend trajectories. For example: new cancer screening techniques could increase diagnosis rates for cancer but not for stroke; improvements in cardiac surgery may specifically reduce CABG claim rates; changes in diagnostic criteria affect only specific conditions. Projecting separately preserves these distinct trends and allows age-specific adjustments (where morbidity improvements differ across the age range) to be applied correctly. Combining before projecting would mix these distinct trajectories into a single average, obscuring the specific dynamics of each disease and producing less accurate overall rates.

subsections.title: Social, Economic, and Regulatory Influences on Incidence
bullets: State welfare provision: more generous State disability support and acute healthcare provision makes it harder to sell and retain private insurance (reducing demand), but may also affect moral hazard (reducing the financial incentive to return to work under IP). Government planning announcements about future welfare changes are a material factor in both pricing assumptions and lapse rate assumptions.

Economic pessimism and unemployment: encourages employees to use group PMI cover while still employed (higher PMI claim frequency); provides a disincentive for IP claimants to return to work — especially self-employed whose client base has contracted. Economic deterioration also drives higher lapse/non-renewal rates through affordability pressures.

High inflation: directly increases PMI renewal costs, triggering selective non-renewal — healthier policyholders drop cover, worsening the risk profile of the remaining pool. Long-term products are less affected because cover is guaranteed-renewable and requires active lapsing, but index-linked premiums can also cause some degree of selective lapsing.

exam_qa.q: Why are PMI renewal rates more sensitive to inflation than long-term health and care insurance products?
exam_qa.a: PMI contracts expire at year-end; policyholders must actively choose to renew and face the new, higher premium each year. A sharp premium increase can cause many policyholders — particularly the healthier ones who value the insurance less — to decline renewal. Long-term products have contractually guaranteed renewal until expiry; policyholders must actively lapse to stop cover (unlike PMI where inaction = non-renewal), and there may be a strong incentive to retain an existing policy if the insurer has increased new business rates for new policies since the existing policy was taken out.

subsections.title: Recovery Rates — Importance, Drivers, and Adjustments
bullets: Recovery rates are critical for IP insurance (annuity benefit paid until recovery, death, or policy expiry) and for LTCI transitions (though recovery from advanced care need is rare and typically ignored in LTCI pricing). For CI (lump sum payment), recovery rates are irrelevant post-claim.

Recovery rates decrease sharply with claim duration: the majority of sicknesses are short (under 6 months); those still claiming at 6+ months are disproportionately serious/chronic cases with very low recovery probability. Recovery rates must be modelled as a function of age, gender, occupation, cause of claim, benefit size, and duration since both the start of sickness and the start of claim payments.

Key drivers of recovery: (a) Financial incentive — policy conditions that limit total post-claim income (IP benefit + other income) to below pre-disability salary create a direct incentive to return to work; the insurer must monitor over-insurance ratios (benefit to pre-disability income). (b) Rehabilitation — insurer-provided rehabilitation (physiotherapy, counselling, return-to-work schemes) materially improves recovery rates and must be explicitly reflected in assumptions. (c) Benefit size — large-benefit claims continue significantly longer than small-benefit claims.

Medical advances have opposing effects: faster recovery from some conditions (better surgical outcomes, new therapies) reduces average claim duration for those conditions; longer survival in disability (eg better management of MS, stroke sequelae) extends IP and LTCI claim duration. The net effect on total claim costs is ambiguous and must be analysed cause-by-cause.

key_terms.definition: The rate at which IP claimants cease claiming — by recovering, dying, or reaching the maximum benefit period — expressed per claiming life-year at each duration and age. Duration-dependent because serious cases are progressively concentrated as short-duration cases resolve.

exam_qa.q: How do recovery rates vary with (a) duration since the policyholder fell sick; (b) deferred period length; (c) duration since claim payments started?
exam_qa.a: (a) Duration since sickness: Recovery rates are highest in the first weeks/months — most short sicknesses resolve early. Beyond 6 months, those still sick represent an increasing proportion of serious/chronic cases; recovery rates drop sharply and remain low. (b) Deferred period: A longer deferred period means those who do claim have already survived the deferred period without recovery — they are therefore more serious cases with lower recovery rates at any given claim duration. The deferred period also affects the type of individual attracted to the product (long deferred period products attract those expecting only long-duration sicknesses). (c) Duration since claim payments: Similarly to (a), recovery rates tend to decline as payment duration increases, reflecting concentration of more serious cases. Spikes in recovery can occur at specific durations if benefit terms change (eg a reduction in benefit after 2 years) or if specific claims management triggers are applied at defined durations.

subsections.title: Mortality in Health and Care Insurance — Varying Significance
bullets: Mortality of non-claiming lives (IP, LTCI, CI): for products without a significant death benefit, overestimating healthy-life mortality slightly overstates morbidity exposure (eg a 10% reduction in healthy-life mortality increases CI incidence exposure by much less than 1%). Standard insured lives mortality tables with projected improvements are normally adequate; minimal research effort justified.

Mortality of claiming lives (IP, LTCI): requires much more effort — sick lives have substantially higher and more variable mortality than healthy lives; the assumption has a large financial impact because it determines when the income benefit payment stream terminates. Data should be subdivided similarly to claim recovery rates: by age, gender, occupation, cause of claim, and benefit size.

CI survivorship requirement: most CI policies require the claimant to survive 28 days after illness diagnosis for the claim to be valid. The effect of mortality during this survivorship period is captured by reducing the gross claim incidence rate. The adjustment is illness-specific — post-diagnosis survival for cancer differs markedly from stroke or heart attack.

For products without a significant death benefit (eg stand-alone CI, stand-alone IP), neither mortality rate should be over-estimated: for healthy lives, overestimating mortality reduces the exposure to claim risk; for claiming lives, overestimating in-claim mortality reduces the expected claim duration — both would cause under-pricing of the product.

key_terms.definition: The condition in a CI policy requiring the claimant to survive a specified period (typically 28 days) following diagnosis — designed to exclude deaths occurring very shortly after diagnosis, which would be classified as life insurance events rather than CI claims.

exam_qa.q: Why is the mortality assumption more financially significant for claiming lives than for non-claiming lives under IP insurance?
exam_qa.a: Two effects: (1) Financial impact — a claiming life who dies after 2 years of payment has a much lower total claim cost than one who continues claiming for 10 years; death simply terminates what could otherwise be a long annuity-style payment stream. For a non-claiming life, death merely reduces the population exposed to risk of future claims by a tiny margin. (2) Mortality level — the mortality rates of sick policyholders are substantially higher than for healthy lives, so a 10% improvement in claiming-life mortality has a much larger absolute impact on expected claim duration and total claim costs than the same improvement applied to healthy lives.

subsections.title: Data Sources, Limitations, and Drivers of Lapses
bullets: Primary source is own company data for the specific product or the closest similar product. Unlike morbidity and mortality, population data for lapse rates does not exist — there is no population equivalent of lapsing a policy (members of a population can die or become sick, but cannot 'lapse' from the population).

Market data is much less useful for lapse rates than for morbidity because: (a) lapse rates are highly unpredictable — driven by policyholder choice influenced by economic, political, and commercial factors as well as general consumer awareness; (b) significant heterogeneity between insurers (distribution channel, product design, target market, after-sales service all produce very different lapse profiles); (c) past data and future experience are both heavily influenced by economic conditions, competition, and customer service quality — all rapidly changing and difficult to predict.

Lapse data should be split by: distribution type; policy duration; premium payment method (direct debit vs cheque — DD payers have systematically lower lapse rates); premium size; age; gender; smoker status. For CI additionally: policy series/cohort year, policy type (stand-alone vs accelerated vs rider), and sum insured band.

Four categories of driver of lapse decisions — economic (affordability of premium), political (availability of State alternatives), commercial (availability of a cheaper or better competitor product), and awareness (belief in whether the policy is necessary). All four must be considered when making adjustments to derived lapse rates for the expected future environment.

key_terms.definition: The rate at which policyholders stop paying premiums and terminate their long-term policies (or fail to renew short-term policies) — expressed per in-force policy-year. Distinct from surrender where a cash payment is made on withdrawal.

The practice where healthy policyholders lapse their existing policy and purchase a new policy at cheaper current market rates when premium rates are falling — only viable for healthy lives, worsening the remaining portfolio's risk profile and reducing the insurer's average premium income.

exam_qa.q: How would you expect an economic deterioration to affect withdrawal rates for (a) stand-alone CI; (b) pre-funded LTCI; (c) PMI?
exam_qa.a: (a) Stand-alone CI: premiums are relatively low so few policyholders will be unable to afford them; there is typically no surrender value so no financial incentive to lapse. Economic deterioration is not expected to materially increase CI lapse rates. (b) Pre-funded LTCI: premiums are a substantial portion of income; some policyholders will no longer be able to afford premiums. Some LTCI policies offer paid-up policy benefits on withdrawal. An increase in withdrawal rates is expected. (c) PMI: premiums are expensive relative to discretionary income; policyholders make an active annual renewal decision and no surrender value is payable. Economic deterioration is very likely to increase non-renewal rates materially — and the healthiest policyholders (who value the insurance least) are most likely to lapse, worsening the remaining pool's risk profile.

subsections.title: Three Effects of Lapses on Insurer Profitability
bullets: Effect 1 — Early lapse (negative asset share): if the accumulated value of premiums received minus initial expenses (including commission) is negative at the date of lapse, the policy generates an overall loss. Initial commission and underwriting costs are the primary driver of early-year strain; this is why high-commission distribution channels increase lapse risk.

Effect 2 — Late lapse (positive asset share, no surrender value): if a reserve has accumulated and no surrender value is payable, a lapse generates an overall profit for the insurer equal to the asset share at the date of lapse. For lapse and re-entry, however, this profit is offset by fresh commission and underwriting costs for the new policy.

Effect 3 — Selective lapse (lapse deterioration): healthier policyholders lapse at higher rates than sicker ones, leaving an increasingly adverse morbidity pool among continuing policyholders. For PMI, the lowest-claiming members have substantially higher lapse rates than high-claiming members (the latter cannot easily find equivalent cover elsewhere). Sensitivity modelling is essential to understand how selective lapsing could affect profitability and solvency.

Lapse and re-entry risk is most acute during periods of falling premium rates (eg UK CI market in the 1990s): only healthy lives can take advantage; unhealthy lives who would like to re-enter on cheaper terms cannot obtain standard rates. The insurer ends up retaining the unhealthy cohort at the original (pre-fall) premium rates while healthy lives pay lower new-business rates — overall profitability of the portfolio declines and can become negative.

exam_qa.q: Describe how you would determine claim inception and termination rate assumptions for pricing a new short-term accident and sickness (A&S) product, given that the insurer has existing long-term IP experience.
exam_qa.a: Use existing IP data as the primary source — choose a suitable investigation period; split by group/individual, deferred period, and other relevant factors; compare actual rates against expected rates based on a standard IP table. Remove claim termination experience beyond 2 years (not relevant for a short-term product). Adjust the derived rates for: the expected characteristics of the new target market and distribution channel; anticipated changes in underwriting and claims control standards; differences in product design (eg claim definition, benefit structure); gaps in data credibility (eg deferred periods not currently offered); calendar-year trends extrapolated to the mid-point of expected future usage; any known economic conditions that may have distorted historical data. Supplement with: reinsurer data on short-term accident and sickness experience; industry data where own data is thin. Derive average rates weighted by expected mix of business (eg age/gender split). Validate final rates against competitor market premiums as a reasonableness check.

14.summary:
Chapter 14 Summary
Determining demographic assumptions
The process of determining demographic assumptions involves:
• collecting appropriate data – the data would relate to an appropriate period of years, such that the volume of data is adequate, but excessive heterogeneity due to changes over time is not introduced
• grouping the data – the data would be divided into relevant homogeneous groups, subject to adequate levels of data being retained within each cell; some cells may be grouped into broader homogeneous groups
• calculating the rates – in some cases these will be based on an adjustment to a standard table; in others exposed to risk techniques may be used
• adjusting the rates – if the adjusted rates are to apply to a class of lives that is expected to have a different experience from that to which the analysed data relates, then adjustments may need to be made.

It is important to know the risk factors (subdivisions) for each assumption and for each product type.

Claim incidence and recovery rates
The insurer’s own data is likely to be the most relevant source of data. Reinsurers’ data, market data and population data may also be useful.

Data will be grouped into risk cells. Legislation may prevent the use of certain rating factors for pricing, eg gender.

Allowance should be made for trends, both improving and worsening. Key social and economic influences include:
• State benefits
• the economic situation
• inflation.

Claim incidence rates are very important for all product types. Single claim incidence rates will be derived for IP and LTCI. Separate rates will be calculated for different illnesses for CI insurance and for different treatments for PMI.

Claim recovery rates are important for IP insurance. Keenness to return to work, claims management / the involvement of the insurer in rehabilitation and claim size are key influences on recovery rates.

Medical advances should be carefully allowed for, as they can produce opposing effects on overall claim costs, depending on cause of claim and product type.

Mortality
The process of deriving mortality assumptions is similar to that of deriving claim incidence and recovery rates.

Mortality rates are important:
• for claims in payment under IP insurance and LTCI
• for accelerated CI insurance
• within the survival period for stand‐alone CI insurance.

For claims in payment, the mortality assumption is less important pre‐claim, unless a significant death benefit is provided.

Lapse rates
The insurer’s own experience is likely to be the main source of data. Market and reinsurers’ data may also be used, however population data will not exist.

The commercial and economic environment is important.

Lapse rates are more important when:
• they occur early in the policy term, when a financial loss would be made
• a surrender value is payable (where no surrender value is payable and a reserve has been accumulated, a lapse will work to the benefit of the insurer)
• the lapse is selective, leaving a worsening propensity to claim among those remaining
• there is a lapse and re‐entry risk.

15.title: Assumptions (3) – Financial Assumptions

subsections.title: Overview of Financial Assumptions
bullets: Financial assumptions covered in this chapter: benefit amount, benefit inflation, expenses, expense inflation, commission and clawback, investment return, and taxes.
New business volumes and mix assumptions are covered in Ch16.
Material is described primarily in the pricing context but applies equally to reserving and embedded value contexts.
A key guiding principle across all assumptions is consistency — the implied relationships between assumptions (e.g. investment return and inflation) must be realistic.

subsections.title: Long-term Contracts (IP, CI, LTCI)
bullets: For long-term products, the benefit level is generally fixed in the policy terms — no assumption is needed about the amount itself; the average benefit size is part of the business mix assumption (Ch16).

Premiums for nearly all long-term H&C products are expressed as a rate per unit of sum insured (e.g. £3 per £100 pa benefit). The same rate applies for any benefit size, with a single premium calculated for the average benefit size.
Only for very large policy sizes would the insurer alter assumptions — potentially higher expenses due to extra underwriting evidence required.

Underlying experience does vary significantly by benefit size: larger policies tend to have better (lighter) morbidity experience because policyholders come from higher socio-economic groups and face stricter underwriting.

subsections.title: Short-term Contracts (PMI)
bullets: For PMI, the policy promises largely to indemnify medical expenses — the actuary must forecast both the number of claims and the average claim amount.
Best starting point for claim size is an analysis of the insurer's own recent experience; if unavailable, data from a similar insurer is used.

Average claim size from an existing insurer should be adjusted for: differences in policy conditions; differences in negotiated hospital contracts (deals with hospitals to perform specific treatments at agreed standard costs reduce both expected value and volatility of claims); future trends in provider capacity and charges (reduced capacity tends to increase costs).
Alternatively, treatment price lists for private hospitals can be used as a basis.

Hospital deals make claim costs more predictable and help keep them under control; their presence or absence affects both expected average claim cost and its volatility, and hence the margins needed in the basis.

subsections.title: Long-term Contracts (IP, CI, LTCI)
bullets: For most long-term H&C products, the sum insured needs to be inflated periodically if the policy is to remain in line with its original purpose (replacing lost income, covering care costs, etc.).

Increasing both premium and benefit at the same rate in line with an index is not wholly appropriate — the risk increases with age, so initial funding is still required (same cashflow projection modelling as for level-premium contracts).

The per-unit premium rate stays constant throughout the term (as both premium and benefit inflate in the same proportion); the annual increase in expected claim cost due to ageing must be pre-funded through higher-than-needed early premiums.

No simple index exists for medical costs — consumer price inflation (CPI) is often used as an alternative, but it does not capture salary inflation for medical personnel above CPI or costs of modern medical technology.
Many companies avoid unknown future indexed increases by escalating premiums and payouts at a fixed annual percentage (often capped at CPI if lower). The cost is fully modelled.

Most suitable inflation index by product: (1) IP insurance — national earnings index (replacing lost employment income); (2) CI insurance — depends on purpose: care costs index, no inflation (fixed debt repayment), earnings inflation, or price inflation; in practice sum insured is usually fixed; (3) LTCI — index of nursing/residential home costs is most appropriate; insurers often put a ceiling on the rate of benefit inflation to contain costs.
For index-linked contracts, an explicit inflation rate assumption must be included in the model; for fixed percentage contracts, no inflation assumption is needed but the cost is still modelled.
Even where benefits and premiums increase at the same rate, an explicit assumption for that rate must be included in the model.

subsections.title: Short-term Contracts (PMI)
bullets: For indemnity products (PMI and some LTCI), an assumption for medical cost inflation is required.

PMI benefit inflation is a function not just of medical inflation but of: likely changes in medical treatment protocols (new treatments, different specialist staff requirements); the future charging structure of hospitals and consultants (e.g. time-based vs procedure-based charges); the future age profile of the portfolio (age-specific claim amount assumptions may be needed).

subsections.title: Types of Expenses
bullets: Direct expenses relate specifically to a class of business; indirect expenses (overheads) cannot be allocated directly. The distinction is subjective — e.g. property costs of a 'new business admin' department could be direct or overhead.
Direct expenses can be fixed (independent of business volume) or variable (depend on number of policies sold or amount of claims paid).

Expense assumption must cover the incidence of direct expenses from: initial acquisition (initial commission, marketing, sales); initial medical underwriting; initial administration; renewal administration; renewal reward to sales channel (renewal commission); investment; withdrawal expenses; claim administration; termination.

PMI has higher renewal administration costs than long-term products — at renewal, the insurer must contact each policyholder, assess any changed circumstances, and recalculate the premium; costs are lower than initial but higher than for a long-term policy's annual upkeep.

Expense types that are not important for PMI: (1) Investment — short-term nature means reserves are small, investment expense/assumption makes little difference; (2) Withdrawal/termination — both are effectively lapses at renewal; their cost is swamped by overall renewal costs.
In addition to direct expenses, premiums must make an appropriate contribution to indirect (overhead) expenses.
Per-policy expenses are expenses that do not vary by policy size (e.g. policy administration costs).

key_terms.definition: Expenses that can be allocated directly to a specific class (or classes) of business, e.g. underwriting costs for IP insurance.
Expenses that cannot be allocated directly to a class of business, e.g. head office property costs. Each product must contribute a fair share.

The maximum benefit for which an applicant can apply for cover without automatically requiring specified underwriting evidence (e.g. a medical examination or private medical attendant's report). Non-medical limits for H&C products are often lower than for life insurance due to greater morbidity complexity.

subsections.title: Data and Grouping
bullets: Expense assumption values are determined by analysing the company's recent experience for the product type. If insufficient own experience exists, data from a similar line, industry data, or reinsurance company data may be used.

Result of analysis: expenses divided by function (initial, renewal, claim, termination) and by whether the level varies proportionally with premium/benefit or is expressed as an amount per contract (per-policy).
Fixed expenses associated with the product and a suitable contribution to overheads should also be identified.

subsections.title: Loading Expenses into Premiums
bullets: Ideally each policy pays its share of all expenses including a contribution to overheads; in practice a degree of cross-subsidy between policies is unavoidable for practical and competitive reasons.

Expense loadings are often used as the optional margin to secure competitiveness — but only with careful monitoring and on a short-term basis. Failure to cover expenses is an important contributor to insolvency.

In price-sensitive broker channels, insurers sometimes set premiums with expense loadings that only marginally exceed variable expense requirements — justified on the grounds that any positive contribution to fixed expenses is better than zero contribution from an uncompetitive product.
What matters is the total contribution to fixed expenses (and profit) across the whole product, not per individual policy.
Expenses that vary with premium size: loaded as a percentage of premium (e.g. commission).

Per-policy expenses: because premiums are quoted per unit of benefit (e.g. £3 per £100 benefit), the per-policy loading is spread in proportion to benefit size. The insurer calculates a premium for the average benefit size and applies the same rate per unit benefit — meaning large policies subsidise small policies unless corrected.

Three approaches to handle per-policy expense cross-subsidy: (1) Individual calculation of premium for each case using actual benefit level — practical only for large cases; small policies would face unacceptably high expense loadings. (2) Policy fee added to premium (or deducted from income benefit) — most common approach; the fee may need to be reduced below its theoretical level for competitiveness, with the shortfall recovered via a higher percentage-of-premium expense loading, calibrated using a model portfolio. Risk: if more small policies are sold than assumed, the contribution to overheads is insufficient. (3) Sum insured differential — different premium rates by benefit band; requires estimation of expected policy numbers in each band.

Fixed expenses (e.g. product development costs) must also be recouped. The loading must ensure that, for the anticipated volume over the product's lifetime, total loadings at least cover all direct fixed and variable expenses and a fair share of overheads. A circularity problem can arise: future volume depends on price, which depends on the expense loading, which depends on future volume. Resolution: assume volume is insensitive across bands of price and iterate to a final answer.

exam_qa.q: Which of the main H&C products (IP, CI, LTCI, PMI) has the highest renewal administration costs, and why?
exam_qa.a: PMI has the highest renewal administration costs. At renewal, the insurer must contact each policyholder, consider any changes in their circumstances and policy conditions, and recalculate the premium. For long-term contracts, annual renewal is simply a matter of updating records, inflating premiums/benefits, and collecting the premium — much lower cost.

exam_qa.q: Why can the loading for per-policy expenses create a cross-subsidy problem for long-term H&C products, and how can it be mitigated?
exam_qa.a: Because premiums are quoted per unit of benefit, the per-policy expense loading (which is fixed regardless of policy size) ends up being proportional to benefit size — large policies overpay and small policies underpay relative to the actual per-policy cost. This is mitigated by: (1) individual premium calculation for large cases; (2) adding a policy fee to the premium (most common), reduced from theoretical level if necessary for competitiveness, with the shortfall recovered via a higher percentage-of-premium loading; (3) a sum insured differential structure with different rates per benefit band.

subsections.title: Comparison with Life Insurance
bullets: More work is generally required to put an H&C product on the books than a life-only product — the main reason is the extra underwriting effort: non-medical limits are often lower, more information is required, and more time is needed to assess morbidity.
Product development costs are also higher: more effort is needed to derive incidence rates, develop policy literature, and train sales staff — so initial expenses are higher.

H&C products normally have to be actively sold (consumers do not always see the need), requiring considerable time explaining key features — unlike life insurance which is often purchased as a mortgage condition.

Claims expenses are more onerous than for life insurance: more time and medical information are needed to establish claim validity; ongoing income claims (IP, LTCI) require continued maintenance. Main items: administering payments; monitoring claim validity; enforcing changes to benefit entitlement (e.g. reducing IP benefits on partial return to work); rehabilitation efforts; assessing claims to higher benefit levels (e.g. LTCI upgrades).

subsections.title: Setting the Expense Inflation Assumption
bullets: Any product offering a level premium against an increasing risk must incorporate an expense inflation assumption, even if premiums are annually reviewable.

For reviewable contracts: premiums are only changed at review dates if expected future portfolio experience changes. With no change in expectation, premiums are not changed. Since future expense inflation is expected, an allowance is included regardless of reviewability.
For annually renewable contracts (e.g. PMI): an implicit allowance is made by increasing per-policy expenses to the level expected to apply at the average time those expenses will be incurred.
Inflation assumption applies to: renewal administration, claim administration, and policy termination expenses.
Expense inflation is driven partly by price inflation and partly by salary costs (the majority of insurance company costs are staff costs).

Data and adjustments: analyse recent expense inflation experience of the company (or industry if unavailable) to determine the composition of inflation (e.g. 20% price inflation + 80% earnings inflation historically implies that same weighting for future projections, unless reasons exist for the weightings to change).

Factors to consider when setting expense inflation: current rates of price and earnings inflation; expected future rates; differential between return on government fixed interest and index-linked securities (proxy for expected inflation, though approximate — affected by any inflation risk premium in fixed interest bond prices); recent actual company/industry experience.

Two distinct aspects of expense inflation must be modelled when pricing: (1) inflation of expenses during the term of each future policy (from issue to termination); (2) inflation of expenses between 'now' (when premium rates are being set) and the dates at which future policies are actually issued. For example, if initial expenses are £250 now, they might be £260 in year 1, £272 in year 2, etc. — the pricing basis should use an average appropriate for the expected period over which the basis will apply.

subsections.title: Commission
bullets: Commission is often paid at a higher rate on the initial premium than on subsequent premiums, reflecting the additional work involved in the sale.
Commission assumptions are determined by the amounts the insurer intends to pay the distribution system — influenced by current market levels of sales remuneration and/or legislatively imposed rates.
Commission can be expressed as a percentage of premium, a percentage of sum insured, or (occasionally) a fixed sum.

If a single tariff is sold by different distributors with different commission structures, the pricing actuary must forecast the volume mix by distribution channel. This must be monitored against actual experience.

subsections.title: Clawback
bullets: Where initial commission is higher than renewal commission, there is a risk that the insurer loses out if a policyholder lapses early and the product has a level annual commission loading.

Commission is not deemed 'earned' for a number of months into the policy term; the salesperson may be required to repay part of the initial commission if the policyholder lapses during this earning period (clawback).

The insurer takes a 'credit risk' on initial commission paid — the risk that the intermediary defaults on clawback repayment (e.g. due to bankruptcy). The commission loading should not assume full clawback will be obtained in all theoretically due cases.
The actuary must estimate: early lapse rates by product type and distribution channel; the proportion of unearned commission that will actually be reclaimed.

Data sources for clawback: company experience by distribution type (early lapses vs exposed to risk at each clawback-eligible duration); industry, reinsurer, and consultant benchmarks. Credibility of in-house data may be an issue.

Clawback numerical example: initial commission with a two-year earning period, 50% clawback on lapse at end of year 1, 10% lapse rate, 5% of clawback payments not received. Expected clawback = 0.10 × 0.50 × 0.95 = 4.75% of initial commission paid per policy.

key_terms.definition: The recovery by the insurer of part of the initial commission paid to a salesperson when a policy lapses within the 'earning period'. Protects the insurer against paying high upfront commission that has not yet been offset by premium income.
The risk that an intermediary defaults on repayment of clawable commission, e.g. due to insolvency. The insurer's pricing should not assume full clawback recovery in all theoretically eligible cases.

subsections.title: Comparison with Life Insurance
bullets: Commission levels may be higher for H&C products than for standard life insurance, justified by: the extra effort needed by the salesperson to explain H&C benefits to prospective policyholders; the additional information required for the more complex morbidity underwriting process.

subsections.title: Principles and Significance
bullets: The investment return assumption must be consistent with the inflation assumptions — the implied real rate of return must be realistic.

Three factors affect the value assigned to the investment return assumption: (1) the significance of the assumption for contract profitability; (2) the intended investment mix; (3) the extent of reinvestment risk and the ability to reduce it through asset choice.

Significance is driven by: size of reserves relative to cashflows (larger reserves = more profit from investment income = higher sensitivity); duration of the policy (longer term = longer period for reserves to earn returns); extent of investment guarantees (more onerous guarantees require more caution in asset selection, reducing expected return).
Investment return has little relevance for PMI (one-year policies, small reserves, claims settled quickly) but is critically important for single-premium LTCI (large reserves, very long duration).

Importance by contract type: (1) Regular premium WP CI — not very important; reserves relatively small. (2) Regular premium WP pre-funded LTCI — important; large reserves, change in assumption significantly affects price. (3) Single premium WP pre-funded LTCI — very important; largest and longest-held reserves; however, matching assets may be available to secure a known yield at outset. (4) PMI — investment return expected to have minimal impact.

exam_qa.q: Comment on the importance of the investment return assumption for: (1) regular premium WP CI; (2) regular premium WP pre-funded LTCI; (3) single premium WP pre-funded LTCI; (4) PMI.
exam_qa.a: (1) Not very important — reserves are relatively small so profits are insensitive to changes in investment return. (2) Important — large reserves mean a change in the assumption significantly affects the resulting price. (3) Very important — reserves are much larger for much longer; however, matching assets (e.g. index-linked bonds) may be available to partially secure the yield at outset, potentially reducing the required margins. The investment assumption must be set at or below the yield actually securable on the day the single premium is invested. (4) Of minimal importance — one-year policies; claims settled quickly; little investment income.

subsections.title: Intended Investment Mix
bullets: Process for determining the likely future investment return: (1) identify the likely future asset mix backing the contract; (2) investigate current and past returns on those assets; (3) predict future returns from that mix given expected changes in the economic environment.
For contracts with fixed monetary benefits: fixed interest bonds/assets are the primary matching asset.
For contracts with inflation-linked benefits: index-linked bonds are preferred (provided they have good security and are readily available).

For contracts with real increases in benefit or expense outgo (e.g. LTCI): some equity-based investment may be appropriate, given the long expected investment period and need to mirror care cost increases. However, equities are generally only appropriate if benefits are neither fixed in monetary nor in real terms.

For unit-linked products: a managed-fund approach is typical; the investment return assumption reflects the expected mix of equities and bonds within the managed fund over the policy duration (gives the unit-fund growth rate). A separate assumption is also needed for investment return on the insurer's non-unit reserves.

The intended mix is not derived from the contract in isolation — it is also affected by the level of free assets available to the insurer (more free assets = less matching required = potentially higher-returning asset mix possible).

If a market-consistent approach is used (deterministic or stochastic), the investment return can be set as the risk-free rate regardless of the actual underlying assets (risk-neutral calibration). However, for stochastic approaches, investment return volatility and correlation assumptions remain dependent on the actual asset type(s).

subsections.title: Reinvestment Risk
bullets: Reinvestment risk: the uncertainty of the return obtainable from future investment. The best estimate of future investment returns may differ from current yields, and may vary by how far into the future the investment is made.
The overall best estimate investment assumption reflects the expected balance between current and future investment yields.
If future net cashflow is positive, the insurer must buy assets — the more future investment is expected, the more the assumption should reflect expected future yields.
Even with negative future cashflows, asset-liability mismatching by term may still require future buying and selling of assets, making future reinvestment yields relevant.

At contract inception, all investment is future — returns are more certain in the near term than further out. The expected period before the pricing basis is next revised is therefore important in setting the investment assumption.

Reinvestment risk by contract type: (1) Regular premium WP CI — limited exposure; investment income is of limited importance anyway. (2) Regular premium WP pre-funded LTCI — high exposure; cannot match most liabilities until late in the term. (3) Single premium WP pre-funded LTCI — lower than (2) but still some reinvestment risk due to uncertainty of when claims start and need to reinvest future income. (4) PMI — of almost no importance.

key_terms.definition: The uncertainty of the return obtainable from investing future cashflows. Arises because future investment yields may differ from current yields; more significant for long-duration contracts where a larger portion of total investment occurs in the future.

subsections.title: General Principles
bullets: The actuary must allow for rates of tax applicable to the healthcare insurance business in the relevant territory.
Most tax approaches affect the return that shareholders receive for supplying capital, and hence must be considered in the premium basis.
Data/adjustments: use current known tax rates, adjusted for any future changes that are known in advance.

subsections.title: Types of Tax
bullets: Taxes on profits: the insurer is taxed on profits from in-force and new contracts. No changes to individual basis elements are needed, but the premium basis must target a return sufficient to deliver the required net-of-tax profit. Formula: expected PV of future profits per policy × (1 − tax rate) must meet the target profit level. In many countries the tax calculation uses a specific (often restricted) reserving basis — tax authorities typically restrict the level of prudence permitted in reserve calculations to prevent tax deferral.

Taxes on investment income less expenses (I-E basis): the insurer is taxed on (investment income − expenses). The actuary includes investment income and expenses net of the relevant tax rate; the resulting amended profit is the shareholder return. In some territories, initial heavy expenses reduced taxable income significantly, prompting rules to stagger expense relief over several years (e.g. 3 or 7 years) — requiring profit-testing techniques to model correctly.

Taxes on premiums: the insurer is financially neutral as it collects premium tax on behalf of the State; no direct allowance is needed in the pricing model (the tax is simply added to the quoted premium, e.g. +6%). However, changes in premium tax indirectly affect other pricing assumptions — higher premium tax may reduce sales volumes and increase lapse/non-renewal rates, affecting assumptions for new business volumes and persistency.

key_terms.definition: A taxation methodology where the insurer is taxed on the excess of investment income over expenses incurred. Investment income and expenses are modelled net of the applicable tax rate.

exam_qa.q: Q15.1: Suggest two possible problems that might result from using fixed annual percentage increases to IP benefits.
exam_qa.a: (1) If actual living cost inflation exceeds the fixed rate, policyholders become under-insured — benefits fail to cover the costs they were designed for, leading to bad public relations, marketing risk (reduced future sales and/or higher lapse rates). (2) If actual living cost inflation is lower than the fixed rate, policyholders may become over-insured; premiums also rise at the fixed rate, potentially making them unaffordable and increasing lapse rates. Over-insurance is particularly problematic for IP policies where benefits may be capped at claim time to stay within the maximum permissible replacement ratio — policyholders caught in this way may be seriously dissatisfied.

exam_qa.q: Q15.2: For PMI, new policies and renewals are charged the same premium. Suggest and explain how you would allow for the relevant expenses when pricing the product.
exam_qa.a: Initial and renewal expenses: initial expenses (notably initial medical underwriting) are significantly greater than renewal expenses. An assumption is made about the expected number of years policies will be renewed; the excess of initial over renewal costs is amortised over this period. The amortised annual initial expense is added to the expected renewal cost for the year to give the total per-policy administration expense assumption. Commission is treated similarly — initial commission exceeds renewal commission and is amortised over the expected renewal period, expressed as a percentage of premium. Claims administration expenses are typically allowed for as a percentage increase to the assumed average claim cost for the year.

exam_qa.q: Q15.3: Explain how future developments in medicine might affect claim costs under (a) CI insurance, (b) IP insurance, (c) PMI.
exam_qa.a: (a) CI: Medical advances can increase diagnostic rates — causing claims to occur sooner or within the policy term that would previously have occurred after expiry (increases costs, especially the latter). Some illnesses will be newly diagnosed that would never have been recognised (increases costs). Advances may prevent some illnesses altogether (reduces costs). Treatments that are insured events (e.g. heart bypass) may no longer be needed due to medical advances (reduces costs); alternatively, such treatments may become more widely available (increases costs). (b) IP: Effect depends on whether the condition is chronic or temporary. For chronic conditions (unlikely ever to return to work): medical advances prolong life, increasing claim duration and total cost. For temporary conditions: faster recovery hastens return to work, reducing total claim costs; recovery may now occur within the deferred period, eliminating the claim entirely. Advances reducing disease incidence reduce claim costs. (c) PMI: New, more expensive treatments increase costs. Previously expensive treatments may become routine and cheaper (reduces costs). More effective treatments reduce treatment duration and costs (e.g. shorter hospital stays). Some advances may eliminate the need for treatment altogether (reduces costs).

exam_qa.q: Q15.4: Discuss the importance of investment return, expenses, and withdrawal rate assumptions for: (i) without-profits CI insurance; (ii) without-profits immediate needs LTC annuity; (iii) large unit-linked group IP insurance (monthly charge for claims; % of fund for expenses; employer entitled to residual unit fund on discontinuance).
exam_qa.a: (i) WP CI insurance. Investment return: not a key assumption — reserves are relatively small so profits are insensitive; even if wrong, impact is limited. Expenses: second most important assumption after morbidity; competition is price-based with small margins; small typical premiums make expense loadings relatively significant; if too low, volume rises but business is written at a loss; if too high, volume falls and contribution to fixed costs is insufficient. Withdrawal rates: no benefit on withdrawal but lapses still occur; lapses are predominantly among healthier lives, worsening remaining portfolio morbidity; insurer makes a loss if lapse occurs when asset share is negative (early durations); makes a profit if lapse is at positive asset share — so importance differs by duration. (ii) WP immediate needs LTC annuity.
