exam_qa.q: Explain how moral hazard arises on IP policies and state which transition probabilities are affected.
exam_qa.a: Moral hazard arises because policyholders receiving IP benefits have reduced financial incentive to return to work compared to uninsured individuals in the same health situation — the insurance alters their behaviour. Transition probabilities affected: (i) sick-to-healthy (recovery) rate is REDUCED — claimants take longer to return to work; (ii) healthy-to-sick (inception) rate may also INCREASE — insured individuals are more likely to go off work sick while uninsured individuals may struggle on.

subsections.title: CI Insurance Risks
bullets: Main CI risk: rates of diagnosis of specified critical illnesses. Because CI is relatively new in most markets, data are limited, making estimates of historical and future CI rates unreliable — leading to high parameter risk and high probability that actual experience significantly exceeds expectations.

Heterogeneity in CI industry data: different insurers cover different conditions and use different claim definitions. Industry data may therefore be very heterogeneous and misleading as a basis for any individual insurer's assumptions.

Medical advances create CI-specific risks: prolonging life (more illnesses survive to be diagnosed, more stand-alone claims paid); improving screening (earlier, more frequent diagnosis, more claims triggered, potentially before policy term expires); enabling identification and prevention of susceptibility (could improve experience). Net effect depends on the interaction of all three.
Anti-selection is significant on individual stand-alone CI contracts; greatly reduced on group CI. Selective lapses create additional risk on both stand-alone and rider benefit contracts.
Expense risk similar to IP; investment risk usually minimal because reserves are small on most CI products (protection-focused, relatively low claim probability).
Capital requirements for stand-alone CI or marginal requirements for rider CI benefits are of the same order as for IP.

subsections.title: Long-Term Care Insurance Risks
bullets: Main LTCI risk: transition probabilities in a multi-state model with states such as: healthy, needing own-home care, needing residential home care, needing nursing home care, dead (plus withdrawn). No recovery transitions are assumed — policyholders only deteriorate. Transition rates between care states, not just inception rates, are critical.

LTCI data shortage: reliable data are scarce in most markets because the products are relatively new. US data may require significant adjustment due to differences in population health, social attitudes to claiming and self-help, and policy wordings reflecting different State provision levels.

A high proportion of LTCI policyholders eventually make a claim (many people need some form of care before death), leading to significant reserves even on what is technically a protection product. Investment risk is therefore a major consideration.

Additional LTCI-specific risks: investment risk (long pre-claim accumulation period); expense risk; anti-selection; selective withdrawals; moral hazard (managed partly through ADL definitions); marketing/reputational risk (policyholders may incorrectly expect full indemnity of care costs — using 'cost of care needed' as the benefit definition would expose the insurer to theoretically unlimited claims).

Where the policy pays the care provider directly, the insurer risks care provider insolvency after advance payment. Where the policy indemnifies the cost of care, higher-than-expected care cost inflation is the risk.

Capital requirements can be comparable to those for endowment assurance — similar premium-payment-then-waiting-for-benefit structure — and may be high due to the extent of guarantees and high uncertainty in assumptions.
Lapse risk: when asset share is negative there is a financial risk from withdrawal; at other times the risk depends on the relationship between any surrender value paid and the asset share.

key_terms.definition: Standard measures of self-care capability (e.g. bathing, dressing, eating) used in LTCI policy definitions to determine claim eligibility, helping to control moral hazard by requiring objective evidence of need.

The probabilities of transitioning between care states in a LTCI multi-state model (e.g. from own-home care to residential care). Estimation of these is the primary actuarial challenge in LTCI pricing and reserving.

exam_qa.q: Describe the main risks facing a LTCI insurer offering indexed benefits to age 75 under a regular premium contract.
exam_qa.a: Claim transition probabilities — risk of higher-than-expected inception rates and movement to higher care states. Longevity — claimants may live longer than assumed, extending benefit payment. Benefit inflation — benefits rise more than expected; premiums are also indexed but premiums cease after a claim or at 75, so the insurer is most exposed to inflation at long durations and post-premium-cessation. Expenses — higher expense inflation or fixed overhead undercoverage if volumes are low. Investment — substantial fund build-up means investment return is a significant risk; index-linked assets help but may not be available. Persistency — lapse risk when asset share is negative; selective withdrawals leave worse risk pool. Capital strain — high reserves and solvency capital requirements mean heavy new-business strain if volumes exceed expectations.

subsections.title: Private Medical Insurance Risks
bullets: Main PMI risk: limited control over benefit payments. Consultants and anaesthetists set their own charges independently; the insurer's main tools to influence costs are agreed fee schedules, hospital preferred-provider lists, and directing insured members to contracted providers.
Claim frequency risk: the primary trigger for a PMI claim is the GP's referral decision — entirely outside the insurer's direct control.

Anti-selection risk exists for individual and small group PMI; mitigated by medical underwriting at inception. Some planned health requirements (pregnancy, dental, optical) remain a risk even with underwriting.

Volume risk: in markets with a free State health alternative, competition from the State healthcare option creates constant risk of non-renewal — especially in economic downturns when premiums become unaffordable. Loss of business threatens expense coverage and potentially solvency. Cash benefits for using the State alternative mitigate claims costs for less critical treatments.

Accumulation risk: while most PMI claims are modest, the insurer faces risk from individual very large claims (e.g. expensive US treatment) and from accumulations of claims from a single incident (e.g. accident affecting one insured workforce). Most PMI policies specify per-claim limits to mitigate this.

Capital strain under PMI is generally lower than for long-term products because its short-term nature means reserves need only cover a one-year horizon. However, significant new-business cost can arise if enhanced initial commission is promised to recruits or a new sales team.

key_terms.definition: The risk that a single incident (e.g. accident affecting an entire insured workforce) gives rise to multiple simultaneous PMI claims, potentially overwhelming the insurer's aggregate claims budget.

exam_qa.q: List the main risk types for each of IP, CI, PMI, and LTCI.
exam_qa.a:
IP: claim inception and termination rates; anti-selection and selective lapses; moral hazard; lesser risks from mortality, expenses, investment; generally low capital requirements.
CI: rates of illness diagnosis (high parameter risk due to data scarcity); anti-selection and selective lapses; limited investment risk; capital requirements similar to IP.
PMI: third-party control over claim costs; claim frequency driven by GP referrals; anti-selection risk on individual/small group; accumulation of claims from single incidents; volume risk from competition with State provision; generally lower capital strain than long-term products.
LTCI: claim transition probabilities (inception and between care states); longevity; investment risk (large reserves); expense and benefit inflation; anti-selection; moral hazard; marketing/reputational risk on benefit coverage; capital requirements potentially comparable to endowment assurance.

24.summary:
Chapter 24 Summary
The following are some of the risks faced by a health and care insurer:

Guarantees and options
To calculate the cost of guarantees and options, an insurance company will use a model.
Model, parameter and random fluctuations risks therefore occur.

Competition
The need to compete may lead management to take unacceptable risks. This might involve decisions to:
• reduce premium rates or charges under new business contracts
• offer additional guarantees and options under new business contracts
• increase the coverage under existing contracts
• increase salaries or commissions
• arrest the future growth of charges.

Actions of management
The company’s management may choose to ignore the actuary’s advice concerning what the actuary views as unacceptable risk. Possible reasons for this are:
• to be competitive
• to increase the size of the business
• to maximise shareholder earnings.

Counterparties under reinsurance arrangements
If an insurer has reinsurance agreements with other insurers then it is at risk from the possible failure of the reinsurers to cover their share of policy liabilities.

Counterparties in distribution
The risks are that a distributor may:
• commit the insurer to new conditions
• delay premium or claim payments or become bankrupt
• bring the insurer into disrepute.

Counterparties in provision of medical services
There may be some loss of claims cost control to third parties.

Regulatory and fiscal developments
Developments might relate to tax, policy conditions, exclusions and premium rating etc.

Reputational risks
Reputational risks might arise through media exposure, court cases and regulators.

Internal audit failures / fraud
Examples of internal audit failures / fraud are leaking of information and embezzlement of funds.

Physical risks
Examples of physical risks are fire, flood, impact, loss of key staff.

Aggregation and concentration of risk
An example of aggregations / concentrations of risk is the outbreaks of local illnesses.

Catastrophes
Example of catastrophes are epidemics and wars.

Non‐disclosure and anti‐selection
Non‐disclosure makes premium rating more difficult. The extent of this risk depends in part on whether a moratorium approach is used.

There is also a risk that anti‐selection is greater than anticipated in the pricing basis.

Product‐specific risks
IP insurance
• claim inception and termination rates, including anti‐selection
• selective and normal lapses
• to a lesser extent, mortality, expenses and investment
• capital requirements will normally be low

CI insurance
• diagnosis rates, including anti‐selection
• selective and normal lapses
• expenses, and to a lesser extent, investment
• capital requirements will normally be low

Long‐term care insurance
• claim inception and transfer probabilities, including anti‐selection
• investment and expenses
• selective and normal withdrawals
• marketing risk
• capital requirements could be extensive

PMI
• third party control over claims
• single large claims and accumulations
• capital strain is even lower than IP and CI, unless commission is high

This page has been left blank so that you can keep the chapter summaries together for revision purposes.

25.title: Nature of Risks (3)

sections.title: Aims of the Company and the Actuary

subsections.title: Company and Actuarial Aims
bullets: Management's primary financial aims: (i) maximise (or optimise within acceptable risk) total company profits — whether distributed to shareholders (proprietary) or accumulated for mutual policyholders; (ii) maximise (or optimise) the return on available capital, ensuring capital is deployed to its best advantage across competing uses.
These two aims usually align but do not always: Chapter 19 showed that maximising present value of profit and maximising return on capital can sometimes lead to different decisions.

Proprietary company: profits belong to shareholders. Mutual: profits belong to with-profits policyholders. Some structures divide profits between participating policyholders and shareholders in specified proportions.

The actuary's aim is to help the company achieve its aims within the context of: (i) the company's risk profile; (ii) the resources available to it; (iii) the public interest need (as expressed in supervisory legislation) to avoid insolvency; (iv) other applicable legal requirements.

Supervisory insolvency would be disastrous for the company and is therefore the ultimate risk constraint. Note: policyholders will normally receive most or all of the fair value of their policies under insolvency procedures, but continuity of the insurance relationship may be lost unless another insurer takes over administration.

key_terms.definition: An insurance company whose equity is owned by shareholders, who receive all profits.
An insurance company managed on behalf of its with-profits policyholders, who ultimately own all profits accumulated within the company.
The regulatory determination that an insurer does not meet the minimum solvency requirements set by supervisory authorities — the ultimate adverse outcome for an insurance company's risk management.

sections.title: Implications for the Insurance Company — 11 Actuarial Problem Areas

subsections.title: Policy Data, Product Design, and Product Marketability
bullets: Policy data: are the data provided by the company complete and accurate? Data quality is the foundation for all other actuarial work; inaccurate data lead to incorrect advice with potentially serious consequences for both insurer and actuary. Chapter 27 covers data checking procedures.

Product design: what contracts should be offered, and what benefits and design features should be included, given the company's risk profile and available resources? Key considerations: profitability; marketability; competitive rates; capital requirements; risks and sensitivity of profit to adverse deviations; onerousness of guarantees; extent of cross-subsidies; administration and IT systems; regulatory constraints.

Product marketability: how to optimise the balance between price and volumes — for both new business and renewing short-term contracts — given competition? Price and volume have an inverse relationship whose slope depends on demand elasticity, which is strongly influenced by competitive intensity. Additional features (options, guarantees) can improve marketability but must be balanced against the resulting risks and costs.

key_terms.definition: The sensitivity of business volumes to changes in price. High elasticity means a small price increase leads to a large volume reduction; low elasticity means volume is relatively insensitive to price. Competition in the market is the primary driver of demand elasticity for insurance products.

subsections.title: Pricing, Return on Capital, and Profitability of In-Force Business
bullets: Pricing: what is the expected profit, and its variance, from selling a new contract at particular premium rates or charges — and does the company have the resources to sell at those terms? Emphasis: finding a price that makes the policy worthwhile to the insurer without over-stretching capital.

Return on capital: what return on capital will the company expect to make by investing capital in the development and issue of a new contract? Even a profitable product generating the right absolute return may be unattractive if the percentage return on capital deployed is below the company's target.

Conflict between pricing and return on capital: higher prices produce more profit per policy (meeting profitability needs) but may reduce sales volumes (reducing total return on capital invested in product development). The insurer must balance these competing objectives.

Profitability of in-force business: continuous monitoring of existing business profitability is part of the control cycle. If the in-force book becomes unprofitable, remedial actions include: altering benefit levels; amending withdrawal payment scales; adjusting expense/sickness/mortality charges (where reviewable); reducing actual renewal expenses; ensuring new business being written is not susceptible to the same source of loss.

key_terms.definition: The percentage return that the company earns on the capital it has invested in developing and writing a particular product or class of business — a key measure of capital efficiency alongside absolute profit.

subsections.title: Supervisory Reserves, Investment, Capital Management, Risk Management, and Claims
bullets: Supervisory reserves and solvency capital: assumptions used must be prudent — the overall goal of holding reserves plus solvency capital is to ensure a high probability of meeting all future liabilities. Higher required security (e.g. VaR at 99.9% vs 99.5%) demands greater prudence and hence more capital.

Tensions with supervisory reserves: (i) vs return on capital — more capital reduces return and may require higher premiums, reducing marketability; (ii) vs profitability — larger reserves defer recognition of profit, reducing present value; (iii) vs investment freedom — larger reserves reduce free assets, restricting investment policy; (iv) vs capital management — larger reserves consume capital needed for business development.

Investment: the insurer has two potentially conflicting aims — meeting guaranteed benefits with high certainty, and adopting an adventurous policy for best returns. Free assets provide the cushion for adventurous investment; larger free assets permit a more aggressive investment stance.

Capital management: the insurer must be able to achieve its short, medium, and long-term plans within available capital. Projecting future assets and liabilities helps assess coherence of investment, new-business, and expense plans. Certain products cause capital strain when sold at high volumes — a profitable and marketable product can still create capital management problems.

Risk management: underwriting and reinsurance are the two primary risk control tools for a health and care insurer. Both cost money, so increased use reduces expected profit; the benefit is reduced risk exposure. The trade-off must be managed carefully.

Claims: are procedures adequate? Are controls being properly followed? Are there effective fraud control measures? Third-party involvement in health claims (e.g. managed care organisations, claims assessors) increases the risk of claims deterioration without strong procedures and monitoring.

key_terms.definition: The excess of an insurer's assets over its supervisory liabilities and required solvency capital — representing the buffer available to absorb adverse experience and, where large enough, to support more adventurous investment.

The continuous process by which an insurer plans, sets assumptions, monitors experience, compares actuals to expected, and revises assumptions and plans in light of what it has learned — including ongoing monitoring of in-force business profitability.

exam_qa.q: State the problems that confront an actuary in helping a health and care insurer achieve its aims.
exam_qa.a: 1. Policy data — ensuring data are complete and accurate. 2. Product design — determining what contracts to offer given risk profile and resources. 3. Product marketability — optimising the balance between price and volume (new business and renewals) given competition. 4. Pricing — estimating expected profit and its variance from selling new contracts, and assessing whether the company can afford the resource commitment. 5. Return on capital — estimating the return on capital from new product development and issuance. 6. Profitability — projecting expected profit and variance from in-force business. 7. Supervisory reserves — choosing prudent assumptions for reserves and solvency capital. 8. Investment — advising how to invest assets to maximise return within available resources. 9. Capital management — ensuring the company can achieve its long-term plans given available capital. 10. Risk management — applying underwriting and reinsurance to keep risk profile within resources. 11. Claims — assessing adequacy of claims procedures, controls, and fraud measures.

exam_qa.q: A proprietary health insurer writing CI business finds its revised premium rates are uncompetitive. Discuss the reasons and possible responses.
exam_qa.a:
Possible reasons for uncompetitiveness — CI rates: heavier experience than competitors due to weaker underwriting, less sophisticated rating (e.g. no smoker/non-smoker split), weaker claims management, different target market/distribution channel, or more generous CI coverage/weaker claim definitions. Expenses: higher than competitor average, e.g. excessive commission, low salesforce productivity, high overheads. Lapses: worse (heavier) lapse experience, inflating per-policy expense allocations. Profit target: set too high relative to market norms.
Possible responses — CI rates: strengthen underwriting and rating; strengthen claims management; change target market/distribution channel; standardise claim definitions; or, if evidence justifies, use less conservative CI assumptions or switch to reviewable premiums. Expenses: reduce commission if above market rates; improve marketing efficiency and salesforce productivity; reduce administrative overheads. Lapses: improve sales methods and training. Profit target: reprice at reduced target return; reduce overhead contribution requirement.

25.summary:
Chapter 25 Summary
Aims
A key aim of a health and care insurer’s management is to maximise profit and return on capital. The actuary will wish to support this aim, but in the context of:
• the company’s risk profile
• the resources available to the company
• the public interest need for the company to avoid insolvency, and
• applicable legislation.

Implications for the insurance company
The problems that confront the actuaries in achieving their aims relate to:
• policy data
• product design
• product marketability
• pricing
• return on capital
• profitability of in‐force business
• supervisory reserves and solvency capital requirements
• investment
• capital management
• risk management
• claims.

26.title: Reinsurance

subsections.title: Introduction and Key Language
bullets: Reinsurance is insurance purchased by an insurer. The insurer (also called the cedant or direct writer) passes some of its risk to the reinsurer, who has no direct contact with the original policyholders.

Key terminology: 'cede' means to pass on risk; 'cession' refers to risks passed on; 'facultative' means individually negotiated; a 'treaty' is an arrangement covering a group of policies that the reinsurer is contractually obliged to accept.

Reinsurance commission: a payment from the reinsurer to the insurer, typically under proportional original-terms arrangements. When structured as a deduction from the reinsurance premium it is called a rebate.

Even under PMI policies (where the claim amount is not fixed), insurers can estimate a likely maximum cost per policy from past experience. The insurer compares this to its free assets to determine the appropriate extent and method of risk transfer.
The overriding factors governing the reinsurance decision are: the insurer's attitude to risk and the value for money offered by the reinsurance market.

key_terms.definition: The insurer that passes (cedes) risk to a reinsurer. Also called the direct writer or primary insurer.
The portion of risk that the cedant passes to the reinsurer under a reinsurance arrangement.
Individually negotiated reinsurance where the cedant is free to choose which reinsurer to approach and the reinsurer is free to accept or decline each risk.
A standing agreement covering a defined class of risks automatically, within agreed terms. The reinsurer is obliged to accept risks falling within the treaty's scope.
A payment from the reinsurer to the cedant, typically under proportional original-terms arrangements, compensating the cedant for acquisition and other initial costs.

subsections.title: Limitation of Exposure and Avoidance of Large Single Losses
bullets: Reinsurance limits an insurer's exposure to the unpredictable variance of claims outgo. Even with advanced statistical analysis, future claims cannot be predicted with certainty.

Factors determining appetite for reinsurance: size of insurer (larger insurers have bigger reserves and more diversified portfolios — but may also write larger, more unusual risks); experience in the market (more experience means better ability to predict claims and less need for reinsurance); volume of free assets (larger cushion means less need); portfolio size (larger and more diversified produces smoother results); predictability of the business outcome.
These factors interact — an insurer with large free assets may still use substantial reinsurance if it writes highly volatile business.

Avoiding large single losses: excess of loss (XL) reinsurance caps the insurer's cost per claim, converting potentially catastrophic individual losses into a manageable, predictable annual premium outgo.

exam_qa.q: Describe how the size of the insurer, its free assets, and its portfolio size affect the amount of reinsurance it is likely to use.
exam_qa.a:
Size of insurer: larger insurers have greater reserves and more diversified portfolios, reducing exposure to random fluctuation — they may need less reinsurance. However, large insurers may also write larger and more unusual risks and face greater accumulation risk, which could increase reinsurance need.
Free assets: the larger the free asset buffer, the greater the capacity to absorb adverse claims experience without reinsurance support.
Portfolio size: a larger, more diversified portfolio produces more credible experience data and smoother aggregate results — reducing the need for external risk transfer. These factors should not be viewed in isolation; an insurer with large free assets may still use extensive reinsurance if its claims experience is highly volatile.

subsections.title: Smoothing Results and Availability of Expertise
bullets: Smoothing results: almost all reinsurance types smooth profits by reducing the variance of claims outgo. Stop loss directly caps aggregate claims; quota share reduces the size of each individual claim pro rata. Smoother results are preferable to shareholders and improve capital efficiency.

Availability of expertise: when entering new risk areas (new product, new territory), the reinsurer provides technical assistance — product design, pricing, underwriting and claims management guidance. Quota share with a low initial retention is the typical structure; as the insurer's confidence grows, the retention increases and reliance on the reinsurer decreases.

subsections.title: Increasing Capacity and Financial Assistance
bullets: Increasing capacity: reinsurance allows an insurer to accept risks it could not retain on its own due to insufficient capital. Solvency capital requirements are normally reduced in proportion to the amount ceded — but the reduction may be capped because of reinsurer default risk (the cedant remains ultimately liable to policyholders if the reinsurer fails).

Financial assistance — new business strain: initial acquisition and set-up costs exceed premiums received in early years. A reinsurer can provide an upfront commission (effectively a loan repaid through higher future reinsurance premiums or lower future commissions) to relieve this strain. Quota share is the usual structure.

Financial assistance — improving free assets: for a profitable renewing portfolio, the reinsurer may pay an upfront commission in exchange for a share of future surpluses. Unlike a bank loan, this is 'off balance sheet' under many regulatory regimes — assets increase but no matching liability is recorded. However, under Solvency II and other realistic reporting regimes, this benefit may not be recognised.

Financial assistance — merger/acquisition: the reinsurer may advance funds to help the acquiring insurer finance a purchase, against anticipated future profits of the acquired business. Quota share or other proportional forms are typically used.

key_terms.definition: The cash shortfall in the early years of a long-term policy when initial costs and reserve requirements exceed premiums received. Reinsurance commission can be used to finance this strain.
A financial arrangement where a contingent liability does not appear as a formal liability on the insurer's regulatory balance sheet, improving its apparent solvency position.

exam_qa.q: Why is the allowance for reinsurance in reducing solvency capital requirements typically subject to an upper limit?
exam_qa.a: The key reason is reinsurer default risk. Even if the cedant has reinsured 100% of its business, it remains the party contractually liable to policyholders. If the reinsurer were to default, the cedant would still need to meet policyholder claims from its own resources. Therefore, a minimum level of capital must always be maintained regardless of the extent of reinsurance cover.

subsections.title: Arbitrage and Other Reasons
bullets: Solvency capital arbitrage: if the reinsurer is required to hold less solvency capital per unit of risk than the insurer (due to regulatory differences), the total cost of holding capital across the arrangement is lower. The reinsurer can share this saving by offering attractive reinsurance terms, benefiting both parties.

Tax arbitrage: if the reinsurer is subject to a lighter tax regime, reinsurance can be structured to pass income through the more lightly taxed entity, with savings shared between insurer and reinsurer.

Additional reasons: diversification (insurer can write more policies by retaining a smaller share of each); opportunistic cheap reinsurance when market rates are low; improving market standing with rating agencies; reciprocal business arrangements with another insurer.

key_terms.definition: Using reinsurance to exploit a difference in solvency capital requirements between the insurer and reinsurer, reducing the total system-wide cost of holding capital.
An arrangement where two insurers swap quota shares of their respective portfolios, achieving better risk diversification for both parties.

subsections.title: Facultative Reinsurance
bullets: Facultative: both parties have freedom of action — the cedant may approach any reinsurer; the reinsurer may accept or decline each risk individually. Used for individual risks outside the normal underwriting range (unusually large sums insured, atypical medical histories).
Advantages: flexibility for both parties; cedant can shop around for the best terms on each risk.

Disadvantages: time-consuming and costly to arrange per-risk; no certainty of cover being available when needed; terms may be unacceptable even when cover is available; insurer cannot automatically accept business until reinsurance is confirmed, potentially damaging its market standing.

subsections.title: Treaty Reinsurance
bullets: Treaty: a standing agreement under which risks within the treaty's scope are automatically reinsured without individual negotiation. The treaty specifies all terms and conditions in advance.

Arrangements can be: Facultative/Facultative (rarely formalised); Facultative/Obligatory (insurer can choose whether to cede, reinsurer must accept); Obligatory/Obligatory (both parties must operate within treaty terms — the most common in practice).

Advantages over facultative: efficient (risks ceded automatically); certain (cover guaranteed within treaty terms); administratively cheaper (no per-risk negotiation); supports solvency and growth management.
Disadvantage: inflexible once established — both parties are bound by treaty terms until the next renewal.

In health and care, most reinsurance is on a treaty basis. Long-term products favour proportional treaties (quota share or surplus); short-term products may use either quota share or non-proportional (XL) treaties.

The reinsurer protects itself under an obligatory treaty by carefully specifying acceptable business, reviewing the cedant's underwriting procedures, and retaining the right to reprice or exit at treaty renewal if experience deteriorates.

key_terms.definition: The most common treaty structure: the cedant must cede defined risks and the reinsurer must accept them, within the agreed terms and conditions.
The cedant has discretion over whether to cede each risk, but the reinsurer is obliged to accept any risk the cedant chooses to cede.

exam_qa.q: List the main disadvantages of facultative reinsurance to the cedant.
exam_qa.a:
1. It is time-consuming and expensive to arrange individually for each risk.
2. There is no certainty that the required cover will be available when needed.
3. Even when available, the price or terms may be unacceptable.
4. The insurer may be unable to accept business immediately, as it must first find reinsurance — this delays acceptance and can damage the insurer's standing and relationships with distributors.

subsections.title: Original Terms vs Risk Premium Basis
bullets: Proportional reinsurance: risk and premium are shared between cedant and reinsurer in agreed proportions. Two premium sub-types: original terms and risk premium.

Original terms: the reinsurer receives the same proportion of the original premium as its share of the benefit. The reinsurer pays the cedant a commission to cover the cedant's acquisition costs and part of other initial expenses on the reinsured portion.

Risk premium: the reinsurer independently sets its own premium rate (independent of the cedant's premium), reflecting the reinsurer's assessment of expected claims plus its expense and profit loadings. Changes in the cedant's premium rates do not automatically require changes in reinsurance rates — giving the cedant more pricing flexibility.

Risk premium rates may be level over the policy term (net level premium arrangement) or increasing annually as the probability of claim rises with age (yearly risk premiums). Either may be guaranteed or reviewable.

Deposits back: in some jurisdictions, regulators require the reinsurer to deposit its share of the reserve back with the cedant. The cedant holds and invests the full reserve, retaining all investment income. The reinsurer effectively covers only the sum at risk (benefit minus reserve). This also reduces reinsurer default risk to the cedant.
Profit commission: reinsurers may include a profit commission clause — paying back a share of profits to the cedant if the reinsured portfolio performs well. This aligns both parties' incentives.

key_terms.definition: The reinsurer receives the same proportion of the original premium as its share of the benefit. Commission is paid by the reinsurer to the cedant.

The reinsurer independently sets its own premium rate for the risk it covers, regardless of the cedant's premium. Can be level (net level premium) or increasing (yearly risk premium) over the policy term.
An arrangement where the reinsurer deposits its share of the statutory reserve with the cedant, so the cedant holds and invests the full reserve and retains all investment income.
A payment from the reinsurer to the cedant based on profits achieved on the reinsured portfolio, used to share good experience and align incentives.
The excess of the sum insured (or total benefit) over the reserve held at the valuation date. Represents the insurer's true net exposure on a long-term contract.

exam_qa.q: Explain the rationale for reinsuring only the sum at risk rather than the full sum insured on a long-term contract.
exam_qa.a: At any point in time, the insurer already holds assets equal to the statutory reserve for the policy. If a claim occurs, the insurer only needs additional funds to the extent that the sum insured exceeds the reserve — the sum at risk. Reinsuring the full sum insured would mean paying reinsurance premiums on the reserve-backed portion unnecessarily, transferring value to the reinsurer for no useful risk protection. Since reinsurance carries a net cost (reinsurance premiums exceed expected recoveries), this wastes profit.

subsections.title: Quota Share
bullets: Quota share: a constant proportion (R%) of every risk within the treaty's scope is ceded to the reinsurer. The reinsurer receives R% of premiums and pays R% of claims. Always a treaty arrangement.
Can operate on either an original terms or risk premium basis; for short-term business only original terms quota share is used.

Purposes: spread risk (reduces parameter risk — ideal when entering new products or territories); enable writing of larger portfolios (retaining 50% of 1,000 risks is better diversified than 100% of 500 risks); encourage reciprocal business; improve solvency ratio (reduces net written premiums, increasing the free assets/NWP ratio); satisfy statutory capital requirements; finance new business strain via commission.
Advantages: administratively simple; directly reduces solvency capital; useful for financing; reciprocity possible.

Disadvantages: inflexible — cedes the same proportion regardless of sum insured, so does not efficiently target large individual risks; passes a share of all profits to the reinsurer even on small low-risk policies.

key_terms.definition: Proportional reinsurance where a fixed percentage of every risk in a defined class is automatically ceded to the reinsurer. The reinsurer receives that percentage of premiums and pays that percentage of claims.
The risk that the assumptions used in pricing (e.g. claim incidence rates) are incorrect — particularly high for new products or territories with limited data.

exam_qa.q: A health insurer has a 50% original terms quota share. A risk has a gross premium of £10,000; the cedant pays 15% commission to intermediaries and receives 20% commission from the reinsurer. (i) Calculate net premiums for insurer and reinsurer after commissions. (ii) If a claim of £50,000 arises, how much can the insurer recover?
exam_qa.a:
(i) Gross premium split: £5,000 to insurer, £5,000 to reinsurer.
Insurer pays intermediary commission: 15% × £10,000 = £1,500.
Insurer receives reinsurance commission: 20% × £5,000 = £1,000.
Net to insurer: £5,000 − £1,500 + £1,000 = £4,500.
Net to reinsurer: £5,000 − £1,000 = £4,000.
(ii) Reinsurer pays 50% of the £50,000 claim = £25,000.

exam_qa.q: State the main advantages and disadvantages of quota share to an insurer.
exam_qa.a:
Advantages: spreads risk and reduces parameter risk; enables the insurer to write a larger, better-diversified portfolio; may generate reciprocal business; directly improves the solvency ratio (reduces net written premiums); can satisfy statutory capital requirements; administratively simple; can finance new business strain via commission.
Disadvantages: cedes the same proportion regardless of the size of each risk — not efficient at targeting the largest exposures; automatically passes a share of all profits (including from small, low-risk policies) to the reinsurer.

subsections.title: Surplus Reinsurance
bullets: Surplus reinsurance: the cedant sets a retention limit (monetary amount per risk). For each policy, the amount of the sum insured above the retention is ceded to the reinsurer. Policies with sum insured at or below the retention are not reinsured at all.

Only applicable where the sum insured is fixed and known at policy inception — used exclusively for long-term health products (CI, IP with fixed lump sums). Not applicable to PMI (indemnity basis, individual claim amount unknown at outset).

The proportion ceded varies from policy to policy based on the sum insured relative to the retention. Example (retention £75,000): Policy A SI = £150,000, 50% ceded; Policy B SI = £100,000, 25% ceded; Policy C SI = £50,000, 0% ceded.

Purpose: enables the insurer to accept large individual risks while capping its per-policy exposure. More efficient than quota share at targeting morbidity risk — it reinsures a high proportion of very large policies but nothing on small policies, directly reducing claims volatility.

Advantages: allows the insurer to accept risks that would otherwise exceed its writing capacity; reduces concentration of risk on individual large policies; more efficient than quota share at targeting the largest exposures.

Disadvantages: less effective at managing parameter risk across the entire portfolio (the insurer's aggregate share of risk depends on the mix of policy sizes actually written); less suitable for financing new business strain.

key_terms.definition: Proportional reinsurance where the cedant retains a fixed monetary amount per risk (the retention) and cedes all benefit above that level to the reinsurer. The proportion ceded varies by policy size.
The maximum monetary amount of sum insured the cedant retains on any single risk under a surplus treaty. Risks with sum insured at or below the retention are not reinsured.

exam_qa.q: Explain the key difference between quota share and surplus reinsurance, and explain why surplus is more efficient at targeting large individual risks.
exam_qa.a: Under quota share, the same fixed percentage is ceded for every policy regardless of its sum insured. Under surplus, the cedant sets a monetary retention limit and only cedes the amount of each policy's sum insured that exceeds this limit — so the percentage ceded varies by policy and is zero for small policies below the retention. Surplus is more efficient at targeting large risks because it directs reinsurance coverage precisely at the policies where the cedant's concentration risk is greatest (high sum insured), while retaining the full premium income on small policies where the risk is manageable. This produces a better reduction in claims volatility per unit of reinsurance premium paid.

subsections.title: Excess of Loss — Structure and Mechanics
bullets: Non-proportional (excess of loss, XL) reinsurance: the cedant bears all losses up to a lower limit (the retention or excess point); the reinsurer covers losses above this up to an upper limit. Beyond the upper limit, exposure reverts to the cedant — hence the insurer typically purchases multiple stacking layers of XL cover.
Layers must be contiguous — no gaps. A gap between the top of one layer and the bottom of the next leaves the cedant fully exposed in that range.
XL cover is expressed as: '[layer size] in excess of [lower limit]', e.g. '£150,000 xs £50,000' covers losses between £50,000 and £200,000.

Indexed limits (stability clause): where medical cost inflation is significant, limits may be indexed to an appropriate inflation index, preserving the real value of the cover. The cedant typically pays an additional premium if the retention is not indexed.

Proportional within non-proportional: the reinsurer may cover only a proportion (e.g. 90%) of claims within the layer, retaining a 10% co-participation for the cedant. This preserves the cedant's incentive to manage large claims prudently.
No reinsurance commission is payable under XL arrangements (unlike proportional RI), though profit commission is possible for lower layers.

Non-proportional reinsurance is generally appropriate only for short-term contracts (e.g. PMI) and possibly group long-term contracts — not for individual long-term contracts with fixed sums insured (where claim size is already known and XL is unlikely to attach).

key_terms.definition: Non-proportional reinsurance where the reinsurer covers claim costs above a specified lower limit (retention) up to an upper limit. The cedant bears losses below the retention and above the upper limit.
The amount of each claim (or aggregate claims) the cedant bears before the XL reinsurance attaches.
A provision in an XL treaty that indexes the retention and/or upper limit to an inflation measure, preserving the real value of the cover over time.
An XL structure where the reinsurer covers only a fixed percentage (e.g. 90%) of losses within the layer, leaving a co-participation for the cedant to maintain its claims management incentive.

exam_qa.q: An insurer has three XL layers: £140,000 xs £60,000; £300,000 xs £200,000; £2m xs £700,000. (i) Identify the problem with this programme. (ii) Calculate recoveries for claims of: (a) £80,000; (b) £280,000; (c) £2,400,000; (d) £4,000,000; (e) £680,000; (f) £50,000.
exam_qa.a:
(i) There is a gap in cover between £500,000 (top of layer 2: £200,000 + £300,000) and £700,000 (bottom of layer 3). Losses in this range are wholly unprotected by the reinsurance programme.
(ii) Recoveries:
(a) £80,000: Layer 1 covers £80k − £60k = £20,000. Total recovery: £20,000.
(b) £280,000: Layer 1 full = £140,000; Layer 2 covers £280k − £200k = £80,000. Total: £220,000.
(c) £2,400,000: Layer 1 = £140,000; Layer 2 = £300,000; Layer 3 covers £2,400k − £700k = £1,700,000 (within layer 3 capacity of £2m). Total: £2,140,000.
(d) £4,000,000: Layer 1 = £140,000; Layer 2 = £300,000; Layer 3 full = £2,000,000. Total: £2,440,000. Cedant bears the remaining £1,560,000 (above layer 3 upper limit of £2.7m).
(e) £680,000: Layer 1 = £140,000; Layer 2 = £300,000; gap applies (£500k−£700k range unprotected). Total recovery: £440,000.
(f) £50,000: Below retention — recovery: £0.

exam_qa.q: Why in practice would a cedant still be motivated to handle large claims prudently even when 100% of the loss above the excess point is paid by the reinsurer?
exam_qa.a: Three reasons: (1) the cedant will want to maintain a good long-term relationship with the reinsurer — a reputation for poor claims management will result in higher renewal premiums or loss of cover; (2) the treaty may include a profit commission clause that rewards the cedant for favourable claims experience within the layer; (3) future reinsurance premiums are based partly on past claims experience, so excessive claims inflate the cedant's future reinsurance costs.

subsections.title: Risk XL, Aggregate XL (Stop Loss) and Catastrophe XL
bullets: Risk XL: operates on individual claims. The reinsurer covers the excess of any single claim above the retention. Most applicable to PMI portfolios where large individual treatment costs occasionally arise.

Aggregate XL / Stop loss: the excess point and upper limit apply to total aggregated claims over a 12-month period, not individual claims. Used where large numbers of individually small claims could collectively damage profitability (e.g. a regional outbreak, high-volume group scheme). In health insurance, aggregate XL and stop loss are treated as equivalent — morbidity is the only peril.

Stop loss limits are typically expressed as a percentage of earned premiums (e.g. lower limit 110%, upper limit 130% of earned premiums), not absolute monetary amounts — to prevent the insurer from artificially triggering the treaty by expanding volume.

Reinsurers are reluctant to offer stop loss because they have limited control over the cedant's underwriting and claims decisions. When offered, they typically impose a cedant co-participation and/or require oversight of underwriting and claims settlement.

Catastrophe XL: covers losses arising from a single common event (e.g. a chemical explosion, epidemic). Triggered when aggregate losses from one event exceed the retention. Standard pandemic risk is generally not available at affordable premiums because of its extreme uncertainty — government support may be the only realistic backstop.
Profits on long-term individual insurance contracts are rarely sufficiently volatile to warrant stop loss protection.

key_terms.definition: Excess of loss reinsurance that operates on individual claims — the reinsurer pays the excess of any single claim above the agreed retention.

Non-proportional reinsurance where the excess point and upper limit apply to total aggregate claims (not individual claims) over a specified period (usually 12 months). In health insurance the terms are used interchangeably.
Non-proportional reinsurance triggered when losses from a single common event (e.g. epidemic, explosion) in aggregate exceed the retention.
Total claims incurred divided by earned premiums. Used as the basis for setting stop loss limits — typically expressed as a percentage of premiums.

exam_qa.q: A PMI insurer has stop loss cover: lower limit 105% of earned premium, upper limit 125%, reinsurer covers 80% of claims within the layer. Earned premiums are £292m and incurred claims are £333m. Calculate the reinsurance recovery.
exam_qa.a:
Claims ratio = £333m / £292m = 114%.
Lower limit = 105% × £292m = £306.6m.
Upper limit = 125% × £292m = £365m.
Claims within the layer = £333m − £306.6m = £26.4m.
Reinsurance recovery = 80% × £26.4m = £21.12m.

exam_qa.q: Why are stop loss limits expressed as a percentage of earned premiums rather than as absolute monetary amounts?
exam_qa.a: If limits were fixed in monetary terms, the insurer could write substantially more business, increasing the likelihood of triggering the stop loss without the reinsurer having been paid an appropriate additional premium. Expressing limits as a percentage of earned premiums ensures that both the attachment point and upper limit rise proportionally with the volume of business written, keeping the cedant's co-participation proportionate and maintaining appropriate reinsurance pricing.

exam_qa.q: Explain why reinsurers are often reluctant to offer stop loss cover, and what conditions they typically impose if they do.
exam_qa.a: Reinsurers are reluctant because: (1) they have limited influence over the cedant's initial underwriting and claims settlement decisions that directly drive aggregate losses; (2) historically, stop loss contracts have produced poor results for reinsurers. If offered, conditions typically include: a cedant co-participation within the layer (e.g. reinsurer covers only 80%); requirements for some degree of oversight or approval rights over underwriting standards and claims management procedures.

subsections.title: Purposes and Pros/Cons of Non-Proportional Reinsurance
bullets: Primary purpose of XL: permits the insurer to accept risks that could produce very large individual claims or large aggregate losses — losses that are large relative to the insurer's free assets or premium income.
Secondary purposes: reduces insolvency risk from a catastrophe or claims aggregation; stabilises technical results by reducing claims variability — smoother profits are preferable to shareholders.
Capital efficiency: lower claims volatility means the insurer can write the same volume of business with less capital, as lower reserves are required to cover tail scenarios.

Disadvantage: the reinsurer loads its premium for expenses, profit and contingency margins — so in the long run the cedant pays more than its expected recoveries. XL premiums can also be cyclical — rising sharply after years of poor reinsurer results, potentially making cover expensive or unavailable exactly when needed most.

subsections.title: Features and Structures of Financial Reinsurance
bullets: Financial reinsurance (Fin Re): contracts designed primarily to manage the insurer's capital and accounting position rather than to transfer insurance risk. Little or no genuine insurance risk is transferred to the reinsurer.

Two main structures: (1) risk premium reinsurance with financing element — reinsurance commission acts as an upfront loan, repaid through inflated future reinsurance premiums; (2) contingent loan (surplus relief) — the reinsurer advances a loan repayable only out of future profits (VIF), so the repayment obligation may not need to appear as a regulatory liability.

Fin Re achieves its accounting/solvency benefit only under regimes where realistic liabilities are not required and future profit streams are not already recognised as assets. Under Solvency II and similar realistic reporting frameworks, the benefit of Fin Re is significantly reduced or eliminated.

Contingent loan: repayments are contingent on future profits materialising. If profits do not emerge, the reinsurer makes a capital gift to the insurer. Because repayments are contingent, the insurer may not need to reserve for them — assets rise (loan received) but liabilities do not rise by an equivalent amount, improving apparent supervisory solvency.

The realistic embedded value (EV) is broadly unchanged by Fin Re: the initial loan increases assets, but the present value of future loan repayments (which reduce future profit) approximately offsets this increase. However, Fin Re reduces the risk to the EV — part of the uncertain VIF is converted into certain current cash.
Contingent loan repayments will be higher than equivalent non-contingent repayments because the reinsurer must price for the possibility that some repayments will not be made.

key_terms.definition: Reinsurance contracts designed primarily to improve the cedant's accounting or supervisory solvency position, with little genuine insurance risk transfer.
A financial reinsurance structure where the reinsurer advances a loan repayable only out of future profits (VIF). May be off balance sheet under some regulatory regimes, improving apparent solvency.
The present value of future profits embedded in the current in-force book of business, recognised as an asset in embedded value reporting.

exam_qa.q: Explain why you would expect little change to a company's realistic embedded value as a result of entering into a contingent loan financial reinsurance arrangement.
exam_qa.a: The loan received increases current assets and hence embedded value. However, the present value of the future loan repayments (which reduce future profits from the portfolio) broadly offsets this increase. The two effects are approximately equal in value, particularly if the arrangement is priced on a market-consistent basis. The reinsurer's profit margin means the PV of repayments slightly exceeds the loan amount, so EV may fall marginally — but the dominant effect is broadly neutral.

exam_qa.q: How does a contingent loan financial reinsurance arrangement change the risk profile of the insurer's embedded value, and would you expect contingent or non-contingent repayments to be higher?
exam_qa.a:
Risk profile: without the arrangement, the VIF profit included in embedded value is uncertain — it may not materialise. Under the Fin Re arrangement, part of this uncertain future profit is converted into current cash (the loan), which is certain. The insurer's EV therefore becomes less risky, as a portion of uncertain VIF has been crystallised.
Repayment level: contingent repayments will be higher than non-contingent equivalents, because the reinsurer must price for the probability that some repayments will not be made (i.e. the contingency that profits do not emerge). The higher repayments compensate the reinsurer for this default risk.

subsections.title: Factors and Methods for Setting Retention
bullets: The retention level is the amount of risk the cedant keeps per policy (proportional RI) or per claim/aggregate (XL RI). Setting it requires balancing the cost of holding an internal fluctuation reserve against the cost of reinsurance premiums.

Approach 1 — ruin probability method: use a stochastic model to simulate the portfolio's claims experience across many scenarios. Set the retention at the level that keeps the probability of insolvency below a specified threshold (e.g. 1 in 200). For long-term fixed-benefit contracts (IP, CI) the most important variable to model stochastically is claim incidence. For short-term indemnity contracts (PMI), both claim incidence and claim amounts must be modelled stochastically.

Approach 2 — cost minimisation: as retention increases, the cost of purchasing reinsurance falls but the insurer must hold a larger internal fluctuation reserve. Set the retention at the level that minimises the sum: (cost of holding fluctuation reserve) + (cost of reinsurance premiums).

Factors increasing the amount of reinsurance used (lower retention): greater uncertainty about future claims experience; lower acceptable insolvency probability; higher variance in claim amounts; lower free assets.

Large insurers with substantial free assets may dispense with XL cover entirely for standard policies, retaining reinsurance only for exceptionally large individual sums at risk (e.g. >£1m) and against catastrophic events.
The level of free assets directly affects the need for financial reinsurance — the more capital available, the less the insurer needs financing from reinsurers.

key_terms.definition: The probability that an insurer's assets fall below its liabilities over a specified period. Used as a target metric when determining the optimal retention level.
An internal reserve held by the insurer to absorb adverse claims experience above its expected level, as an alternative to purchasing external reinsurance.

exam_qa.q: Describe two approaches to determining the appropriate retention level for an XL reinsurance programme.
exam_qa.a:
Approach 1 — Ruin probability: model the statistical distribution of the portfolio's claims costs stochastically across many simulated scenarios. Set the retention at the level that keeps the probability of insolvency below a specified threshold (e.g. 1 in 200). For long-term contracts with fixed benefits (IP, CI), claim incidence rates are the key stochastic variable. For short-term indemnity contracts (PMI), both incidence and claim severity must be modelled stochastically.
Approach 2 — Cost minimisation: as the retention rises, the cost of purchasing reinsurance falls but the insurer must hold a larger internal fluctuation reserve to absorb volatility. Set the retention at the point that minimises total cost = (cost of holding fluctuation reserve) + (cost of reinsurance premiums).

exam_qa.q: State three factors that would cause an insurer to use more reinsurance (i.e. adopt a lower retention level).
exam_qa.a:
1. Greater uncertainty about future claims experience — for example, writing a new product or entering a new territory with limited pricing data.
2. A lower acceptable probability of insolvency — a more conservative risk tolerance requires a larger external safety net.
3. Higher variance in the distribution of benefit amounts — particularly for indemnity products (e.g. PMI) where both claim frequency and severity are uncertain.
Note: lower free assets would also drive a lower retention — a smaller capital buffer means less internal capacity to absorb adverse claims deviations.

26.summary:
Chapter 26 Summary
Reinsurance involves a direct‐writing company ceding business to another insurance company, the reinsurer. The reinsurer will have no contact with the policyholders.

Reasons for reinsurance
The main reasons for reinsurance are that it might:
• limit exposure to risk
• avoid large single losses
• smooth results
• provide expertise
• increase capacity to accept risk
• provide financial assistance
• provide an arbitrage opportunity.

Types of reinsurance business
Reinsurance can be by treaty or facultative, proportional or non‐proportional (or financial).
Arrangements between cedant and reinsurer can be fac/fac, fac/ob or ob/ob.

Reinsurance commission
Reinsurance commission is paid from the reinsurer to the insurer. The commission may be structured as a deduction from the reinsurance premium. This is known as a rebate.

Proportional reinsurance
For long‐term health business, proportional reinsurance can be:
• quota share or surplus arrangements, written on
• original terms or risk premium bases.

For short‐term health business, only quota share is used.

Under original terms, the reinsurance premiums are in the same proportion to the original premium as the reinsured benefit is to the full benefit level. The reinsurer helps the finances of the insurer by paying it commission. Deposits back may be used so that the cedant maintains the reserves for the whole contract.

For risk premium reinsurance:
• a proportion of either the full benefit or (sometimes) the sum at risk is reinsured
• reinsurance premiums can be net level payments or yearly increasing
• reinsurance premiums may be guaranteed or reviewable
• reinsurance premiums are determined by the reinsurer.

Risks are shared in proportions. For quota share this proportion is the same for all risks, but for surplus, it varies.

Although quota share is inflexible and cedes profit, it is good for:
• spreading risk
• writing larger portfolios
• encouraging reciprocal business
• financing and improving solvency.

Surplus reinsurance enables the insurer to cover individual risks up to an agreed maximum retention level. This limits the insurer’s concentration of risk on individual policies and so reduces claims volatility.

Non‐proportional reinsurance
Non‐proportional reinsurance helps to cap claims costs, and is normally only used in shortterm contracts and possibly group long‐term contracts. It is also used to smooth results by reducing claims fluctuations.

It can be:
• risk excess of loss, which relates to individual losses and may be found on PMI portfolios
• aggregate excess of loss (stop loss) , which covers aggregations of claims over a whole account and is rare for long‐term insurance
• catastrophe excess of loss, which covers events or pandemics.

The limits may be indexed to allow for inflation.

Financial reinsurance
Financial reinsurance can help the cedant to relieve part of its new business financing requirement. It can be structured like a loan, receiving either a lump sum or reinsurance commissions with repayments incorporated into the reinsurance premium or paid out of future profits.

Financial reinsurance typically involves very little transfer of insurance risk.

Financial reinsurance is only effective under supervisory regimes where credit cannot be taken for the insurer’s future profits, and/or where a realistic liability does not have to be held in respect of the loan repayments.

Determining the retention level
The retention level may be determined by estimating the distribution of claim costs under various retentions, and then picking one that gives a suitably low probability of adverse net experience. This could be based on insolvency (or ruin) probabilities using a stochastic model.

The model can be used to find the mix of reinsurance and of holding a risk experience fluctuation reserve that minimises the cost of the claim fluctuation protection.

This page has been left blank so that you can keep the chapter summaries together for revision purposes.

27.title: Other Risk Management Techniques

subsections.title: Three Approaches to Medical Underwriting
bullets: Underwriting is the process of considering an insurance risk — assessing acceptability and, if acceptable, setting appropriate premium, terms and conditions of cover.

FULL MEDICAL UNDERWRITING: Most onerous and detailed; medically underwrites the proposer at the point of application, gathering medical history and other evidence. Most costly and time-consuming but gives the greatest opportunity to learn about individual health status.

Full UW outcome: applicants accepted at standard terms, or offered special terms (loading, exclusion, postponement, decline, alternative product, 100% facultative reinsurance). Arguably clearest for policyholders — they know from outset what is/is not covered.

MEDICAL HISTORY DISREGARDED (MHD): No consideration of past medical history; no exclusions for pre-existing conditions. Least costly in process but creates the greatest potential for anti-selection — product must be priced to reflect this elevated risk.
MHD most commonly used for group PMI business, or when an individual transfers from group to individual PMI cover.
MORATORIUM UNDERWRITING: No formal underwriting at acceptance; past medical history examined only at the time of a claim.

Moratorium mechanics — two defined periods: (1) exclusion period: all conditions pre-existing within a defined period before acceptance (typically 5 years) are excluded from cover; (2) moratorium period: the exclusion is waived if no further treatment is received for the condition during a consecutive period (typically 2–3 years) after policy inception. If treatment is received during the moratorium period, the clock resets — another consecutive clean period must run.

Moratorium excludes not only recurrences of the pre-existing condition but any condition directly related to it — making the scope of exclusion uncertain and hard for non-medically-aware policyholders to understand.

Moratorium most suited to PMI (short-term, renewable annually) and may also be used for group CI. Key marketing advantage: immediate cover can be offered, subject only to a blanket pre-existing condition exclusion.

Cost advantage of moratorium: underwriting cost is incurred only for the proportion of policyholders who actually claim (not for all policies at inception) — significantly lower per-policy cost than full medical underwriting.

Moratorium disadvantage: policyholders cannot know with certainty what is covered; risk of claims being refused at time of medical need, generating policyholder dissatisfaction and reputational damage.
Full UW is preferable where the policyholder wants certainty; moratorium trades certainty for lower cost and easier sales.

key_terms.definition: Underwriting carried out at proposal stage using medical history, possibly a PMAR, medical examination and specialist tests. Proposers are accepted on standard terms, rated, given exclusions, postponed or declined.

Underwriting approach where no account is taken of past medical history and no pre-existing condition exclusions are applied. Used mainly for large group PMI schemes where anti-selection risk is managed by the group context.

No upfront underwriting; instead, a blanket exclusion applies to conditions pre-existing in a defined period (typically 5 years) before acceptance. Exclusion is waived after a consecutive clean period (typically 2–3 years) without treatment for the excluded condition.
A policy exclusion applied (under moratorium or full UW) for conditions from which the applicant has suffered, or received treatment for, prior to the policy start date.

exam_qa.q: Compare full medical underwriting, moratorium and MHD from the insurer's and policyholder's perspectives.
exam_qa.a: COST — Full UW: highest per-policy cost as underwriting done for all at inception. Moratorium: low per-policy cost — UW only for claimants. MHD: minimal UW cost. ANTI-SELECTION — Full UW: best protection; applicants rated or excluded. Moratorium: moderate risk, exclusions on pre-existing conditions. MHD: highest risk — all lives accepted regardless of health. SPEED — Full UW: slowest; may deter applicants wanting immediate cover. Moratorium/MHD: immediate cover offered. POLICYHOLDER CERTAINTY — Full UW: clearest — exclusions known upfront. Moratorium: uncertain — exclusions depend on medical history links, which policyholders may not understand. MHD: most attractive — all conditions covered. PRODUCT SUITABILITY — Full UW: suits individual long-term contracts (IP, CI, LTCI). Moratorium: suits short-term individual PMI. MHD: suits large group PMI.

exam_qa.q: What are the advantages and disadvantages of replacing full medical underwriting with moratorium underwriting for individual PMI?
exam_qa.a: Advantages: (i) Lower per-policy underwriting costs (UW only at claim stage), reducing premiums and improving competitiveness. (ii) No upfront questions — easier to sell, less friction for distributor and applicant. (iii) Immediate cover (subject to PEC exclusion) more attractive to applicants. Disadvantages: (i) Retrospective UW at claim stage is difficult — insurer must reconstruct medical history, information may be unavailable. (ii) Policyholders may not understand scope of PEC exclusions (linked conditions), risking complaints and reputational damage when claims are rejected. (iii) Transition challenge: insurer must manage both old (full UW) and new (moratorium) books simultaneously, adding operational complexity.

exam_qa.q: Why is moratorium underwriting cheaper per policy than full medical underwriting?
exam_qa.a: Full medical underwriting is an expense incurred for every new application at inception. Moratorium underwriting is only performed at the time a claim is made, so the cost is incurred only for the fraction of policyholders who actually claim — a much smaller proportion than the total in-force book.

subsections.title: The Full Medical Underwriting Process
bullets: Medical and other evidence is gathered to assess whether the applicant has the insurer's required standard of health, and if not, how much additional risk premium loading is required.

Four sources of medical evidence: (1) proposal form questions completed by the applicant; (2) Primary Medical Attendant's Report (PMAR) from the applicant's doctors; (3) medical examination carried out by a doctor or nurse appointed by the insurer; (4) specialist medical tests (eg HIV test).

Proposal form questions must be factual — within the proposer's knowledge. Acceptable: 'When were you last an in-patient in hospital?' Unacceptable: 'Was your heart attack serious?' (requires medical judgement).

Non-medical limits: sources 2–4 are expensive, so the decision to use them depends on the sum at risk. For large sums at risk, all four sources may be used; for small sums, the proposal form alone may suffice. Non-medical limits also vary by age — thresholds are typically lower for older applicants.
For IP, maximum sum at risk = weekly benefit × maximum benefit duration. Rules are then applied, eg: sum at risk < £X — proposal form only; £X–£Y — PMAR required; >£Y — full medical examination.
Consent of the proposer (obtained on the proposal form) is required before sources 2–4 can be accessed.
Non-medical risk factors also investigated via proposal form: occupation, leisure pursuits (eg dangerous sports), country of normal residence and overseas travel plans.

Financial underwriting is also used for larger proposals to reduce the risk of over-insurance — limiting benefit to a proportion of pre-claim income prevents the policyholder having a financial incentive not to return to work.
Interpretation of evidence is performed by specialist underwriters using: (a) doctors employed by the insurer; (b) underwriting manuals (prepared internally or by major reinsurers).

Underwriting decisions for substandard risks: (a) premium loading; (b) reduced benefit; (c) postponement (if risk is temporary); (d) decline (if risk too high); (e) offer a different/less risk-intensive product; (f) 100% facultative reinsurance with zero retention; (g) specific exclusion of named conditions or causes.

The underwriter aims to match each policyholder to the risk group assumed in pricing — not charge too much (deterring good risks) or too little (admitting bad risks). Insurer aims for most business to be accepted at standard rates, since loaded/declined cases cause bad feeling, extra admin, and can deter distributors.

Close working relationship between pricing actuary and underwriter is essential: underwriter must understand pricing assumptions, and pricing actuary relies on underwriter to enforce homogeneous risk group boundaries. Reinsurer often assists both parties.

Underwriters contribute meaningfully to product development — especially in defining covered conditions for CI policies and wording exclusions, drawing on front-line claims and underwriting experience.

Ongoing analysis: actuary and underwriter review actual new business mix against expected, analysing departures by risk cell. Significant deviations trigger procedural review or repricing. Reinsurer experience provides valuable credibility for this analysis.

Review of special terms experience: actuary compares claims experience of loaded/excluded business against standard business to assess appropriateness of loadings. In practice, non-standard business is heterogeneous and data quality often insufficient for individual loading validation; portfolio-level review is more feasible.

key_terms.definition: A report from the applicant's treating doctor, obtained by the insurer (with proposer consent) as part of full medical underwriting, providing detailed medical history beyond what is on the proposal form.

Benefit thresholds below which additional underwriting evidence (PMAR, medical exam, specialist tests) is not required. Above the threshold, more intensive evidence is gathered. Limits typically vary by age and product.

Situation where insured benefit exceeds the insured's pre-claim income, creating a financial incentive not to return to work (for IP) or to claim (generally). Financial underwriting caps benefits relative to income to prevent this.

exam_qa.q: Describe the considerations taken into account when underwriting for income protection insurance.
exam_qa.a: Policyholder characteristics: age, gender, smoking status, occupation, industry sector, level of income, socio-economic class, employment status (self-employed vs employed), location, method of distribution. Product design factors: replacement ratio (net and gross of tax), deferred period, disability definition (own vs any occupation), benefit amount and escalation, policy term, maximum benefit duration, options and exclusions. Medical evidence: proposal form (own and family medical history), PMAR, medical examination, specialist tests, supplementary questionnaires. Lifestyle: leisure pursuits (dangerous hobbies), country of residence and overseas travel. Financial information: evidence of income, evidence of employment security. Regulatory constraints: eg restrictions on use of genetic test results.

exam_qa.q: Why is it inappropriate to ask 'Do you have a dangerous job?' on a proposal form, and how should the insurer instead identify higher-risk occupations?
exam_qa.a: Proposal form questions must relate to facts within the proposer's knowledge. 'Dangerous' is a matter of opinion requiring specialist knowledge — not a factual question. Instead, the insurer should identify the specific dimensions of occupational risk that matter (eg height, manual work, hazardous substances, offshore/overseas) and ask factual questions about those: 'Does your job involve working at unprotected heights over 50 feet?'; 'Does it involve manual or physical work?'; 'Does it involve working offshore or travelling abroad?'; 'Does it involve air travel as a non-fare-paying passenger on unscheduled flights?'

subsections.title: How Underwriting Manages Risk
bullets: Medical underwriting protects the insurer from anti-selection — preventing lives whose health is severely impaired from obtaining cover at rates that do not reflect their true risk. Anti-selection increases both claim frequency and average claim cost.

Underwriting enables identification of substandard risks so that special terms can be applied. The aim is for most business to be accepted at standard rates — loaded/declined cases require extra admin, create bad feeling, and can reduce distributor willingness to sell the product.

Adequate risk classification creates homogeneous risk groups — within each group, the variation in expected claims cost should be smaller than the variation between groups. This ensures fairness and pricing consistency.
Underwriting keeps actual morbidity experience close to that assumed in pricing, protecting expected profitability. A close link between pricing assumptions and underwriting guidelines is essential.
Financial underwriting reduces over-insurance risk — benefits are capped relative to pre-claim income to prevent the policyholder having a disincentive to return to work.
The actuary establishes pricing guidelines; the underwriter operationalises them — the two functions must align on the boundary conditions for the standard risk category.

Ban on a risk factor (eg family history or genetic test results): reduces underwriting accuracy; increases anti-selection risk; may require premium increases or broader exclusions; can cause adverse selection spiral if rate rises deter healthy lives. If all insurers face the same constraint, market share impact may be neutral, but the insurer with historically strong underwriting loses a competitive advantage.

key_terms.definition: The tendency for higher-risk individuals to seek more insurance (or insurance on better terms) than lower-risk individuals — leading to adverse experience if not corrected by underwriting.

A category of lives for which expected claims costs are sufficiently similar that a common premium rate can be charged without systematic cross-subsidy. The within-group variation in claims cost should be smaller than the between-group variation.

exam_qa.q: A government bans the use of family medical history in underwriting CI and LTCI policies. Outline the possible implications for a large well-established insurer specialising in these products.
exam_qa.a: Adverse effects: Less information available — underwriting accuracy falls; insurer accepts more substandard risks on standard terms — anti-selection increases. Claim experience worsens, particularly for CI/LTCI where hereditary conditions are most relevant. Premiums may need to increase to compensate — deterring better risks and potentially creating an adverse selection spiral. Increased reserving margins may be required. Risk of lapse-and-re-entry if previously rated policyholders reapply under simplified underwriting and secure standard terms. Reinsurance treaties will need renegotiation. Sales literature and application forms require redesign. The large insurer may lose a competitive advantage if it had historically used family history more effectively than rivals. Favourable effects: Simpler, quicker application process — product easier to sell. Fewer application questions → higher sales volumes. Fewer declined or rated cases → better distributor relationships. Less non-disclosure risk (less to disclose). Lower underwriting costs (less information to process). Market-wide impact — if all insurers face the same constraint, market share may be broadly unaffected.

subsections.title: Purpose and General Controls
bullets: Underwriters manage risk at proposal stage; claims managers manage risk at the claims stage. For health and care products with complex claim conditions, the skill of the claims team has a major influence on the insurer's claims experience.

Core purpose: ensure that claims accepted are consistent with the assumptions made when the product was designed and priced. Claims management team should be involved in product development, with regular dialogue between claims and pricing/design teams.

Close liaison between underwriting and claims is necessary: medical conditions giving rise to a claim (eg LTCI) should be checked against information provided at proposal stage to verify no non-disclosure or material inconsistency.

Every large claim should be investigated — proposal information verified to detect fraud or non-disclosure. For IP, the claimant's medical practitioner may be contacted to confirm the current illness was not an undisclosed pre-existing condition.

Claims managers need specific expertise: initial validity assessment, evidence collection, assessment of appropriate treatment, knowledge of medical procedures and inflationary pressures, and ongoing management of long-duration claims (IP, LTCI, PMI).
