key_terms.definition: The process of assessing, validating and managing claims from inception to settlement, ensuring that benefits paid are consistent with policy conditions, pricing assumptions and absence of fraud or non-disclosure.

subsections.title: IP-Specific Claims Controls
bullets: Replacement ratio management: IP benefit must be set to give the claimant an incentive to return to work. Maximum benefit is typically expressed as a percentage of gross salary (eg 50% or 75% gross salary less State disability benefit).

If a claimant's income falls and they fail to notify the insurer, the maximum benefit formula applied at claim stage may reduce the benefit below what they expected — leading to dissatisfaction at a time of ill health. Policyholders should be encouraged to notify income reductions.

The maximum benefit formula is designed so that the net replacement ratio (post-disability income after tax / pre-disability income after tax) is always below one — maintaining a financial incentive to recover and return to work.

Note: policyholders need not notify the insurer of income increases — if income rises, the existing benefit level is simply a lower proportion of earnings, which disadvantages only the policyholder, not the insurer.

Disability counselling: a specialist representative (employed by the insurer or a third party) visits the new claimant at home at the start of claim. Purpose: (i) assist the policyholder with information on State welfare payments and local support services; (ii) establish the severity of the illness and likely return-to-work date; (iii) promote early return to work as practically possible, minimising claim cost; (iv) provides marketing benefit — the support offered makes the product more attractive.

key_terms.definition: Ratio of post-disability income (net of tax) to pre-disability income (net of tax). A ratio below 1.0 means the claimant is financially better off working — preserving the return-to-work incentive.

A claims management service for IP insurance where an insurer's representative visits a new claimant to provide support, establish claim severity, promote return to work, and advise on State benefit entitlements.

exam_qa.q: Describe the concept of disability counselling for IP insurance and its advantages for both policyholders and insurers.
exam_qa.a: Disability counselling involves an insurer or third-party representative visiting a new claimant at home when benefit payments commence. Policyholder advantages: advice on managing disability; information on State welfare payments and local assistance services available; reminder of benefits payable (amounts, dates, conditions). Insurer advantages: early assessment of claim severity and estimated return-to-work date; opportunity to promote early return to work, reducing claims cost; marketing benefit — the support service makes the product more attractive to potential buyers.

subsections.title: PMI-Specific Claims Controls
bullets: Pre-authorisation: the insurer confirms that proposed treatment is appropriate and medically necessary before it is carried out — the insurer does not rely solely on the claimant's personal doctor as the arbiter of need.

Pre-authorisation process: when the policyholder becomes aware of a need to claim (eg GP referral), they call the insurer to report the condition, discuss proposed treatment and confirm policy coverage before proceeding.

Three advantages of pre-authorisation for the insurer: (1) greater control over claims — can influence or direct choice of treatment provider (eg to a cost-effective hospital in network); (2) claimant is informed upfront of what is/is not covered — reduces disputes and complaints; (3) early warning of impending claims — improves management information and reserving accuracy.
Pre-authorisation has faced consumer resistance in some markets — perceived as infringing policyholders' rights to receive treatment where and when they choose.

key_terms.definition: A PMI claims control whereby the insurer validates proposed treatment against policy conditions and medical need before treatment begins, and may direct the claimant to a preferred treatment provider.

exam_qa.q: Define pre-authorisation as applied to PMI and state its main advantages to the insurer.
exam_qa.a: Pre-authorisation is the procedure whereby, before treatment commences, the claim is authenticated against policy conditions, medical need is established, and the claimant is directed to an appropriate treatment provider. Advantages to the insurer: (i) greater control over the claims process, including ability to direct to cost-effective providers; (ii) ensures claimant is clear about what is/is not covered, reducing disputes; (iii) provides early warning of impending claims, improving management information and reserving accuracy.

sections.title: Checks on Policy and Claims Data

subsections.title: Data Quality Controls
bullets: Policy data accuracy is a major risk for any insurer — both for ongoing administration and as the source data for pricing future business and reserving in-force business. Inaccurate data leads to incorrect advice with potentially serious financial and reputational consequences.
Aligning proposal form format with administration system input screens reduces transcription errors — a straightforward but effective data quality control.

REGULAR VETTING AND SPOT CHECKS: Periodic systematic comparison of paper/electronic records against stored facts; automated system modules should query inconsistencies or unusual data items as they arise. Records should be checked 'end to end' through the full data pipeline — essential if data passes through multiple systems or manual processes.

CONTROLS ON DATA ACCEPTANCE: Software should have built-in validation rules preventing invalid inputs (eg gender field not M/F, age at entry over 120, sum insured in pence). Unusual distributions (eg clustering of birth months) should be flagged. Overrides of validation errors should require authorisation by a specified level of seniority, with a full audit trail recorded.

COMPULSORY FIELDS: All rating factors (age, gender, benefit) must be mandatory fields — the system must not accept an incomplete policy or claim record. Claim records must cross-reference a valid policy number.

STAFF TRAINING: Staff responsible for data input must understand: (a) the value of accuracy; (b) how to identify data that may have been submitted wrongly; (c) how the data they input is used and the financial consequences of errors. An open and trusting relationship between input staff and data users helps embed a data quality culture.
Other checks: reconciliation (data last period + new business – terminations = data this period); consistency checks (premiums consistent with sum insured, benefit consistent with product rules).

key_terms.definition: Data check verifying that: data at prior investigation + new business inflows – outflows = current data. Identifies missing or duplicate records.

subsections.title: Design Features that Control Risk
bullets: Product design is a primary risk management tool — design decisions directly determine the type and magnitude of risk the insurer accepts.
Form of benefits: cash vs indemnity (eg LTCI) — indemnity benefits limit moral hazard; cash benefits are simpler but create over-insurance risk.

Claim definitions: eg own occupation vs any occupation for IP — own occupation is more generous and creates greater morbidity risk; any occupation reduces the insurer's exposure but may trigger consumer protection concerns.
Inclusion of guarantees and options: each guarantee or option (eg guaranteed insurability option for CI) adds risk and cost; must be priced and reserved for appropriately.
Policy terms and conditions: must be clearly worded, internally consistent, and aligned with pricing assumptions — ambiguous wording creates claims disputes and reputational risk.

Appropriateness for target market: product design must match the intended distribution channel and customer segment to manage new business volume, mis-selling and lapse risks. Over-engineered products sold through low-touch channels increase mis-selling risk.

exam_qa.q: List the main features of product design that help control risks to a health and care insurer.
exam_qa.a: Form of benefits (eg cash vs indemnity for LTCI); claim definitions (eg own vs any occupation for IP); inclusion of guarantees and options (eg GIOs for CI); appropriateness of design for the target market (eg new business volume and mis-selling risk for PMI); terms and conditions, in particular exclusions and their wording.

sections.title: Managing the Distribution Process and Customer Relationship

subsections.title: Distribution Controls
bullets: The salesperson is often the first point of contact between insurer and customer — their behaviour directly shapes the customer relationship and risk profile of the book.

Aggressive over-selling boosts short-term commission but creates lapse risk when the customer later reviews finances and identifies surplus or unsuitable coverage. Early lapse may spread to other policies held with the insurer, and negative word-of-mouth compounds the damage.

Six key controls on the distribution process: (1) monitor the sales message; (2) beware business churning; (3) analyse quality of sales staff; (4) beware overgenerous commission; (5) monitor premium receipts; (6) invest in sales training.
Monitor the sales message: sales promises must be consistent with policy conditions; literature must be accurate, clear and not over-sell.

Beware business churning: salespeople must not encourage lapses/replacements merely to generate fresh commission. Commission clawback (recovery of commission on early lapse) is the key control. Commission structure must balance initial and renewal components appropriately.
Analyse quality of sales staff: review each agent's persistency and complaint/compliment record. Agent code must be captured on every sale to enable analysis.

Beware overgenerous commission: commission should be commensurate with the effort required to make the sale and consistent with the commission loading in the premium. Overgenerous commission creates product bias and over-selling incentives.

Monitor premium receipts: premiums must be received from brokers/agents promptly — policies should not be issued without evidence of premium receipt. Delayed premiums deprive the insurer of investment income.

Invest in sales training: salespeople must understand customers' health and care needs, ability to pay, and product features and underwriting procedures — many of these requirements are imposed by regulation.

key_terms.definition: The recovery by the insurer of part of the commission already paid to a salesperson or intermediary if the policy lapses within a defined early period. It discourages mis-selling and churning.

The practice of encouraging a policyholder to lapse an existing policy and take out a new one (with the same or a different insurer) in order for the salesperson to earn a fresh round of initial commission.

subsections.title: Treating Customers Fairly (TCF) and Customer Satisfaction
bullets: Many regulators impose TCF requirements covering the entire customer journey: product design, sales process, ongoing communications, claims handling and complaints.
Factors shaping customer expectations: sales literature and illustrations at point of sale; competitor rates and benefits; premiums paid; moral obligations of the insurer; benefits paid in the past.

TCF aspects the insurer must address: how the policy is sold (eg cooling-off periods); benefits payable and claim eligibility; period of cover and guarantees; reviewability conditions; counselling and post-claim support; premiums and future changes; customer service; documentation and regular communication; complaints and arbitration; ability to amend the policy; data security.

Failure to treat customers fairly risks regulatory action (fines, business restrictions) and increased lapses. Mitigation: product conditions must be made explicit and never overstated; sales processes monitored to align customer expectations with policy reality.

Customer satisfaction surveys: serve to identify gaps between policyholder understanding and policy reality; results must feed back into product literature, sales processes and training. Industry bodies (eg ABI in the UK) have developed Statements of Best Practice for selling IP, CI, LTCI and PMI based on such research.

Meeting needs: the best customer retention tool is a product that genuinely meets the policyholder's identified needs at an affordable price. Artificially created needs through aggressive selling increase lapse rates. Early lapse may cause loss if initial expenses not yet recouped.

key_terms.definition: Regulatory principle requiring that insurers ensure customers receive products and services appropriate to their needs, are kept informed, and are able to make informed decisions throughout the product lifecycle.

exam_qa.q: List the aspects about which a health insurer should ensure its customers are being treated fairly.
exam_qa.a: How the policy is sold (eg cooling-off periods); benefits payable; period of cover and guarantees; reviewability of premiums and/or benefits; counselling and post-claim support; premiums to be paid and future changes; customer service levels (eg 24-hour access); claims eligibility and process, including speed of payment; documentation and regular communication; complaints and arbitration processes; ability to make amendments (eg changes in risk); security of financial transactions and data protection.

subsections.title: Counterparty Risk Management and SLAs
bullets: Counterparties include reinsurers, medical service providers, and providers of outsourced services (underwriting, claims management, administration, actuarial, investment, marketing, IT, training).

Five techniques for managing counterparty risk: (1) due diligence on the counterparty before selection; (2) minimum credit rating requirements — a quicker screen than full due diligence; (3) diversification across multiple counterparties — reduces concentration risk from any single default or poor performance; (4) single counterparty exposure limits; (5) credit insurance or credit derivatives to transfer default risk to a third party.
Diversification across counterparties may be limited by market structure (eg few reinsurers in a niche product line) or by the insurer's size.
Key rationale for outsourcing: third party specialists provide expertise and efficiency at lower cost than in-house, freeing the insurer's management to focus on core strategic functions.

Service Level Agreements (SLAs): legal contracts specifying precisely what each party will do, to what standard and within what timescales. SLA problems often arise from imprecise or ambiguous wording — equivalent in detail to a reinsurance treaty.

SLA key provisions: parties and main contacts; scope and frequency of services; fees (unit costs or periodic payments); monitoring regime; remedies for breach of service standards (eg penalties); cancellation/get-out clauses.

Even after outsourcing, the insurer retains responsibility for the outsourced function — regular reports, checks and inspections are required to guard against reputational, business and security risks. For example, a third-party underwriter should report on: time taken per case, proportion accepted/declined/rated, and customer satisfaction levels.

key_terms.definition: A legal contract between an insurer and an outsource provider specifying the scope, standards, timescales and fees for services provided, and the remedies available if standards are not met.
A structured investigation of a counterparty prior to entering into a contract, enabling an informed assessment of whether the counterparty meets the required quality and financial standards.

exam_qa.q: What areas should be covered in a contract between an insurer and a third-party service provider?
exam_qa.a: Dates for which the agreement is in force; identity of parties, main contacts, and where ultimate responsibility lies; scope of service — what the third party will provide, at what level and frequency; payments to the third party — amounts, frequency, methods and conditions; obligations on the insurer's part; monitoring regime — how often and in what way service quality will be assessed; consequences of service failure (eg financial penalties); cancellation and exit clauses.

subsections.title: Enterprise Risk Management (ERM)
bullets: ERM is a holistic, integrated risk management framework that considers the insurer's risks as a whole — allowing for risk concentrations and diversification effects — rather than managing individual risks in isolation.
The insurer must assess its overall risk profile by aggregating underlying risks, explicitly allowing for correlations: positive correlations increase net risk; negative correlations reduce it.

Example of negatively correlated risks (beneficial diversification): medical advances simultaneously reduce IP claim inception rates and increase longevity. Reduced inception rates help the IP insurer (fewer claims), while increased longevity hurts (longer claim durations) — the combination is less adverse than longevity worsening in isolation.
ERM process (from CP1): identify → classify → measure → control → finance → monitor risks.

Role of the actuary: regular reporting to directors on nature and size of risks faced; particular attention to material risks and those most sensitive to change. Sensitive risks should be modelled across a range of long-term scenarios to assess management strategies.
Once risk management strategies are agreed, they should be documented, implemented, and monitored on an ongoing basis against objective rules and procedures.
Strong governance and controls are vital to the ERM framework.
Value creation through ERM: a strong risk management framework allows companies to identify and assess strategic opportunities — educated risk-taking that creates shareholder value.

key_terms.definition: A holistic, integrated approach to managing all risks facing an organisation — allowing for correlations and diversification effects — with the goal of maintaining a risk profile consistent with available capital and strategic objectives.

exam_qa.q: Give an example of correlated risks that might reduce the overall risk to an IP insurer.
exam_qa.a: Medical advances improve healthcare outcomes, simultaneously reducing IP claim inception rates (fewer people become ill) and increasing longevity (people live longer). These two effects are negatively correlated from the insurer's perspective: reduced incidence is good (fewer claims); increased longevity is bad (claims last longer). The net effect is less adverse than longevity deterioration in isolation, because the two effects partially offset each other.

subsections.title: Experience Monitoring, Big Data and Staff Quality
bullets: Experience monitoring is a core part of the actuarial control cycle — the actuary periodically reviews claims, expenses, distribution and surplus to identify appropriate risk management actions.
Actions triggered by experience analysis: expense controls; policy retention activity; review of new business strategy; asset-liability management; capital management.

Big data: large or complex data sets where traditional processing applications are inadequate. Health and care insurers can leverage big data to: (i) improve risk classification for individual risks; (ii) improve experience monitoring (spot issues and trends earlier); (iii) aid prediction (eg generalised linear models).

Traditional rating factors: age, gender, occupation, location — broad categories assumed to be internally homogeneous. Big data enables finer-grained rating on factors directly linked to morbidity (diet, exercise) and early identification of changing risks in individual insureds.
Big data sources: bancassurers (spending patterns, gym memberships vs high-risk purchases); supermarkets (loyalty card data on food purchasing habits).
Multi-factor predictive modelling (eg GLMs) combines internal customer data with external drivers, allowing for correlations and interactions — increasingly adopted.

Risks of big data: reputational damage if privacy or data protection concerns arise; customer backlash reducing sales and increasing lapses; regulatory change governing data use (including fines for misuse); data may be inaccurate, incomplete or irrelevant (eg fruit purchased but not eaten); model risk (wrong model chosen); cost of data collection may exceed benefit.

Staff quality: successful business performance depends heavily on staff competence and judgement. Health and care insurance has more scope for fraud and judgement calls than life insurance — claims can be exaggerated or falsified more readily than a death claim.

Key areas of claims management competence: initial claim validity assessment; evidence collection; assessment of appropriate treatment; knowledge of medical procedures; knowledge of inflationary pressures on health claims; ongoing management of long-duration claims.

Monitoring staff competence: occasional audits; 'time and motion' analysis by independent experts to identify inefficiencies and training needs; monitoring for both incompetent and fraudulent behaviour.

key_terms.definition: Data sets so large or complex that traditional processing applications are inadequate. Used in health insurance for improved risk classification, predictive modelling, and experience monitoring at the individual insured level.

A multi-factor statistical modelling technique used to predict outcomes (eg claim inception) from multiple explanatory variables, including interactions and correlations between them. Increasingly used in health insurance pricing and experience monitoring.

exam_qa.q: How might big data be used by a health and care insurer, and what are the risks of doing so?
exam_qa.a: Uses: (i) Improved risk classification — rate individuals on factors directly linked to morbidity (eg diet, exercise) rather than broad demographic proxies; enables selection of preferred risks. (ii) Improved experience monitoring — identify adverse trends earlier than with conventional analysis; enables proactive intervention (eg healthy eating advice to customers showing declining fruit/vegetable purchases). (iii) Predictive modelling — GLMs combining internal and external data to forecast future claims. Risks: (i) Reputational damage if customers perceive privacy intrusion or data protection failure. (ii) Customer resistance — policyholders may object to monitoring, leading to lapses and reduced sales. (iii) Regulatory risk — use of big data may be restricted or penalised by future regulation. (iv) Data quality risk — data may be inaccurate or irrelevant (eg supermarket purchasing data reflects purchases, not consumption). (v) Model risk — wrong predictive model chosen. (vi) Cost-benefit risk — expense of collecting and analysing data may exceed the value of additional information.

27.summary:
Chapter 27 Summary
Underwriting
Underwriting can be used to mitigate risk. Medical underwriting might be full medical underwriting, moratorium underwriting (which is common under PMI) or medical history disregarded (which is most likely to be used for group business).

With full medical underwriting:
• evidence (medical, lifestyle or financial) is collected on each risk in order to classify the risks into homogeneous groups so that they can be priced reliably and in line with pricing assumptions
• higher risks can be dealt with, eg premium loading, exclusion clause etc
• new business acceptances can be analysed against expected
• there must be a close link between underwriters and product developers.

With moratorium underwriting:
• no formal underwriting is carried out at the point of acceptance
• past medical history is examined at the time of claim
• the applicant can claim for any condition other than those that were pre‐existing in a defined period before acceptance (often five years)
• this exclusion is waived after a period of time – the moratorium period (usually two or three years) – if the policyholder receives no further treatment for the condition
• if the policyholder does receive treatment for pre‐existing conditions, then the moratorium period is restarted.

Claims management
Claims management control helps to:
• ensure claims are paid in line with policy conditions
• check a claim’s validity with the proposal.

Product‐specific claims controls:
• for IP insurance, include insisting on notice of income reductions, and using disability counselling services
• for PMI, include using pre‐authorisation procedures.

Data checks
Policy and claims data should be checked:
• to ensure the proposal form and administration systems have the same format
• by using regular vetting and spot checks
• using controls on data acceptance
• by use of compulsory fields.

The insurer should ensure staff are properly trained.

Product design
Risks can be controlled by considering the design of the product, in particular, the form of benefits, the claim definition, the inclusion of guarantees and options and the terms and conditions included in the policy wording.

The appropriateness of the product design for the chosen target market is also important.

Managing the distribution process and customer relationship
The distribution process should be controlled by:
• monitoring the sales message
• being aware of business churning
• analysing the quality of sales staff
• being aware of overgenerous commission
• monitoring premium receipts
• investing in sales training.

Survey levels of customer service satisfaction:
• make sure customers know what has been bought
• make sure it meets their needs
• make sure they receive information as expected
• make sure claims are paid correctly and promptly
• keep customers happy, so that they don’t leave.

Treating customers fairly (TCF) should help to:
• make all conditions clear, explicit and in line with customers’ expectations
• monitor sales processes, ensuring sales staff do not oversell products.

Managing other counterparties
Third parties may be used to carry out various functions, and the insurer may be able to benefit from their expertise. Service level agreements with outsourcers may be used to ensure the quality of work carried out by outsourcers and to ensure that the agreement genuinely does act to mitigate risks.

Other internal processes
Enterprise risk management is a risk management that considers the risks of the business as a whole, and so allows for both concentrations of risk and diversification.

The actuary should be involved in assessing the nature and size of the risks faced, analysing and costing them, and documenting and reporting them.

Experience monitoring is covered in detail in a later chapter. Further, insurers may be able to analyse ‘big data’ in order to improve risk classification, predictive modelling and experience monitoring and control.

It is important to ensure staff are competent. This may be done by:
• monitoring in‐house operational risk
• auditing outsourcers.

This page has been left blank so that you can keep the chapter summaries together for revision purposes.

28.title: Investment

subsections.title: Importance of Investment in Health and Care
bullets: Most health and care products are protection products with low reserves — for these, investment returns are relatively unimportant to financial outcomes.
Exception: pre-funded LTCI carries high reserves and long durations — investment returns are materially important for this product.

Where reserves are significant: high investment returns benefit shareholders (higher profits) and/or policyholders (lower premiums). Unit-linked policyholders benefit directly from good unit fund performance.

The insurer faces a fundamental tension: it wants high returns (which require accepting volatility) but also security to meet guaranteed obligations. Investment strategy must balance return and security given the nature of liabilities.
Choice of investment strategy also affects solvency security and, for with-profits and unit-linked business, whether policyholder expectations are met.

exam_qa.q: Explain the importance of policyholders' expectations as far as investment returns are concerned, for health and care insurance.
exam_qa.a: For most health insurance products, investment returns play a minor role in benefits, so policyholders do not normally expect investment gains. Exceptions include unit-linked or with-profits products such as pre-funded LTCI. However, expectations may not be met if the insurer relies on investment returns to such a degree that underperformance triggers premium increases on reviewable products — policyholders of reviewable but fixed-benefit products (eg reviewable CI) would regard investment underperformance as 'not their problem'. TCF principles require the insurer to be careful not to price products in a way that creates this risk.

subsections.title: Characteristics of Major Asset Classes
bullets: CONVENTIONAL GOVERNMENT BONDS: Most secure asset class (after cash); offer a guaranteed nominal return if held to redemption; highly marketable with low dealing costs; market value fluctuates but redemption value does not.

INDEX-LINKED GOVERNMENT BONDS: Coupon and redemption linked to a price or earnings index; less plentiful and slightly less marketable than conventional bonds; ideal for liabilities linked to the same index.

CORPORATE BONDS: Higher expected return than equivalent government bonds, reflecting default risk and lower marketability; security dependent on issuer credit rating; poor marketability with high dealing costs.

EQUITIES: Expected dividend income and capital value both grow in real terms; income yields low relative to bonds; highly volatile in the short term but tend to track inflation over the long term — a reasonable (imperfect) match for inflation-linked liabilities; highly marketable in developed markets.

PROPERTY (DIRECT): High expected return with low income yield that grows in real terms over time; very illiquid, high dealing/management/maintenance costs; large minimum investment size (poor diversification for small investors); each property unique, making valuation difficult; very long-term investment.
CASH: Most secure with least value variability; highly liquid with negligible dealing costs; lowest expected return of all asset classes.

key_terms.definition: The additional yield on an illiquid asset (eg corporate bond, property) over a comparable liquid asset (eg government bond), compensating the investor for being unable to sell quickly at fair value. May be incorporated into the liability discount rate if illiquid assets are held to back predictable long-duration liabilities.

subsections.title: Three Core Investment Principles
bullets: Principle 1 — Nature, term and currency: select assets appropriate to the nature, term and currency of the liabilities. This is the risk-reduction principle.
Principle 2 — Maximise overall return: select assets to maximise total return (income plus capital gains). This is the return-maximisation principle.
Principle 3 — Free assets determine scope to depart from Principle 1: the extent to which Principle 2 can override Principle 1 depends on the size of the company's free assets and its risk appetite.

Combined statement: 'The company should invest to maximise the overall return on the assets, subject to the risk taken on being within the financial resources available to it.' The greater the free assets, the greater the scope for deliberate mismatch in pursuit of higher returns.
Perfect matching eliminates all investment profit — it is only desirable when free assets are so small that any mismatch would create an unacceptably high probability of insolvency.

key_terms.definition: The excess of the insurer's assets over its supervisory liabilities plus required solvency capital. Free assets act as the cushion enabling the insurer to take investment risk (deliberate mismatch) in pursuit of higher returns.

A technique for constructing a bond portfolio that protects the investor against small changes in interest rates, as a second-best alternative to exact matching. Subject to theoretical and practical limitations; the ALM modelling approach is generally superior.

subsections.title: Four Liability Types and Matching Assets
bullets: Liability outgo = benefit payments + expense outgo − premium income. Each component can be classified by nature, enabling identification of the matching asset.

TYPE 1 — GUARANTEED IN MONETARY TERMS: Amount payable is fixed in the contract in money terms (eg conventional CI lump sum). Match: fixed-interest (government) bonds of appropriate term. Problems: (i) assets of sufficient term often unavailable — a best match is the practical aim; (ii) any excess asset proceeds must be reinvested at unknown rates (zero-coupon bonds solve this but are rarely available); (iii) when recent business is growing, net liability outgo may be negative (premiums exceed claims) — no asset can match a cash inflow.

TYPE 2 — GUARANTEED IN TERMS OF AN INDEX: Amount linked to a price/earnings or similar index (eg IP benefit linked to RPI; expense outgo). Match: index-linked securities based on the same index. Alternative if unavailable: real assets (equities, property) that are expected to grow in real terms, though equities are imperfect due to return volatility.

TYPE 3 — INDEMNITY: Amount depends on actual treatment costs (eg PMI). Match: cash and short-term fixed-interest bonds for most PMI liabilities (short-tail, low reserves). For longer-tail indemnity reserves (eg known future treatment): real assets providing a real return over the settlement period, since medical inflation typically exceeds price inflation. Index-linked bonds go some way but may be outpaced by medical cost escalation.

TYPE 4 — INVESTMENT-LINKED: Amount determined by value of a specified investment fund (eg unit-linked LTCI). Match: invest in the same assets underlying the investment link. Deliberate mismatch is theoretically possible but risky — if chosen assets underperform the linked assets, the insurer bears the loss; this is why mismatching is rare in practice.

Expense outgo: moves broadly with an earnings or price index — classify with Type 2 liabilities. Premium income: if fixed, classify with Type 1 as negative benefit (i.e. short a fixed-interest bond); if index-linked, classify with Type 2.
Free assets: back the capital of the company, which must provide a return to capital providers — appropriate to invest in high-yielding (riskier) assets.

exam_qa.q: Discuss the suitability of a portfolio of medium- and long-dated fixed-interest bonds to match IP insurance liabilities, for an insurer with substantial free assets.
exam_qa.a: IP liabilities are partly fixed in monetary terms (benefit amounts may be set at inception) — fixed-interest bonds provide a nominal match. However: (i) IP benefits often escalate with earnings or inflation — to the extent they are index-linked, fixed-interest bonds mismatch the liability nature. (ii) IP liabilities can be very long-duration (benefit paid until retirement) — long-dated bonds provide a reasonable term match, though bonds of sufficient duration may be unavailable. (iii) With substantial free assets, the insurer can accept more mismatch and pursue higher returns (eg through equities to match escalating benefits). (iv) The insurer may also prefer some liquidity if claim volumes are volatile. Overall: a reasonable but imperfect match; the substantial free assets give flexibility to deviate without undue insolvency risk.

exam_qa.q: Describe the considerations when setting an investment strategy for a company writing pre-funded LTCI and group IP.
exam_qa.a: PRE-FUNDED LTCI: Benefits may be in force for many years — long-term assets appropriate, though exact term depends on portfolio age mix and expected claim incidence. Match by nature of benefit: fixed money benefits → long fixed-interest bonds; index-linked → index-linked bonds; indemnity care costs → real assets (equities) to keep pace with care cost inflation; unit-linked → invest in linked assets directly. Premium income is negative benefit and should be subtracted. MATCHING — GROUP IP: Short-term (1–2 year) contracts — cash and short fixed-interest bonds match most claims. For longer-duration claims (ill until retirement), longer bonds appropriate; index-linked if benefits escalate. OTHER CONSIDERATIONS FOR BOTH: Currency — match domestic currency. Expense liability — linked to earnings index; match with earnings-indexed bonds or real assets. Free assets — currently high, so significant departure from matched position is supportable in pursuit of higher returns, but expansion plans may limit how much can be deployed. Regulatory constraints (asset restrictions, mismatching reserve requirements, concentration limits). Liquidity — for group IP, deferred period gives warning of claims; less of an issue for expanding business. ALM modelling to determine optimal risk/return trade-off. Shareholder risk appetite. Tax efficiency between asset classes.

sections.title: Regulatory Framework and Developing Investment Strategy

subsections.title: Regulatory Constraints on Investment
bullets: Regulators may impose eight types of investment constraint: (1) restrictions on permissible asset types (eg equities or property may be forbidden or capped); (2) restrictions on asset amounts admissible for solvency demonstration (eg equities count for only 25% of face value); (3) concentration limits on single counterparty or country exposure; (4) mandatory minimum holding in specified asset class (eg government bonds); (5) currency matching requirement (liabilities in currency X must be backed by assets in currency X); (6) mismatching reserve requirements (more mismatch → larger reserve → lower free assets); (7) limits on permissible degree of mismatch; (8) restrictions on asset valuation method; (9) custodianship requirements.

Mismatching reserve: if the insurer invests in riskier, higher-return assets, the regulator may require a mismatching reserve — this increases liabilities and reduces free assets, constraining the benefit of the strategy.

Asset valuation method affects investment incentives: book-value accounting disincentivises equities (unrealised gains invisible until sale); market-value accounting makes all gains/losses visible immediately.

Investment yield assumption used in liability valuation may depend on backing assets (eg higher interest rate permitted if backing is government bonds vs equities) — creating a regulatory link between asset choice and liability value.

Solvency capital investment: two approaches — (i) invest in the same way as liabilities it reinforces; (ii) invest differently (eg government bonds for investment-linked solvency capital), treating solvency capital as categorically different from the related reserves.

key_terms.definition: An additional regulatory reserve required when the insurer's assets do not match the nature, term or currency of its liabilities. The more the mismatch (ie the greater the tilt to higher-return riskier assets), the larger the reserve required.

exam_qa.q: Describe how an insurer's investment strategy may be affected by the regulatory framework.
exam_qa.a: Restrictions on permissible asset types — eg overseas investment or equities may be prohibited or capped in total. Restrictions on admissibility — an asset may only count at a fraction of its value in demonstrating solvency (eg equities at 25%), limiting the effective investment in that class. Concentration limits on any single counterparty or country, preventing over-concentration. Mandatory holdings — requirement to hold a minimum proportion in a particular class (eg government bonds). Currency matching — requirement to match assets and liabilities by currency. Mismatching reserve — holding riskier assets triggers an additional liability reserve, reducing free assets and limiting the financial benefit of the strategy. Limits on the total permissible degree of mismatch. Restrictions on asset valuation methods (eg book vs market) — book value disincentivises equities. Requirements on custodianship of assets.

subsections.title: ALM Modelling, Liquidity and Fund Manager Assessment
bullets: ALM MODEL OFFICE APPROACH — six steps: (1) allocate a proportion of free assets to support the investment strategy (not all — shareholders want to retain some free assets for new business development); (2) project assets and liabilities forward using ALM assumptions; (3) check that asset/liability surplus exceeds required solvency capital for most (eg 99%) of simulated paths throughout the projection; (4) calculate an aggregate profitability measure; (5) repeat steps 2–4 for alternative (more/less risky) investment strategies; (6) select the strategy with equal ruin probability that produces the highest profitability.

Stochastic investment models generate distributions of future investment income and capital values — thousands of sample paths simulated, each representing a plausible future investment scenario. The probability of insolvency is estimated from the proportion of paths crossing zero surplus.

Modelling requirements: consistency between inflation, interest rates and capital values on the asset side; dynamic liability valuation basis that responds to simulated investment conditions (to avoid misleading results from a static basis); explicit profitability measure to compare strategies.

Weaknesses of the ALM approach: model risk (conclusions only as good as the parameterisation); partial free asset coverage (excluding some assets may be too restrictive); solvency capital may itself vary with investment strategy, complicating comparison.

FUND MANAGER ASSESSMENT: Investment returns should be regularly assessed against: (a) pre-set return targets; (b) the riskiness of the strategy; (c) peer performance (other managers with similar investment briefs). Assessment over multiple periods reduces noise from luck. External managers require closer monitoring due to reduced control and data access.

LIQUIDITY: Products with volatile claims (eg run-off business, small portfolios) require higher liquidity — ready access to cash or near-cash assets. Reinsurance can reduce the peak liquidity requirement, but the insurer must still advance gross claims before recovering from reinsurers. Liquidity needs can conflict with matching (which may favour long-duration, illiquid assets).

INVESTMENT'S EFFECT ON PRICING: Higher investment returns reduce pricing costs for long-term products (greater investment income offsets liability outgo) — a company with large free assets can invest more widely, earn higher returns, and price more competitively.

ASSET VALUATION METHODS: For short-term assets (PMI, cash) — market value only practical basis. For long-term assets: (1) discounted cashflow — discount future income and proceeds at a rate consistent with liability valuation; (2) market value — most common in practice, reflects what the asset could be sold for today.

key_terms.definition: A projection model of an insurer's in-force business that simulates future asset and liability values under different investment strategies and random economic scenarios, enabling assessment of ruin probability and profitability for each strategy.

exam_qa.q: Describe how stochastic cashflow modelling might be used to determine strategic investment guidelines for health insurance investment managers.
exam_qa.a: Specify a solvency measure (assets vs liabilities) and an acceptable probability of ruin. Specify a profitability measure (eg aggregate earned profit over projection horizon). Model future asset behaviour stochastically (investment return assumptions, including stochastic returns by asset class). Model future liability behaviour dynamically — linked to simulated investment conditions so the valuation basis responds to modelled asset returns; may include dynamic strategic responses (eg switching strategy if solvency threatened). Include consistent expense inflation. Decide on new business allowance and projection period/frequency. Run multiple strategies (varying asset mix) across many stochastically generated scenarios. For each strategy, measure: probability of ruin and aggregate profitability. Select the strategy that, for the target ruin probability, maximises expected profitability — this becomes the investment guideline.

28.summary:
Chapter 28 Summary
The investment strategy of a health and care insurer is of importance to its commercial success, financial performance and security. The scale of importance depends on the product and in particular on the size of the reserves.

The insurance company should invest so as to maximise the overall return on the assets, subject to an acceptable level of risk.

Assets should be selected to match the nature, term and currency of the liabilities.

The nature of the liability outgo can be considered as one of the following:
• guaranteed in monetary terms
• guaranteed in terms of some (non‐investment) index
• indemnity
• investment‐linked.

Assets would normally be selected to match these four components as follows:

Liabilities guaranteed in monetary terms
Immunisation theory can be used to build up a suitable portfolio of government bonds, but there are a number of problems and limitations with this approach.

Liabilities guaranteed in terms of some non‐investment index
The ideal is to invest in an asset based on the same index. An alternative is to invest in real assets.

Indemnity liabilities
Short‐term assets are appropriate for most of these liabilities. There may be some longerterm liabilities that could be matched by real long‐term assets.

Investment‐linked liabilities
It is normal to invest in the assets underlying the investment link concerned.

Other considerations
The existence of free assets means that an insurer can depart from a matched position in order to improve overall investment returns.

The regulator can also affect the assets held by applying:
• restrictions on the types of assets in which an insurer can invest
• restrictions on the amount of any particular type of asset that can be considered for demonstrating solvency
• restrictions on the maximum exposure to a single counterparty / country
• a requirement to hold a certain proportion of total assets in a particular class – for example government stock
• a requirement to match assets and liabilities by currency
• a requirement to hold a mismatching reserve
• a limit on the extent to which mismatching is allowed at all
• restrictions on the valuation method used
• custodianship of assets.

Overall strategy
The overall principle is to invest away from a matched position, to the maximum extent possible, consistent with the desired level of risk, in order to maximise returns.

The insurance company will perform asset / liability modelling, to test the proposed investment strategies, which could be used alternatively to investigate:
• the level of riskiness of investment strategy that can be supported,
• the level of free assets required to support any strategy, or
• the resulting probability of insolvency noting the interdependence between these three aspects.

The insurance company should keep a close eye on the performance of its fund managers.

It may need to keep a supply of ready funds, to allow for the volatility of its day‐to‐day cashflows, particularly if modelling reveals that outcomes are very sensitive to experience.

There are various approaches to valuing assets. Two possibilities are a market value approach and a discounted cashflow approach. The purpose of the valuation will have an influence on the method used.

Health and care policyholders usually do not expect poor investment returns to influence the premium reviews under reviewable contracts, unless maybe if the policies are unit‐linked.

29.title: Monitoring and Feedback into the Control Cycle

subsections.title: Why Monitor Experience
bullets: Experience monitoring closes the actuarial control cycle loop: actual experience is compared against the assumptions made at pricing/reserving stage, and assumptions are revised or actions taken when deviations are identified.

Five reasons to monitor: (1) update assumptions for future experience; (2) identify adverse trends; (3) monitor actual vs expected and take corrective action; (4) provide management information to support business decisions; (5) make more informed pricing and reserving decisions.

Monitoring also serves underwriting and reinsurance functions: for underwriting — check effectiveness of UW procedures and identify anti-selection; for reinsurance — assess ongoing need and calculate profit share entitlement under treaties.
Monitoring is proactive, not just reactive — it can identify profitable products, channels, agents, markets, and investment strategies, enabling the insurer to double down on what works.
Corrective actions triggered by adverse experience: re-pricing/redesign; changing investment/sales/reinsurance/underwriting/profit distribution strategy; reorganising workforce and IT systems.
Assumptions used in: product pricing, valuations, embedded value calculations, profitability monitoring, financial projections, ALM, reinsurance requirements, discontinuance terms.

Experience is monitored through two complementary lenses: (1) actual vs expected (A/E) investigation — direct comparison of observed rates against assumed rates; (2) analysis of surplus — financial impact of A/E differences expressed in money terms.

key_terms.definition: The iterative process of: setting assumptions → pricing/reserving → monitoring actual experience → comparing A vs E → revising assumptions and taking corrective action — and repeating. Experience monitoring is the feedback mechanism that drives the cycle.

exam_qa.q: State the reasons why an insurer would want to monitor its experience.
exam_qa.a: To update assumptions for future experience. To identify adverse trends before they cause significant financial damage. To compare actual vs expected experience and take corrective action (re-pricing, redesign, underwriting changes, claims management changes, etc). To provide management information to aid business decisions. To make better-informed decisions about pricing and reserve adequacy. Additional reasons: to check underwriting effectiveness and identify anti-selection; to assess need for reinsurance and calculate profit share under treaties; to identify profitable and unprofitable segments for strategic action.

subsections.title: Data Requirements for Experience Investigations
bullets: Require a reasonable volume of stable, consistent data. 'Consistent' means: data across groups is extracted from the same source, grouped by the same criteria, with the same definitions — eg 'age' must be calculated the same way across groups; 'smoker' must be defined consistently across time periods.

Data must be divided into sufficiently homogeneous risk cells by relevant risk factors — but the ideal must be balanced against having enough data in each cell to be statistically credible. Heterogeneous cells can mask genuine trends within subgroups.

Investigation period trade-off: longer periods yield larger cells and more stability, but may obscure recent trends. Typical periods: expense investigation — 12 months; morbidity investigation — 3–5 years. Upper limit set by the rate at which experience changes.
In practice, the degree of cell subdivision depends on available data volumes. At minimum, different contract types should be separated.

Data sources beyond in-house portfolio: insurance market intelligence; published population data; reinsurers' data (wider pool, but may reflect different UW/product conditions, and confidentiality constraints apply); competitors (if available); similar contracts.

subsections.title: New Business and Renewal Monitoring
bullets: New business monitoring checks: (a) capital strain — is capital allocated to the line sufficient to support volumes written? New business strain occurs when the initial statutory reserve exceeds the available asset share at time zero, even for profitable business; (b) business mix vs pricing assumption — a different mix can invalidate pricing; (c) adequacy of staffing levels to process volumes; (d) commissions paid vs those assumed — differences may signal a shift in distribution mix.

Renewals (critical for short-term products like PMI): analyse lapse rates by region, product type and distribution channel. Impact is highest where initial costs are amortised over multiple renewal years — for PMI, a policy typically becomes profitable only after 2–3 years of renewal.

Persistency analysis factors by decreasing importance: type of contract (long-term vs short-term have structurally different lapse dynamics); duration in force (lapses highest in early years when customer is still assessing suitability); sales method/target market (more needs-based selling → higher persistency); premium frequency and size (monthly premiums create more lapse opportunities; very high premiums may be unaffordable).
Renewal analysis is important for sales management, commission clawback, and marketing impact.

key_terms.definition: The capital consumption arising when the cost of establishing supervisory reserves at policy inception exceeds the available asset share (premium less initial expenses). Even single premium profitable business can cause strain if the reserving basis is sufficiently strong.

subsections.title: Mortality, Morbidity and Claims Amount Experience
bullets: Mortality/sickness data subdivided by: type of contract; age; gender; duration from entry (or duration of claim for termination analysis); smoker/non-smoker status; underwritten status; source of business; occupation/industry; benefit conditions (especially deferred period for IP); geographical location; group size (for group business).
For PMI, additionally subdivide by: excess level, NCD level, hospital band. For CI: subdivide by cause of claim (individual illness).

Deferred period for IP: critically affects claim experience — short deferred periods attract trivial/short claims; a 26-week deferred period will see only genuinely long-term conditions (eg conditions that would heal within 26 weeks will never reach claim stage).

Investigation period: long enough for sufficient data and to smooth seasonal effects; short enough to capture recent trends. Usually not more than 5 years for morbidity. The rate at which health and social trends change sets the practical upper limit.

Process: calculate exposed to risk for each homogeneous group (separate exposures for healthy and sick states for IP); calculate expected transitions by multiplying exposed to risk by assumed transition rates; sum over ages and compare A/E for each transition type (healthy→sick, sick→healthy, healthy→dead, sick→dead).

Claims amount experience — INDEMNITY BUSINESS (PMI, indemnity LTCI): compare incurred amounts against expected by risk cell. For PMI, also analyse by procedure type, hospital band, hospital chain, and individual consultant. Key metric is not just the absolute amount but the trend (inflation) in claim escalation relative to a standard price index — sustained escalation above index is a key long-term driver of deteriorating experience.

Claims amount experience — FIXED BENEFIT BUSINESS (CI, IP): analyse incidence by bands of sum insured. Higher sums insured showing higher claim rates may indicate anti-selection (from sales techniques or underwriting inadequacy).

Use of market vs in-house data for trend analysis: market data advantages — wider pool, trends spotted earlier, essential for new products with no own experience; disadvantages — may not reflect own UW/product conditions, publication lag, may be heterogeneous, limited accessibility and verifiability.

subsections.title: Expense Investigation
bullets: Expenses are converted from total known costs (salaries, property, IT, investment management) into per-policy cost units usable in pricing and reserving. Commission is excluded — it is known with certainty and loaded explicitly in pricing.
Five expense categories: initial (at policy inception); renewal (regularly during policy term); termination (at lapse, death or other exit); claims (in benefit payment); investment management.

Three expense drivers within each category: per policy (fixed per contract); per unit premium (proportionate to premium); per unit sum insured / benefit (proportionate to benefit level). Most expenses are per-policy in practice.

Exceptions to per-policy: marketing expenses → per initial commission; underwriting expenses → per benefit size (larger sum at risk = more UW effort); sickness/LTCI claim expenses → per benefit size (claims management effort scales with benefit).

Direct vs overhead: direct expenses vary with new or in-force business volumes; overhead expenses relate to general management and are insensitive to volume. In practice, per-premium and per-sum-insured categories are almost entirely direct; per-policy categories mix direct and overhead.
Investigation period: minimum one year to capture seasonal effects and full new business cycle. Additionally review 3–5 years to identify trends, adjusting prior years for expense inflation.

Staff cost allocation: staff exclusively in one cell → direct allocation; staff split across cells → use timesheets to apportion; overhead staff → allocate pragmatically (eg in proportion to direct expenses). Property costs → notional rent per department by floor space, then to cells by departmental salary. Computer costs → amortised purchase cost plus maintenance, allocated by usage. Investment costs → expressed as reduction in yield, separately by asset class. One-off capital costs → amortised over useful lifetime; treat as overhead. Exceptional (non-recurring) items → excluded, but a margin in assumptions may be added to allow for unpredictable future one-offs.
Final reconciliation: sum of expenses attributed to all cells should equal total expenses per published accounts.

key_terms.definition: The unit basis on which an expense is attributed to policies: per policy (fixed per contract), per unit of premium, or per unit of sum insured / benefit. Drives both expense investigation and pricing model structure.

Direct expenses vary with the volume of new or in-force business. Overhead expenses (general management, service departments) are insensitive to business volumes. Most overhead expenses are modelled as per-policy costs.

subsections.title: Investment Experience Monitoring
bullets: Investment return is monitored to compare actual return against the assumption used in pricing and reserving, and to assess fund manager performance.
Approximate return formula for period [t, t+1]: i_t = (A_{t+1} − A_t + CF_t) / (A_t + ½CF_t), where A_t = asset value at time t and CF_t = net positive cash flow over the period.
Market value basis includes both realised and unrealised gains in the return measure. Book value basis includes only realised gains — relevant for markets where assets are valued at book value.
Investment experience is analysed by main asset type, and may be assessed gross and net of investment expenses.

Fund manager assessment: compare actual return against pre-set targets, strategy riskiness, and peer managers with similar briefs. Assess over multiple periods to distinguish skill from luck. External managers require closer monitoring than internal.

sections.title: Analysis of Surplus and Embedded Value Profit

subsections.title: Analysis of Surplus — Purpose and Structure
bullets: Surplus at time t = A_t − L_t. Surplus arising over a year = (A_1 − L_1) − (A_0 − L_0).

Analysis of surplus is a breakdown of surplus arising into its sources, enabling identification of which assumptions are most financially significant and how actual experience deviates from valuation assumptions.

Sources of surplus (or deficiency) include: actual mortality/morbidity better/worse than valuation basis; actual expenses lower/higher than assumed; actual withdrawals better/worse than assumed; actual investment return higher/lower than assumed; financial effect of new business written.

Four purposes of analysis of surplus: (1) show the financial effect of A vs E divergences — which assumptions are most financially sensitive; (2) show the financial effect of new business; (3) provide a valuation data/process check if done independently; (4) identify non-recurring surplus to inform dividend/profit distribution decisions; plus compliance with regulatory requirements.

On a prudent supervisory basis: an insurer expects a surplus from each source (since prudent assumptions build in a margin). Negative items on a supervisory basis are a warning signal. On a realistic basis: negative items are as likely as positive, particularly for small portfolios.

Trend analysis: most valuable when several consecutive years of analysis of surplus are reviewed together — reveals genuine deterioration vs random fluctuation, and confirms whether observed deviations warrant assumption revision.

Action triggered by persistent deficiency in one source: revise the assumption; perform a profit test on the revised basis to assess repricing need; consider whether supervisory reserves are still adequate or need strengthening.

key_terms.definition: A breakdown of the surplus arising over a year (change in excess of assets over liabilities) into components corresponding to each actuarial assumption — mortality, morbidity, expenses, withdrawals, investment, new business — quantifying the financial impact of each A vs E deviation.

exam_qa.q: Why and how is an analysis of surplus used by a long-term health insurer?
exam_qa.a: Purposes: (1) Identify which assumptions have the largest financial impact when experience deviates — highlights which risks are most material. (2) Quantify the financial effect of new business written in the year (new business typically reduces surplus on a prudent statutory basis due to acquisition costs and initial reserve establishment). (3) Provide a cross-check on the valuation data and process if derived independently. (4) Distinguish recurring from non-recurring surplus to inform profit distribution decisions. (5) Regulatory compliance. How it is used: Consecutive year analyses are compared to identify trends. Where a source consistently produces less surplus than expected, the underlying assumption is likely wrong — the actuary revises it and profit-tests the product on the new basis. If reserves are on a prudent basis, this also informs whether the prudential margin remains sufficient. If on a best-estimate basis, it informs whether repricing is needed. Solvency capital may also need review.

exam_qa.q: How would you expect surplus on a prudent supervisory basis to be affected by writing new business?
exam_qa.a: New business normally reduces surplus arising in the year of inception. Premiums must cover acquisition costs (commission, marketing), initial administration costs, overheads, and establish prudent supervisory reserves. On a regular-premium basis, the cost of setting up reserves typically exceeds first-year premiums net of acquisition expenses — producing a material negative contribution to surplus (new business strain). The effect is less severe for single premium business due to lower acquisition expenses relative to premium size. On a realistic basis, initial reserves may be zero or negative (reflecting expected future profits), so new business strain may not arise — unless required solvency capital exceeds expected future profits.

subsections.title: Analysis of Embedded Value Profit
bullets: Embedded value (EV) = net assets + present value of expected future profits from existing business. The change in EV over a year is the EV profit.

EV profit analysis purposes: (1) validate calculations, assumptions and data; (2) reconcile EV values between successive years; (3) provide management information for strategic and capital decisions; (4) provide data for executive remuneration schemes (eg EV-based bonuses); (5) provide detailed information for publication in accounts — particularly value of new business written.

EV profit is a more forward-looking measure than statutory surplus: it captures the value of new business written at inception, rather than waiting for the statutory profits to emerge over the policy term.

EV analysis is iterative: over time, the actuary accumulates more credible experience data and can improve model parameterisation. However, ever-changing experience prevents assumptions from fully converging to reality — margins for model error are a permanent feature.

key_terms.definition: The sum of an insurer's net assets and the present value of expected future profits from in-force business, discounted at the risk discount rate. Provides a forward-looking measure of shareholder value from existing business.

The EV contribution attributable to new policies written in the period — calculated as the present value of expected future profits from those policies at inception. A key component of EV profit analysis and management information.

subsections.title: Using Monitoring Results to Control Risks
bullets: Experience analysis results feed into eleven control areas: (a) improve pricing basis; (b) establish/revise reserving basis; (c) change/improve marketing message; (d) revise sales procedures — training, distributor selection, literature wording, commission structure and clawback; (e) ensure adequacy of staffing levels and competence; (f) revise underwriting processes; (g) revise claims handling processes; (h) alter capital allocation methodology; (i) improve data recording systems; (j) revise policy design (eg remove an option rarely exercised or creating moral hazard); (k) improve policy wording (especially CI condition definitions).

Competitor monitoring: watch competitor risk selection, pricing, product design, distribution, and volumes written. If a competitor offers finer risk rating, they attract better risks away — the remaining insurer's pool deteriorates. Monitoring enables response through own rating refinement or product repositioning.

Market experience monitoring advantages vs in-house: wider pool catches trends earlier and provides credibility for new products. Disadvantages: publication delays; different UW/product conditions may make it non-representative; heterogeneous data may create spurious trends; limited access and verifiability.

The process is iterative — assumptions gradually converge toward actual experience, but the ever-changing nature of healthcare, demographics, medical advances, and social trends means perfect convergence is never achieved. Assumption margins for model error are a permanent necessity.

exam_qa.q: List the ways in which the results of experience analyses can be used to control risks.
exam_qa.a: (a) Improve pricing basis — revise assumptions used in premium calculation to reflect updated experience. (b) Establish/revise reserving basis — strengthen or relax assumptions for liability valuation. (c) Change marketing message — address segments or conditions generating unexpectedly high lapse or claim rates. (d) Revise sales procedures — training, distributor selection, literature wording, commission structure and clawback. (e) Ensure adequate staffing levels and competence. (f) Revise underwriting processes — tighten or relax UW where experience shows anti-selection or excessive loadings respectively. (g) Revise claims handling — address sources of claims leakage or fraud. (h) Alter capital allocation methodology — where a product is uncompetitive partly due to excessive capital allocation, a re-assessment may allow competitive repricing. (i) Improve systems and data recording — to support better future monitoring. (j) Revise policy design — remove options rarely exercised, address features creating moral hazard. (k) Improve policy wording — particularly CI condition definitions where claims disputes are arising.

29.summary:
Chapter 29 Summary
Experience will be monitored to:
• update assumptions for future experience
• monitor any adverse trends in experience
• monitor actual compared to expected experience and take corrective actions
• provide management information to aid business decisions
• make better informed decisions about pricing and about the adequacy of reserves.

A reasonable volume of stable consistent data will be required for experience investigations, preferably of sufficient size to allow credible analysis down to the level of broad product groups split by statistically important risk factors.

Analysing market experience and competitor activity is important.

Experience investigations would include:
• new business and renewal rates
• mortality, sickness and other contingencies
• claims amounts
• persistency rates
• expenses
• investment return.

New business analyses
The purposes of these are to check:
• new business strain
• business mix against that assumed in the pricing basis
• adequacy of staffing levels
• commissions paid against those assumed.

Expense investigations (see also the two intermediate summaries in the course notes) Expense investigations aim to convert known total costs such as salaries, systems etc into initial, renewal, termination and investment costs per policy. The allocation will need to assume that some policy costs are independent of policy premium size, some vary by premium size and some vary by benefit size. Many assumptions, some rather arbitrary and pragmatic, will be needed in allocating costs down to cells.

Analysis of surplus/profit
For short‐term insurance, actual vs expected claim and expense ratios for cohorts of business will be analysed.

For long‐term insurance, an analysis of surplus arising may be performed in order to:
• show the financial effect of divergences between the valuation assumptions and the actual experience, exposing which assumptions are the more financially significant
• show the financial effect of writing new business
• provide a check on the valuation data and process, if carried out independently
• identifying non‐recurring components of surplus, thus enabling appropriate decisions to be made about the distribution of surplus to any with‐profits policyholders so entitled (if the health insurance business is written within a wider long‐term business fund) or to shareholders (if a proprietary company) or to members (if a mutual organisation)
• comply with regulatory requirements.

For long‐term insurance, the change in embedded value might also be analysed in order to:
• validate the calculations, assumptions and data used
• reconcile the values for successive years
• provide management information
• provide data for use in executive remuneration schemes
• provide detailed information for publication in the company’s accounts or those of any parent company, in particular the value of new business taken on by the company.

The results of experience analyses may be used to help control risks in a number of different areas, including:
(a)       improving the pricing basis
(b)       establishing / revising the reserving basis
(c)       changing / improving the marketing message
(d)       revising sales procedures in terms of training and selection of distributors, wording and format of literature and the mechanics or any commission payments and
clawback
(e)       providing for the adequacy of staffing (numbers and competence)
(f)       revising underwriting processes
(g)       revising claims handling processes
(h)       altering the capital allocation methodology
(i)       improving the systems and data recording processes
(j)       revising policy design
(k)       improving wording of policy contracts.

subsections.title: Introduction — Combining Knowledge Across Chapters
bullets: The exam tests ability to apply SP1 knowledge holistically to financial management scenarios — not topic by topic.
Students must combine ideas across chapters and apply them to examiner-proposed scenarios.
Three practical frameworks help generate structured responses: (1) accounts-based analysis; (2) life-of-policy analysis; (3) stakeholder-perspective analysis.
A fourth integrating framework is the actuarial control cycle, which structures any scenario into: aims → risks → solutions → monitoring and feedback.

subsections.title: Framework 1 — The Revenue Account and Balance Sheet
bullets: Revenue account structure: Premiums (+), Investment income (+), Increase in reserves (−), Benefit payments (−), Expenses (−), Commission (−), Profit/loss pre-tax, Tax (−), Profit/loss post-tax, Dividends (−), Transfer to balance sheet retained profit.
Using the revenue account as an idea-generation tool: trace any proposed action through each line item to determine financial impact on profit.

Writing a new regular-premium policy: premiums ↑, investment income minimal, reserves ↑ (possibly > premium if valuation basis prudent → new business strain), expenses ↑, commission ↑, benefit payments small in first year → net result usually a loss.
Benefit payment components: claims paid, change in IBNR, change in RBNS, reinsurance recoveries, claim expenses.
Balance sheet: Total assets = Total liabilities (reserves + shareholders' capital/retained profit). Free assets = shareholders' capital.

Revenue account connects to balance sheet: transfer of post-tax profit (net of dividends) increases shareholders' retained profit; reserve increase flows into balance sheet liabilities; cashflow reconciles asset movement.
SCR: often not explicit in revenue account or published balance sheet but must be demonstrated to supervisor via supervisory balance sheet. SCR is covered by free assets.
Variations in accounts format: separate entries for reserves vs SCR; split of 'reserves b/f' and 'reserves c/f'; commission included in expenses; benefit split by component.

key_terms.definition: The immediate capital requirement on writing a new policy, arising because reserves (on a prudent basis) plus any SCR exceed the premium received net of acquisition expenses. Typically a loss of ~1.35×P on day one for a regular premium policy.
Total assets less non-shareholder liabilities (i.e., reserves). Equal to shareholders' capital and retained profit. Must cover the SCR.

exam_qa.q: A new policy is sold part-way through the year. How does this affect each line of the revenue account?
exam_qa.a: Premiums ↑ by a fraction of annual premium (part-year); investment income ↑ marginally (half-year's interest on net cashflow); reserves ↑ significantly (valuation strain if prudent basis); expenses ↑ (initial admin, underwriting); commission ↑ (if broker-sold); benefits unlikely in first partial year. Net effect: a loss, reducing free assets.

subsections.title: Framework 2 — Life and Times of a Policy
bullets: Six stages: (1) The idea, (2) The sale, (3) The first day, (4) A typical year, (5) The last day, (6) Subsequent to the last day.

(1) The idea: market need identified → regulatory/tax environment assessed → company decides to write → product design (structure, assumptions, profit test, sensitivity test, model office, EV impact).
(2) The sale — agent/broker process: initial enquiry → fact find → suitability assessment → quotation → application form → premium payment arrangement → inception.

(3) The first day: reserves established (possibly at prudent basis → new business strain); acquisition costs (commission, initial admin) paid; initial expenses must be covered by loadings; net effect = a loss on the revenue account (e.g. −1.35×P for regular premium); EV increases if policy profitable on EV basis (large ↑ PVFP, small ↓ free assets).

(4) A typical year: premium received, invested; surplus arises if experience better than reserving basis (not premium basis, since reserves already set aside); embedded value moves if experience differs from EV basis — worse experience → ↓ free assets and possibly ↓ PVFP.

(5) The last day (claim): claim paid → loss = (claim amount − reserve released); claim expenses incurred; reinsurance recovery received (typically with a quarter's lag); EV impact = ↓ net asset value (net of tax) + small ↓ PVFP for that policy (offset if portfolio experience on target).
(6) Subsequent to last day: retrospective contest (fraud); extra premiums recovered (non-disclosure); cross-selling opportunities; data feeds into claims investigation and future assumption-setting.
This framework is especially useful for exam questions about the impact of a change on the insurer — trace the change through each stage.

key_terms.definition: Free assets plus present value of future profits (PVFP) from in-force business. Increases when profitable policies are written; decreases when experience is adverse relative to EV basis.

exam_qa.q: What is the embedded value impact of writing a new policy?
exam_qa.a: EV increases if the policy is profitable on the EV basis: large ↑ in PVFP (discounted value of future profits from the policy), small ↓ in free assets (due to new business strain). Net effect is positive if the policy is priced to meet the risk discount rate.

exam_qa.q: If experience is worse than the EV basis over a year, how does EV move?
exam_qa.a: Free assets ↓ by more than expected (worse immediate profit). PVFP may also ↓ if the worse experience changes expectations of future profitability. Net EV decreases.

subsections.title: Policyholders and Future Policyholders
bullets: Policyholders want: products matching their needs (appropriate deferred period/cover level); simplicity of understanding; smooth processes (guaranteed premiums, fast claims); tax-efficiency; affordability and value for money; inflation protection; high security of benefits.
Future policyholders additionally want: smooth and accessible sales process; simple underwriting; wide choice of insurers.
Dependants/relatives additionally want: advice on suitable care facilities (esp. LTC); flexibility and choice over hospitals/care facilities.
Note: many policyholder desires conflict — e.g. low premiums vs guaranteed premiums vs inflation-linked benefits.

subsections.title: Employers
bullets: Employers (as group policyholders) share standard policyholder wants, plus: annual flexibility to change benefit levels as employee needs evolve; simple communication channels between employees and insurer; year-to-year premium guarantees.

subsections.title: The Insurance Company — Internal Stakeholders
bullets: Shareholders: return ≥ risk discount rate; low risk (stable share price); smooth dividends; useful financial information (EV results alongside statutory accounts).
Management: maximise long-term profits; ensure legal compliance and solvency. Short-term pressures may create tension: desire to maximise short-term profits or new business volumes.
Underwriters: non-onerous process for applicants; efficient turnaround; risks meeting company objectives; accurate data capture.
Claims managers: genuine valid claims paid; invalid/fraudulent claims rejected; efficient administration; accurate data collected.
Actuaries: sound pricing and reserving assumptions; monitoring of actual vs expected; actuarial control cycle properly applied.
Marketing department: competitive and appealing products; good relationships with distribution channels; brand awareness.
Administrators: efficient systems; low error rates; good customer service.

subsections.title: External Parties
