bullets: Competitors: maximise their own profits and new business by: lower premiums (from lower margin or lower expenses/economies of scale); better investment returns; better service/underwriting speed; brand; niche products; easier-to-understand designs; higher commissions to sales channels; policyholder incentives.

Sales channels (brokers/agents): maximise remuneration; provide good service; sell suitable products; build loyal customer base. Tension: commission incentive may cause mis-selling (selling highest-commission product rather than most suitable). Mitigated by shift from initial to renewal commission structures.

Reinsurers: maximise long-term profit (solvency constraint); charge risk-adequate premiums + expenses + margin; help insurers reduce claims cost (UW advice); compete on commission, expertise and financial strength; may themselves retrocede some risk.

Healthcare providers (hospitals, surgeons, etc.): profit objectives — adequate pricing, economies of scale, supplier deals, efficiency; service objectives — target success rates, patient expectations, treatment timelines.

Government: protect national health; subsidise the poor; balance the budget; follow social culture/political promises. Additional insurance-specific aims: maximise policyholder security; incentivise socially desirable products; encourage fair competitive marketplace (anti-monopoly rules, consistent disclosure standards, cross-border freedom). May act in own interest (e.g. require investment in government bonds).
Regulator: ensure legal compliance; ensure future solvency; prevent policyholder mistreatment; prevent misleading marketing.
Taxation authorities: maximise tax revenues within the law.
Others: employees (job security, prosperity); investment analysts (EV, profit forecasts if proprietary); competing financial services providers (banks, building societies).

exam_qa.q: What are the implications of sales channels wanting to maximise their remuneration?
exam_qa.a: Agents may prefer products with highest commission over most suitable product for the client. Mitigants: shift to renewal commission (rewards retention of well-matched policies); regulatory conduct rules; agents' own interest in long-term reputation and client relationships.

exam_qa.q: How might the government encourage a fair and competitive marketplace?
exam_qa.a: Restrictions on monopolies; standardised benefit projection assumptions; regulated advertising and sales processes; equivalent regulation and tax treatment across competing financial sectors; cross-border freedom of insurance provision.

sections.title: Framework 4 — The Actuarial Control Cycle Applied

subsections.title: Structure of the Control Cycle in Problem Solving
bullets: Control cycle applied to any exam scenario: (a) identify the company's aims (solvency + profit maximisation); (b) specify the risks threatening those aims; (c) develop solutions; (d) monitor experience and modify approach.
This framework ensures comprehensive coverage in open-ended exam questions about a proposed new product, regulatory change, or management decision.

subsections.title: Worked Example — New Tax-Efficient CI Product (Ruritania)
bullets: Scenario: Government allows house-owners to buy accelerated CI policies up to house value tax-free (premiums from pre-tax income, benefit tax-free). Insurer asked to design and launch the product.
(a) RISKS:

Mortality/morbidity: model risk (constants vs time-varying, omitted CI conditions); parameter risk (limited comparable data — house-owner population may differ from existing CI portfolio); trend risk (gradual mortality improvement, step-change in CI diagnosis rates); random fluctuation risk.

Expenses: unknown product-specific costs; future expense inflation; insufficient sales to recover development costs; cross-subsidy risk (if small policies subsidised by large, adverse mix → insufficient overhead contribution).

Persistency: house-owner policyholders with mortgages may be more economically vulnerable → higher lapse in recession; lapse-and-reentry risk (existing CI policyholders who own homes may lapse current policies to re-enter under new tax-advantaged format).
(b) SOLUTIONS:

Morbidity/mortality: study experience on existing accelerated CI and stand-alone CI; use reinsurer/industry data for unusual conditions and extreme ages; use reviewable premiums to allow repricing; apply standard rating factors (age, gender, smoker status, medical); maintain strict underwriting; quota share reinsurance for parameter error; individual surplus or aggregate XL reinsurance for random fluctuation; claim fluctuation reserve as alternative to reinsurance.

Persistency: price on high withdrawal assumption (within competitive constraint); shift commission structure from initial to renewal; to tackle lapse-and-reentry — offer to reclassify eligible policies (with administration fee); or offer increments to existing policyholders equivalent to expected administration cost saving.

Expenses: reviewable premiums to allow repricing; aggressive marketing to build volume; optimise cross-subsidy — price so all policies at least cover marginal expenses; profit test on range of new business mix scenarios; market research on likely mix.
(c) MONITORING AND MODIFICATION:

Monitor: new business composition (age/gender/premium level); mortality/morbidity investigation (A/E vs priced basis); persistency investigation; expense investigation; EV analysis of movement; analysis of valuation surplus.

Actions: reprice if assumptions inadequate (or too conservative); adjust fixed-vs-percentage-of-premium expense split to reflect actual mix; investigate persistency outliers (by area, by agent); watch related portfolio for lapse-and-reentry signal.

key_terms.definition: Risk that existing policyholders lapse current policies and take out new ones under more favourable terms (e.g. new tax treatment), causing adverse selection and administration strain.
Pricing structure where large policies subsidise small ones so that small policies remain competitively priced. Risk: if actual mix skews small, overall overhead contribution is insufficient.

exam_qa.q: Describe (a) the risks, (b) solutions, and (c) monitoring approach when launching a new tax-advantaged accelerated CI product for house-owners.
exam_qa.a: (a) Risks: mortality/morbidity (model, parameter, trend, random fluctuation); expenses (unknown costs, inflation, volume shortfall, adverse mix); persistency (economic vulnerability of mortgage holders; lapse-and-reentry from existing CI policyholders). (b) Solutions: use existing CI experience + reinsurer data; reviewable premiums; quota share + XL reinsurance; standard rating factors; incentives for retention; cross-subsidy optimisation; market research. (c) Monitor: A/E morbidity, persistency, expense investigations; EV movement; valuation surplus analysis → reprice, adjust expense structure, investigate outliers, watch for lapse-and-reentry.

subsections.title: Conclusion — Using the Control Cycle
bullets: The control cycle is the most powerful structuring device for SP1 exam answers: it ensures business context is set, risks are enumerated systematically, solutions are mapped to risks, and monitoring closes the loop.
Combine all three other frameworks (accounts, life-of-policy, stakeholders) within the control cycle to build comprehensive, well-organised answers.
Key exam technique: use the Syllabus as a checklist after drafting an outline to ensure no topic area has been omitted.

subsections.title: Accelerated critical illness benefit
bullets: This benefit is provided when a policy pays the sum insured upon death or diagnosis of a critical illness, whichever occurs first.
If the life insured suffers a critical illness, then the sum insured is paid and the policy is terminated, ie payment of benefit is ‘accelerated’ forward from payment on death.
Some policies accelerate a portion of the sum insured in which case the contract stays in force and pays the balance of the sum insured upon subsequent death.
Most policies accelerate 100% of the sum insured.

subsections.title: Access to Medical Records Act (SA1)
bullets: In some jurisdictions, legislation may set down the rights that an individual has in relation to the disclosure of the information contained in their medical records.
It could apply to reports obtained for insurance purposes (ie underwriting and claims) or in connection with employment.

subsections.title: Accident, sickness and unemployment (ASU) insurance
bullets: Short-term insurance covering accident, sickness and unemployment.
See also Creditor insurance.

subsections.title: Activities of Daily Living (ADLs)
bullets: Activities of daily living are a number of functional tests against which disability can be measured.
See Incapacity.

subsections.title: Activities of Daily Working (ADWs)
bullets: Activities of daily working are an alternative set of functional tests against which disability can be measured.

The functional tests are specifically related to tasks that might be encountered in the workplace, against which incapacity (disability) can be measured (eg dexterity, ability to follow instructions).
See Incapacity.

subsections.title: Acute illnesses
bullets: Illnesses or conditions of a non-degenerative nature where a cure is a reasonable prospect.
This should be contrasted with Chronic illnesses.
See Chronic illnesses.

subsections.title: Acute illnesses (SA1)
bullets: In some jurisdictions, PMI may only cover surgery and other treatment for illnesses deemed to be acute.

subsections.title: Affinity group
bullets: A group of people with something definitive in common, such as holding a membership or being an employee of an organisation (eg a motoring organisation such as the RAC in the UK or the armed forces), but not common employment.
See Voluntary group.

subsections.title: Age‐at‐entry pricing
bullets: This phrase relates to the practice in some PMI markets of calculating premiums with allowance for the increasing probability of claim as the age of the policyholder increases over the prospective period of cover (often to age 65).
Therefore such policyholders are not subject to the age-related increases that affect PMI products with standard pricing.
Provided policyholders renew their policies each year, premiums will not increase because of policyholders’ increasing age.
However, the insurer usually retains the right to increase premiums subsequently to allow for medical inflation or medical inflation in excess of levels assumed in the original calculation.
The PMI contracts would still be written on an annual renewal basis.

subsections.title: Anti‐selection
bullets: People will be more likely to take out insurance contracts when they believe their risk to be higher than the insurance company has allowed for in the premium, ie the benefits are worth more than the premiums payable.
This is known as anti-selection.

An example in critical illness cover may be where an individual begins to suffer pains in the chest; they take out a policy without mentioning the chest pains and then go to the doctor to see if the pains are due to heart disease.
If they are, then there is a greater likelihood of a claim for benefit.
Anti-selection is also recognised in the tendency for sick or sub-standard lives to legitimately renew policies or take up options providing additional cover without evidence of health.

subsections.title: Appointed representatives
bullets: See Tied Agents.

subsections.title: Assessment period
bullets: The period during which the insurer will assess a condition before deciding upon their acceptance of a claim under critical illness or total permanent disability cover.
The assessment period will not normally be longer than 12 months, provided all the evidence needed has been provided.
Also, the assessment period should only apply to claims for the conditions that must be permanent for cover to apply.

subsections.title: Asset share
bullets: The asset share is the retrospective accumulation of past premiums, less expenses and the cost of cover, at the actual rate of return on the assets.
The accumulation could be done for a single contract or a group of contracts.
It is also referred to as the earned asset share or the retrospective earned asset share.

subsections.title: Bancassurers (Bancassurance)
bullets: This term is generally used to describe companies which offer financial services that encompass both banking and insurance operations.
A major objective is to cross sell the products of one operation to the customers of the other.

subsections.title: Benefit limitation
bullets: Benefit limitation is required so that the claimant with an IP policy retains a financial incentive to return to work.
Benefit payments under an IP policy will be subject to an upper limit, typically specified in a maximum benefit formula.
A major part of the maximum benefit formula will be a maximum replacement ratio (eg 75%).
Many companies impose more stringent limitations for higher salaries in excess of specified limits, and many companies have an overall maximum benefit.
When calculating the amount to be paid to the policyholder, many companies deduct any other income that the claimant may receive if benefits are up to the maximum.

The effect on income net of tax is also very relevant as there may be certain circumstances where the benefit permitted by the formula is more than that required to maintain a claimant’s net income at his or her pre- disability level.

subsections.title: Budget policies
bullets: These are cheaper policies (generally PMI policies) with restricted cover.

Examples are PMI policies with excesses (the insured bears the first tranche, a pre-specified fixed sum, of each claim), policies contingent on public service waiting periods (cover is only provided where the official waiting list is longer than a pre-specified time, often six weeks) and policies that provide in-patient cover only.
The term can also apply to CI policies (restricted list of diseases covered) and IP policies (limited benefit period).
See Limited benefits, Excess.

subsections.title: Bulk rate
bullets: This refers to a premium rate applied uniformly per head on large schemes across a membership type (independent of actual members’ ages or gender).
It can also be referred to as a Unit rate.

subsections.title: Burning cost
bullets: Typically the burning cost is the estimated cost of claims in the forthcoming insurance period, calculated from previous years’ experience adjusted for changes in the numbers insured, the nature of cover and medical inflation.
The term can also be used to describe the historic cost of claims only.

subsections.title: C‐ROSS (SA1)
bullets: C-ROSS is the regulatory framework for insurance companies in China.
The C-ROSS framework is the Chinese equivalent of Solvency II. The C-ROSS framework does have some differences to Solvency II to reflect the characteristics of the Chinese insurance market.

subsections.title: Capitation
bullets: This term relates to the practice of charging for cover by forecasting the likely claims on an individual basis and charging this, adjusted for expenses and profit, as the premium.

In effect, the insurance company separates out a set of medical benefits (such as dental claims or mental health claims) and passes this risk onto the provider - giving them a proportion of the insurance premium for each person managed up-front rather than an amount per claim.
The risk that funds are insufficient to cover treatment lies with the provider of the healthcare service.
In some jurisdictions, capitation may apply in the field of dental covers where the dentist anticipates the costs incurred by his/her patient.

subsections.title: Career break
bullets: A career break clause allows the policyholder to resume his or her coverage after a break in employment (eg study, retrain, travel, start a family), subject to time restrictions.
Career break is an increasingly common feature of IP contracts.

subsections.title: Cash plan or Health cash plan
bullets: This product pays a pre-specified cash sum on the occurrence of certain medical events (hospital stays, physiotherapy, need for spectacles, etc).
Typically the benefits paid are low relative to the true cost and represent cash rather than indemnity benefits.
There is also normally coinsurance and an annual limit.

subsections.title: Change of occupation
bullets: This is a requirement that may be found on IP and possibly CI insurances, whereby the insured must notify the insurer of a change in occupation, possibly resulting in the insurer reclassifying the policyholder to a different occupation class and so revising premiums or disability definition.

subsections.title: Chronic illnesses
bullets: Chronic illnesses are degenerative and/or generally incurable.
The purpose of any treatment is palliative.
This should be contrasted with Acute illnesses.
In some jurisdictions, PMI may not generally cover treatment for chronic illnesses, although some insurers may offer treatment for cancer that includes palliative care.

subsections.title: Claim escalation rates
bullets: This is the rate at which claims, in the course of payment, escalate under IP or LTCI policies.
Unless otherwise stated, they compound annually.
Escalation rates for claims often can fall into three categories such as increase in an index or earnings, or they could increase at a fixed rate.
Benefit increases can apply both prior to and during claim payment.
Increases may be subject to a maximum or minimum rate.

subsections.title: Claim notification period
bullets: In order to improve claims handling procedures, many companies require that claimants notify them of potential claims at an early stage.
The notification requirements of companies generally fall into two broad categories:
The insured is required to notify the company a set time after the incapacity begins,
irrespective of the deferred period.
The insured is required to notify the company at a set time before the end of the
deferred period.

The purpose of this period is twofold: to ensure that valid claims are ready to be paid at the end of the deferred period and for the purposes of early intervention from a claims management perspective.

subsections.title: Claims – late notification (IP)
bullets: To protect itself from financial disadvantage, an insurer will set up rules for dealing with claims that are notified very late (ie notification occurs after the end of the deferred period).
Practice on the late notification of a claim varies greatly, and may depend on whether the notification is received within a certain period from the end of the deferred period.
The options might be:
Backdate the claim to the end of the deferred period (possibly requiring supporting
medical evidence to be provided).
Do not backdate benefits, so that benefits will be lost:
-         Treat the date of notification as the date the benefit becomes payable.
-         Assume the commencement of incapacity to be the date of notification.
-         Assume that incapacity started at a set period prior to notification.
Reduce the benefit payable.
Refuse / decline the claim.
Subject all late notified claims to individual consideration.

subsections.title: Claims paid, claims incurred
bullets: When certain group insurance policies are to be renewed, prospective insurers are given information on claims history over previous scheme years.
This comes in one of two forms:
Claims paid, where the amounts actually paid by previous insurers in each scheme
year are totalled, or
Claims incurred, where the amounts relative to all claims that commenced in a
particular year of insurance are totalled; this equates to amounts paid plus outstanding reserves.
Claims paid or incurred will also be integral components in many forms of reporting, eg the assessment of profitability in management reports, and appropriate usage is important.

subsections.title: Claims pre‐authorisation
bullets: Some PMI insurers require (others recommend) that intended courses of treatment or surgery be approved by them for policy coverage before costs are incurred.
This process can often provide opportunity for the insurer to manage care provision or reduce after-the- event claim denials.

subsections.title: Coinsurance
bullets: Coinsurance is the general term given to a PMI policy condition whereby the policyholder is required to pay, at least in part, for medical expenses incurred, maybe on a percentage basis.
See also Excess.
The insured and the insurer share the cost of the claim in fixed proportions (eg insured pays 20% of the claim).
In other contexts, coinsurance also refers to the situation where two insurers share the contract with the policyholder and also may refer to reinsurance on an original terms basis.

subsections.title: Combined ratio
bullets: See Loss ratio.

subsections.title: Community rating
bullets: Community rating most often refers to the practice of charging all policyholders (or a significant subset of the persons insured) the same premium rate irrespective of rating factors such as age, gender and medical history.
Community rating sometimes refers to the process of applying tabular rates to applicants irrespective of claims history.

subsections.title: Comprehensive cover
bullets: The standard or comprehensive PMI cover provides for full reimbursement of all medical costs incurred in hospitals within the appropriate bands and for such other treatments as the policy stipulates.
Occasionally some high policy limits will apply (per year or per section of risk).
See Policy limits.

subsections.title: Continuation option
bullets: A continuation option describes a benefit under an insurance policy whereby the insured can choose, without having to provide evidence of health, to continue the cover provided by the policy under circumstances where it has otherwise ceased.
Such circumstances might apply where the individual has left their place of work and is no longer covered by an employer-sponsored scheme or where an individual policy has expired.
The terms under which the option is effected are those applicable to a healthy life for the age at the date when the option arises.

subsections.title: Continued personal medical exclusions (CPME)
bullets: This is ‘no worse terms’ acceptance whereby the new PMI insurer promises only to carry forward such cover for medical conditions as existed under the previous insurance policy.
See No worse terms.

subsections.title: Cost plus
bullets: Many of the largest group PMI schemes will self-insure.
Some of these will seek to limit the possible downside by insuring against an extreme experience.
Insurance will be arranged to cover the excess of a pre-agreed claims fund.
Such an arrangement is called cost plus cover or stop loss cover
See Stop loss.

subsections.title: Credibility
bullets: This relates to experience rating of group healthcare insurance products.
The term credibility refers to the factor, between 0 and 1, which represents the proportion of the final risk premium that is derived from past experience, the balance coming from book rates.
The value of the factor depends on the size of the scheme.
See Experience rating.

subsections.title: Creditor insurance
bullets: This is a form of cover that is taken out to protect a loan or mortgage.
It may consist solely of life insurance that will repay the outstanding loan if the borrower dies before it is repaid.
Often cover is extended to pay off the loan following a total and permanent disability and, more recently, critical illness.
Creditor insurance can also be taken out to cover repayments during temporary disability or unemployment.

subsections.title: Critical illness
bullets: This term can be used to refer to the type of contract (see Accelerated critical illness benefit and Stand-alone critical illness plans) that provides benefits on the diagnosis of a ‘critical illness’, or to the specific illnesses covered under such contracts.
These illnesses are defined by the insurer, and may cover conditions such as cancer, heart attack, kidney failure, major organ transplant, multiple sclerosis, stroke.
The illnesses or conditions are typically perceived by the public to be serious (ie life threatening or lifestyle threatening), and to occur frequently.

subsections.title: Day case admissions
bullets: This term relates to the increasing practice of treating the more straightforward operations in hospital surgical units on the day of admission, occupying a bed but being discharged on the same day (ie no overnight stay).

subsections.title: Deductible
bullets: See Excess.

subsections.title: Deferred period
bullets: This is a term most often encountered in IP insurance, meaning the period of incapacity before any benefit is paid.
For example in the UK, the most common deferred periods in the market are 4, 13, 26 and 52 weeks.
Other less common deferred periods are 0, 1, 8 and 104 weeks. 28 weeks is often found as a deferred period under group arrangements.
See Waiting period.

subsections.title: Definition of incapacity
bullets: The majority of companies effectively have a standard definition of incapacity such as ‘totally unable through sickness or accident to perform your own occupation and not following any other for profit or reward’ although the exact words used will vary.
This definition is normally applied throughout the duration of the contract.
Some companies amend their definition of incapacity after a certain claim period has elapsed (usually two years).
Most companies reserve the right to apply an alternative definition of incapacity depending upon the occupation of the insured.
Some companies have introduced a definition of incapacity based on an abilities test.
Companies will also generally use different definitions of incapacity when cover is offered to housepersons and where cover is maintained on a restricted basis during a period of unemployment.
See Housepersons, Unemployment benefit, ADWs.

subsections.title: Direct marketing
bullets: This is the marketing of products directly to the potential customers with no intermediary or salesperson involved.
It could be via mail, by advertising in newspapers or periodicals, by telephone, email or the internet.
In all situations the customer is invited to apply for the product, usually directly to the insurance company.

subsections.title: Direct salesforce
bullets: An insurance company that employs the services of salespeople to sell only their products directly to the customer is said to operate a direct salesforce.

subsections.title: Earned premium
bullets: Earned premium is the proportion of premiums written and received that relates directly to the expired period of cover.
For example, consider:
A policy commencing on 1 October of year N with an annual premium of £300. This

£300 would contribute £75 to earned premiums in the year N and £225 to earned premiums in the year N + 1. The second annual premium would contribute £75 to earned premiums in year N + 1 and £225 to those in year N + 2 etc.
A policy commencing on 1 October of year N with a single premium of £800 for two
years’ cover.
Such a policy would contribute £100 to earned premiums in the year N, £400 to earned premiums in year N + 1 and £300 to earned premiums in the year N + 2.

subsections.title: EIOPA (SA1)
bullets: EIOPA is the European Insurance and Occupational Pensions Authority, one of the EU’s main financial supervisory bodies.

Its core responsibilities are to support the stability of the financial system, transparency of markets and financial products across the EU, as well as the protection of insurance policyholders, pension scheme members and beneficiaries.
It has powers to develop and implement technical standards and has been heavily involved in the development of Solvency II.

subsections.title: Elective surgery
bullets: This is surgery that is deemed to be non-emergency.

subsections.title: Excess, Deductible
bullets: These terms (the latter more often in the United States) are used to describe the policy condition whereby the insured is responsible for the first £x or $x of any claim.
The excess / deductible may operate not on individual claims but on the aggregate of claims over a policy year, or be applied per life or per policy.

subsections.title: Exclusions
bullets: These are causes of disability (or ‘perils’) that are explicitly excluded from the cover provided by a policy.
The most common exclusions are:
war, terrorism, acts of violence, civil unrest
self-inflicted injury or attempted suicide
drugs
alcohol
hazardous pastimes or sports
aerial activity other than as a fare-paying passenger
criminal acts
failure to seek or follow medical advice.
A further common exclusion for PMI business is treatment relating to standard pregnancy, and potentially also complications of pregnancy and childbirth.

subsections.title: Experience rating
bullets: Experience rating is the process by which partial or full credibility is given to the past claims history of the risk in assessing the premium payable at renewal.
If the premium for a risk is assessed by only using the claims history for that risk, the experience is said to be 100% or fully credible.
If the risk is assessed using both the clams history for the risk and the claims history for the rating group to which the risk belongs, the experience is less than 100% or partially credible.

subsections.title: Facultative
bullets: Meaning optional, this is a process of reinsurance where there are no obligations on either the insurance company or the reinsurer to offer and accept risks.
The insurance company offers facultative business when and where it chooses, and the reinsurer can decide whether or not to accept it.

subsections.title: Financial Conduct Authority (FCA) (SA1)
bullets: The FCA is the UK regulator responsible for regulation of conduct in financial markets (and the infrastructure that supports those markets) and the prudential regulation of financial services companies that do not fall under the scope of the PRA (eg insurance brokers and smaller investment firms).

subsections.title: Financial Reporting Council (FRC) (SA1)
bullets: including setting technical actuarial standards, and issuing accounting standards in the UK.

subsections.title: Fixed price surgery
bullets: This phrase relates to an agreement between a PMI insurer and a hospital (or hospital chain) whereby all surgical procedures of a type will be charged at a particular cost per case rate, regardless of the individual complexity.
These rates are commonly referred to as ‘case rates’ or ‘procedure pricing’.
The price includes all care, including any that may be necessary following complications.

subsections.title: Free cover
bullets: This term refers to a benefit level in a group risk arrangement, below which the member is not subject to individual underwriting.
Free cover is usually a function of the number of members or the aggregate of benefits provided.
Free cover limits are also known as ‘non-selection limits’.

subsections.title: GAAP (SA1)
bullets: This is the set of Generally Accepted Accounting Principles (‘GAAP’) that may be used to produce accounts in a particular jurisdiction.
For example, you can get US GAAP, Chinese GAAP, UK GAAP etc.

subsections.title: General practitioner (GP)
bullets: This phrase refers to the individual’s own doctor, who provides primary medical care and is normally the first port-of-call for all health concerns.
The GP may or may not hold all individual health records pertinent to that person.

subsections.title: GP referral
bullets: Normal medical protocols would involve the patient being referred for secondary care after an initial consultation with his or her general practitioner.
In the UK, most PMI policies require a GP referral as a pre-condition to cover.

subsections.title: Group business
bullets: Group business is insurance covering a number of individuals under a single policy.

The members of the group are linked in some way (eg employees in the same company or holders of the same credit card) and there is often a sponsor (eg employer) who facilitates in the payment of premiums and administrative matters.
Membership may be compulsory or voluntary.

subsections.title: Guaranteed premium rates
bullets: This describes the situation where the benefit / premium relationship is set from the outset for the duration of the policy.
See Reviewable premiums.

subsections.title: Health cash plan
bullets: See Cash plan.

subsections.title: Health Maintenance Organisation (HMO)
bullets: This is a form of health organisation akin to insurance that combines a range of coverages on a group basis.
A group of doctors and other medical professionals offer care through the HMO for a monthly subscription.
However, only visits to professionals within the HMO network are covered by the policy.
All visits, prescriptions and other care must be cleared by the HMO in order to be covered.
A primary doctor within the HMO handles referrals.
They are more common in the USA.

subsections.title: Hospital bands
bullets: PMI insurers sometimes split the hospitals permitted under the policy into categories (by price of treatment), known as hospital bands, usually three in number.
The policy premium is generally scaled in line with the policyholder’s choice of hospital group.
In some jurisdictions, insurers are moving away from this classification and using more sophisticated methods of rating, such as geographical rating factors.

subsections.title: Housepersons
bullets: People who are not in paid employment but whose contribution to a family might be measured in financial terms.
The majority of IP insurers also provide housepersons cover.
Two categories have been identified by reference to the definition of incapacity, as follows: Category 1:         Ability to perform certain activities of daily living (ADLs).
Category 2:         Confinement to home or hospital or inability to perform normal household duties.
See ADLs.

subsections.title: IFRS (SA1)
bullets: International Financial Reporting Standards (‘IFRS’) are the accounting standards developed and maintained by the International Accounting Standards Board.
They are intended to provide a consistent basis for accounts globally, and are currently used in over 100 countries including the EU.

subsections.title: Immediate needs annuity
bullets: This is an immediate annuity purchased by an impaired life requiring long-term care.
It provides regular payments guaranteed throughout the annuitant’s lifetime, and thus provides protection against the uncertain survival duration.

subsections.title: Incapacity
bullets: According to the ABI Statement of Best Practice for IP in the UK: The term ‘incapacity’ should normally be used instead of the term ‘disability’, in particular as regards the definition of disability clause in the policy contract, which should be called the definition of incapacity clause.

subsections.title: Income protection insurance
bullets: Income protection insurance (formerly known as permanent health insurance (PHI)) provides cover against incapacity.
The benefit is paid as an income (usually monthly) for as long as the disability exists up to a pre-defined age, usually 60 or 65, or retirement if earlier.
This is a protection policy that insures against the loss of income, as a result of sickness or injury, during the term of the policy.

subsections.title: Increase options
bullets: There are two types of option under which increases in the benefit can be secured without formal underwriting.
The first covers those options whereby the increases in benefit are incorporated in the original contract and operate automatically.
They may be subject to predetermined premiums, which may:
be level throughout the lifetime of the policy
increase in line with the benefit
increase by some other specified pattern.
Alternatively, increments may be costed as they arise.
The increase can be a fixed monetary sum, or be in line with a fixed percentage or some form of earnings or prices index.

This second type of option is that which includes a periodic opportunity for the policyholder to increase the benefit by a fixed percentage, with the new policy being costed in the normal way, based on the then-current age and outstanding term.
Increase options are found under critical illness and income protection insurance policies.

subsections.title: Incurred but not reported (IBNR)
bullets: This term relates to claims where the claim event has happened but the insurer has yet to receive notification.
The insurer is required to hold reserves against such events when assessing results or submitting accounts for a particular period.

subsections.title: Indemnity
bullets: Under the principle of indemnity, the insured is restored to the same financial position after a loss as before the loss.
PMI is usually an indemnity contract.
However some policies incorporate cash limits in particular circumstances, and so do not provide full indemnity.

subsections.title: Individual business
bullets: This is insurance covering an individual under a single policy document.
Where the insurance additionally covers immediate family members on a joint life basis, this is also considered to be individual business.

subsections.title: Industry body (SA1)
bullets: In some jurisdictions, industry bodies have been set up to support health and care insurance companies.

An example of an industry body is the Association of British Insurers in the UK, which is a trade association which supports insurance companies in the UK. An example of the work done by the ABI is the development of industry standard wording for health and care insurance companies in the UK.

subsections.title: In‐patient
bullets: This is a person who is admitted to hospital and occupies a bed overnight.

subsections.title: Insurance intermediaries
bullets: Insurance intermediaries are independent of any particular financial services company (contrasted with a member of a direct salesforce).
They select and recommend the products that they consider to be most appropriate for the customer using various criteria.
They are known in some territories as brokers or financial advisers.
See Direct salesforce and Tied agents.

subsections.title: Insurance premium tax (IPT) (SA1)
bullets: In some jurisdictions general insurance premiums may be subject to tax.
For example in the UK most general insurance premiums are subject to an insurance premium tax (IPT), currently (May 2018) at the rate of 12.0%.
So, for example, this tax is levied on PMI premiums.

subsections.title: Insurtech (SA1)
bullets: Insurtech is a term used to describe the use of innovations in technology to provide savings and or efficiencies from the current models used by insurance companies.

subsections.title: International Classification of Diseases (ICD)
bullets: This is a classification of diseases and surgical operations, by both coding and wording, in order to maintain an international standard.
Most countries classify diseases using ICD-9 or ICD-10 codes.

subsections.title: International Actuarial Association (IAA) (SA1)
bullets: The IAA is a worldwide association of professional actuarial bodies.
Its aim is to represent the actuarial profession and promote its role, reputation and recognition in the international domain.
One of the ways it does this is by developing ‘model’ International Standards of Actuarial Practice.

subsections.title: Investigative surgery
bullets: This is surgery that is undergone to advance the diagnosis (nature and extent of the complaint).
It is generally covered under PMI products but may not be so under MME products.

subsections.title: Irreversible
bullets: Irreversible conditions are those that cannot be cured by medical treatment and/or surgical procedures at the time of the claim.
This term is generally used to define CI conditions - for example, blindness, deafness, loss of speech and paralysis of the limbs.

subsections.title: Key Features document (SA1)
bullets: Insurance companies in some jurisdictions may be required by legislation to provide new policyholders with a short synopsis of the product which is easy to read and capable of being understood.

This documentation could set out the required information such as aims and purposes of the policy, nature of the policyholders’ commitment, a description of the risk factors and illustrative projections.

subsections.title: Keyperson cover
bullets: Keyperson products are taken out by an employer to cover key employees.
They fall into two categories:
Those designed to provide compensation for loss of profits.
Those designed to cover the key employee’s salary (to facilitate the temporary
recruitment of a replacement).
The insurance should cover sickness, incapacity or death of the key employee.

subsections.title: Limited benefits / budget plans
bullets: A number of IP providers offer policies under which, in the event of a claim, benefit payments are restricted to a certain number of years.
These policies are sometimes referred to as budget plans because they are cheaper than contracts that provide benefit to the terminal age.
See Budget policies.

subsections.title: Linked claims
bullets: These relate to IP claims whereby a claimant, having been in receipt of claim payments, recovers and returns to work, but within a specified period (the ‘link period’) suffers a recurrence of the same disability, and is eligible for immediate claim payments without the imposition of another deferred period.
The standard definition refers to a linked period of six months.
The link period is also sometimes called the ‘off-benefit’ period.

subsections.title: Long‐term care insurance
bullets: This type of insurance can be used to help provide financial security against the risk of needing either home or nursing-home care as an elderly person, ie post-retirement.
The contract could pay for all the costs of care throughout the remainder of life (an indemnity contract), or could provide a cash lump sum or annuity to contribute towards the costs of care.
in terms of ADLs.

subsections.title: Loss ratio
bullets: The ratio of claims incurred, including allowance for ultimate settlement amounts, to the relevant premium.
In some instances the ratio is calculated with expenses also in the numerator, but this statistic should be more properly termed the combined or operating ratio.

subsections.title: Major medical expenses (MME)
bullets: MME in the UK is a PMI product variant that pays a fixed amount (from a schedule relating to severity) to policyholders who undergo surgery of a non-investigative and non-cosmetic nature.
MME in the USA is a comprehensive PMI product type offering reimbursement for the costs of primary, secondary and tertiary care as defined in the policy.

subsections.title: Managed care
bullets: Managed care is the term given to a process whereby an insurer intervenes in the provision of medical care with the dual objective of optimising the quality of treatment for the policyholder and controlling the cost (by such means as preferred provider utilisation and claims pre-authorisation).

subsections.title: Medical history disregarded (MHD)
bullets: Under this approach, an individual’s proposal to effect insurance will be underwritten without regard to that individual’s past medical history, ie there are no exclusions for pre-existing medical conditions.
It is most commonly used for group PMI business, or it may be offered to an individual on transfer from a group to individual PMI policy.

subsections.title: Medical inflation
bullets: This term generally refers to the annual increase in the average cost of medical treatment per insured life.
It could reflect increases due to an increase in treatment costs and/or an increase in average incidence.

subsections.title: Microinsurance
bullets: Microinsurance is the term used for the growing market in insurance products (including health insurance) that are characterised by low premiums and low coverage limits.

Based on a pooling or community approach, microinsurance is generally targeted at the low wealth segments of a population and provides a social benefit in providing access to insurance cover for such socio-economic groups.
It is currently well developed in India and some parts of Africa.

subsections.title: Minimum Capital Requirement (MCR) (SA1)
bullets: Under Pillar 1 of Solvency II, there are two distinct capital requirements that have to be held in excess of technical provisions: the Solvency Capital Requirement (‘SCR’) and the Minimum Capital Requirement (‘MCR’).
The MCR is the capital level below which the insurer would lose its authorisation.
It is determined using a simple factor-based linear formula which is targeted at a Value at Risk over one year with 85% confidence.

subsections.title: Moratorium
bullets: Certain PMI products (and possibly also group CI insurance), in place of formal medical underwriting at inception, do not cover medical conditions that existed during a pre- specified period, often five years, but will then cover these conditions if no treatment, symptoms or advice takes place for a period of, typically, two or three consecutive years after first taking out the policy (or after receiving further treatment).
The insurer is however on immediate risk for all other conditions.
See Pre-existing conditions.

subsections.title: No claims discount (NCD)
bullets: Under PMI, an NCD system refers to an increasing discount to some reference premium applied for each year that no claims are made, to a limit.
For each claim made, the level of discount reduces and could result in a premium higher than the reference premium.
This is used as an own-experience proxy for more accurate risk segmentation.

subsections.title: No worse terms (NWT)
bullets: In certain circumstances, on PMI or group business policy renewal, a new insurer will agree to offer cover at least as comprehensive and with no additional underwriting conditions as the policyholder’s current policy; the renewal or ‘switch’ is accepted on no worse terms.

subsections.title: Non‐contestability clause (SA1)
bullets: A non-contestability clause states that once an applicant has successfully completed underwriting and gone on risk, the insurer is not able to challenge a claim after a defined period of time has elapsed (eg two years) on the basis of deliberate, careless or negligent non-disclosure on the original application.

subsections.title: Non‐medical limits
bullets: Non-medical limits denote the maximum (long-term) policy benefits for which one can propose without needing an automatic medical examination or PMAR.
The fact that a benefit falls within the non-medical limit is no guarantee that the proposer will not be asked to attend a medical examination.
Insurers always reserve the right to call for any additional medical evidence that they feel is necessary in the light of any information that they already have.
See Private Medical Attendant’s Report.

subsections.title: Non‐proportional reinsurance
bullets: In non-proportional reinsurance, the reinsurance protection covers amounts above certain pre-defined limits, rather than splitting individual claims in given percentages.
Under PMI, it will often apply to a portfolio of risks, not individual risks; the reinsurer will pay when the losses on the whole portfolio exceed an agreed limit.
Examples of non-proportional reinsurance include stop loss and catastrophe excess of loss cover.

subsections.title: Occupational classes
bullets: Occupational classes are used to rate proposers in many lines of health and care insurance.
Examples of occupations that fall into different sample classes are:
Class 1 - actuary, accountant, archaeologist, astrologer, author.
Class 2 - doctor, dog breeder, counsellor.
Class 3 - teacher, plumber.
Class 4 - roofer, Heavy Goods Vehicle driver.

subsections.title: Open enrolment
bullets: Open enrolment refers to the process in some markets whereby an insurer is obliged to accept all proposers for insurance at standard rates.

subsections.title: Operating ratio
bullets: See Loss ratio.

subsections.title: Original terms
bullets: The original terms method of reinsurance is where the reinsurer has a virtually identical contract to the insurance company in respect of the reinsured portion of the risk.
The reinsurer receives the same gross premiums for its share as the insurance company, except that the policy fee is normally retained in full.
The reinsurer is responsible for its full proportionate share of the sum insured in the event of a claim, and, likewise, for any surrender values that are paid.

subsections.title: Out‐patient
bullets: This is a person who attends hospital for treatment or consultation but who does not occupy a bed.
In some markets this is known as ‘ambulatory treatment’.

subsections.title: Own funds (SA1)
bullets: Under Pillar 1 of Solvency II, the phrase ‘own funds’ refers to assets in excess of technical provisions and subordinated liabilities.
These are split into basic and ancillary own funds, which are then tiered based on specific criteria.

subsections.title: Own Risk and Solvency Assessment (ORSA) (SA1)
bullets: Under Pillar 2 of Solvency II, each insurance company is required to carry out an Own Risk and Solvency Assessment (ORSA).

The ORSA has been defined as: The entirety of the processes and procedures employed to identify, assess, monitor, manage and report the short and long term risks an insurance undertaking faces or may face and to determine the own funds necessary to ensure that the undertaking’s overall solvency needs are met at all times.

A number of other jurisdictions where Solvency II does not apply now require insurers to carry out ORSA assessments that are similar to the assessments required under Solvency II under their own regulatory regime.
For example ORSAs are required by insurers in the US and China.

subsections.title: Permanent
bullets: This term is used to describe a health condition that is expected to last throughout the insured person’s life, irrespective of when the cover ends or the insured person retires.
This definition would be used, for example, in relation to a claim for total permanent disablement (TPD) benefit under a CI contract.

subsections.title: Personal accident insurance
bullets: This type of insurance provides specified fixed benefit amounts in the event that the insured suffers the loss of one or more limbs, or other specified injury.

subsections.title: Personal capability assessment (PCA)
bullets: An alternative way of assessing disability that is largely independent of the age and the occupation (if any) of the person being assessed.
Personal capability assessment involves assessment of the ability to complete everyday tasks of living (eg climbing stairs, bending, lifting and carrying).
See Activities of Daily Living (ADLs), Activities of Daily Working (ADWs).

subsections.title: Policy limit
bullets: This is the maximum amount that can be paid out under a policy, sometimes expressed over a defined period of time (eg annual limit).
Some indemnity policies limit the benefits payable under particular sections of the policy (eg PMI policies may limit payments for physiotherapy to £500 in any policy year).
Such policies only provide partial indemnity.
See Indemnity policy.

subsections.title: Pre‐existing conditions (PEC) exclusion
bullets: Under the terms of such an exclusion, cover is not provided in respect of any of the conditions listed in the policy that the life insured has already suffered, ie where the condition pre-existed at the commencement of cover.
It is also usual to exclude cover for any condition where the life insured has previously suffered from another medical condition that gives a materially greater risk of that condition occurring.
PEC exclusions can be used in PMI, CI and IP contracts.
See Moratorium.

subsections.title: Preferred provider organisation (PPO)
bullets: A PMI policy may restrict cover to such treatment as is carried out in certain medical establishments (with whom it has special arrangements, often financial) or may limit the scale of reimbursement outside these establishments.
The premium for such policies where treatment must take place in a PPO will usually be cheaper than if the claimant had the choice of any possible provider.

subsections.title: Pregnancy clause (SA1)
bullets: In some jurisdictions insurers may have a clause in their IP policies terms which covers how claims should be treated with respect to complications with pregnancy.

Actual practice may vary, but one approach could be that ‘disability attributable to pregnancy or complications thereof is not covered unless the condition continues for more than 13 weeks after the termination of the pregnancy.
The deferred period is then deemed to commence’.

subsections.title: Primary care
bullets: Primary care is the term given to such advice and treatment as provided by a general practitioner.
This is not typically covered by a PMI policy in the UK, although elsewhere primary care would be an insured benefit.
See Secondary care, Tertiary care.

subsections.title: Private medical attendant’s report (PMAR)
bullets: This report is sought by the insurer to provide further insight into an individual’s state of health at the proposal or claim stage.

subsections.title: Private medical insurance (PMI)
bullets: This is a product under which in return for premiums, an insurer promises to pay certain sums of money on the occurrence of certain medical events.

The product is generally classified as short-term insurance, the events are frequently surgical in nature (predominantly so in the UK) and the amounts paid usually indemnify costs incurred by the individual in receiving treatment.

subsections.title: Profit sharing
bullets: Profit sharing relates to the practice under group scheme business whereby the insurer rewards the group for better than expected experience with some share in the profit arising.
This share might be expressed as a cash refund or as a discount against the future premium.

subsections.title: Proportionate benefit
bullets: Under IP policies, if a claimant takes up employment in an occupation that is different to the one from which they were originally incapacitated, it is usual for the continuing benefit to be reduced.
The reduction will relate to the ratio that the gross earnings from the new job bear to those from the occupation against which disability was being claimed.
It is a standard requirement that disability from the original occupation continues whilst a proportionate benefit is being paid.
Some companies require a full claim to be established before a proportionate benefit can be claimed.

subsections.title: Prudential Regulation Authority (PRA) (SA1)
bullets: prudential regulation of all deposit-taking institutions, insurance providers and large investment firms.

subsections.title: Rehabilitation / partial benefit
bullets: This IP benefit is payable when a claimant is no longer totally unable to follow his or her original occupation and returns to it in a reduced capacity.
The amount of benefit is usually calculated in the same way as that for proportionate benefit, as described above.

Rehabilitation can also apply to the process of counselling whereby disability counsellors assist disabled persons with advice on practical matters to do with the benefit and their disability, in order to aid a return to work.

subsections.title: Replacement ratio (or net replacement ratio)
bullets: This refers, in the context of income protection insurance, to the ratio of net (in benefit) income to net pre-disability income.

A value of less than one is desirable from the insurer’s viewpoint, to provide a financial incentive for the claimant’s return to work, especially given that expenses in disability may be less than those in normal (working) health.

subsections.title: Reported but not settled (RBNS)
bullets: This term relates to claims that have been notified to the insurer, but the sum insured due has yet to be agreed and paid.
The insurer is required to hold reserves against these claims, when assessing results or submitting accounts for a particular period.

subsections.title: Residence
bullets: This clause limits the countries in which the policyholder may be resident to make a valid claim, to ensure effective claims management.

subsections.title: Retail Price Index (RPI)
bullets: An index published by the Office for National Statistics (ONS) in the UK. It is an average measure of change in the prices of goods and services bought for the purpose of consumption by the vast majority of households in the UK. It is compiled monthly and has been published regularly since 1948.
The RPI includes prices for food and drink, tobacco, housing, household goods and services, personal goods and services, transport fares, motoring costs, clothing and leisure goods and services.
The data are collected through surveys.
See National Average Earnings Index (NAEI).

subsections.title: Reviewable premiums
bullets: This is a form of premium offered by income protection and critical illness insurance providers.

It allows the insurer to alter the premiums if the prospective claims experience (or any aspect of the premium basis specified in the contract) for the portfolio is different from that which was originally expected.
Most companies that employ reviewable rates undertake the reviews every five years, though experience monitoring is done on a regular basis.
See Guaranteed premium rates.

subsections.title: Rider benefits
bullets: These are extra benefits that can be added to a basic policy either at commencement of the cover or sometimes at defined policy anniversaries of the contract.
These benefits would be underwritten at outset and would normally affect premium rates and possibly initial underwriting requirements.
For marketing reasons, some riders are provided for the policyholder at no additional charge.

subsections.title: Riders (IP)
bullets: A number of additional benefits may be added to standard IP covers, such as:
hospitalisation benefit
lump sum TPD benefit
critical illness benefit
terminal illness benefit
unemployment and health waiver of premium benefit
death benefit
State benefit option (this pays an additional IP benefit if you do not receive State
benefits)
fatal accident benefit
retirement protection option.

subsections.title: Risk equalisation
bullets: Risk equalisation applies in some markets whereby the profits or losses on specified policies or risks are pooled and reapportioned among participating insurers so that each shares in the average market experience.

subsections.title: Risk premium
bullets: The risk premium method of reinsurance is used when a long-term insurer wishes to reinsure only the risk element (mortality or morbidity) of a policy.
The insurer and reinsurer agree a set of risk premium factors to be applied to the benefit reinsured; these factors will generally (if permitted by legislation) vary by age, gender and smoker status.
Under CI and other health contracts, these risk premium rates are often used as the basis for the insurer’s office premium rates.

subsections.title: Secondary care
bullets: Secondary care is the term given to such advice and treatment as provided by hospitals, consultants and other specialists, usually after referral by the patient’s general practitioner.
See Primary care, Tertiary care.

subsections.title: Smoker / non‐smoker rates
bullets: Most companies offer different rates for smokers and non-smokers for long term contracts, reflecting the different morbidity or mortality of smokers and non-smokers.
Smoker / non- smoker differentials would not apply to the pricing of larger group schemes that are fully experience-rated.
Smoking status is defined by each insurer, eg not used smoking materials of any kind for the last three years.

subsections.title: Solvency II (SA1)
bullets: Solvency II refers to the set of regulatory requirements implemented on 1 January 2016 for insurance firms in the EU, based on a three pillar approach.

The aim of EU solvency rules is to ensure that insurance undertakings are financially sound and can withstand adverse events, in order to protect policyholders and the stability of the financial system as a whole.

subsections.title: Solvency Capital Requirement (SCR) (SA1)
bullets: Under Pillar 1 of Solvency II, there are two distinct capital requirements that have to be held in excess of technical provisions: the Solvency Capital Requirement (‘SCR’) and the Minimum Capital Requirement (‘MCR’).
The SCR is a Value at Risk measure calibrated to a 99.5% confidence level.
It can be calculated using a prescribed standard formula approach, or by using a company-specific internal model which must be approved by the regulator.

subsections.title: Stand‐alone critical illness plans
bullets: These are policies that only provide cover against critical illness and do not provide (or accelerate in the case of a stand-alone rider) any benefit in the event of death.
Following payment of the critical illness benefit, the policy terminates.
Occasionally such policies may offer a nominal sum in the event of death before a critical illness is suffered.
See Accelerated critical illness benefit.

subsections.title: Statutory actuarial role (SA1)
bullets: In many jurisdictions, there is a statutory requirement for a health and care insurance company transacting long-term business to appoint actuaries into certain positions.
The titles applied to these roles vary depending on local regulations.

For example in the European Union under Solvency II there is a statutory requirement to have a Chief Actuary, whilst other jurisdictions have a requirement for an Actuarial Function Holder, Appropriate Actuary or Appointed Actuary.
The detail of any statutory actuarial roles and their responsibilities in a particular jurisdiction will be set out in the local regulations.

subsections.title: Stop loss
bullets: Similar to cost plus, stop loss is a reinsurance contract whereby an insurer’s claims exposure will be restricted at some multiple of premium.
The stop loss arrangement will stipulate a loss ratio above which the reinsurer becomes responsible for all or the majority of further claims.

subsections.title: Subject access request (SAR) (SA1)
bullets: In some jurisdictions this could be used as an alternative to a GP referral whereby the insurer uses Data Protection legislation to request, on behalf of the applicant, a full copy of medical records held by the GP. Use is controversial but perceived advantages are a fuller medical history and faster turnaround speed.

subsections.title: Surplus reinsurance
bullets: Under a surplus reinsurance arrangement, the long-term insurer will cede to the reinsurer all sums that exceed its retention on each individual life.

subsections.title: Switch
bullets: In the context of PMI, switch applies to the process whereby an existing policyholder, individual or group, changes insurer on renewal, possibly without further underwriting or with simplified underwriting ie a declaration of good health.

subsections.title: Technical Actuarial Standards (TAS) (SA1)
bullets: Technical Actuarial Standards are professional standards issued by the Financial Reporting Council, which cover both generic and specific areas of actuarial work.
Each TAS is that is within the scope of that TAS.

subsections.title: Technical provisions (SA1)
bullets: Under Solvency II, the technical provisions consist of the best estimate liability and the risk margin.
The latter is intended to increase the technical provisions to the amount that would have to be paid to another insurance company in order for them to take on the best estimate liability.
It therefore represents the theoretical compensation for the risk of future experience being worse than the best estimate assumptions, and for the cost of holding regulatory capital against this.

subsections.title: Telemarketing
bullets: This refers to the marketing of products via the telephone.
Usually enquiries are generated by direct marketing (see above) with the customer telephoning the advertiser (usually the insurance company).
The aim is to complete all application procedures over the telephone.

The policy will then be despatched together with a direct debit instruction for completion and a copy of the completed application for signature by the applicant confirming the answers given to the underwriting questions.
See Direct marketing.

subsections.title: Terminal illness
bullets: This is a medical condition that is expected to result in the person’s death within a short period, eg twelve months.

subsections.title: Tertiary care
bullets: Tertiary care is medical care that is only provided in specialist centres, eg neurosurgery, burns care.

It is usually on referral from secondary medical care personnel, by specialists working in a centre that has personnel and facilities for special investigation and treatment (eg treatment in a specialist neurological unit).
See Primary care, Secondary care.

subsections.title: Tied agents
bullets: Also known as appointed representatives, tied agents are salespeople who act more independently than a member of a direct salesforce (see above), but sell only the products of one insurance company, ie they are ‘tied’ to one company.
They enjoy more freedom in how they operate and may employ salespeople themselves to sell the products of the selected company.
See Direct salesforce.

subsections.title: Total (and) permanent disability (TPD)
bullets: This disability cover is often included within a critical illness product.

It is the permanency of the disability that distinguishes it from income protection cover, although ‘permanent’ needs to be defined carefully. ‘Total’ in practice means the failure of ability to perform a major or substantial part of the job or function.

subsections.title: Treating customers fairly (TCF) (SA1)
bullets: In many jurisdictions, insurers are expected to act honestly, fairly, and professionally with the best interests of its clients.
This is often referred to as treating customers fairly.
The interpretation of what constitutes treating customers fairly can vary significantly by jurisdiction.
In some jurisdictions, this concept is well established, and can be enshrined in local law.

For example in the UK the regulatory concept of Treating Customers Fairly (‘TCF’) enshrined within UK regulations is that a firm must pay due regard to the interests of its customers and treat them fairly.

subsections.title: Treaty
bullets: A treaty is a formal agreement between an insurer and a reinsurer setting out the terms of a reinsurance arrangement.
A treaty imposes an obligation on the reinsurer to accept automatically business ceded within the scope of the treaty.
It also usually imposes a similar obligation on the insurer to pass business to the reinsurer that falls within the scope and other terms of the treaty

subsections.title: Unemployment benefit
bullets: Where cover for unemployment is provided under an IP policy, it is usually for an amount lower than that which would be payable on incapacity.
The level is usually identical to that offered under the Housepersons benefit.

Some companies provide cover during unemployment but change to a definition of incapacity that requires confinement to the house (this may be extended to include a medical institution) or being unable to go outdoors without assistance.
The student should note that this is not unemployment insurance, which is payment if the insured becomes unemployed.

subsections.title: Unit rate
bullets: See bulk rate.

subsections.title: US GAAP (SA1)
bullets: This is the set of Generally Accepted Accounting Principles used in the USA and other countries.
It is based on historical cost, revenue recognition and matching principles, with standards being issued by the Financial Accounting Standards Board (‘FASB’).

subsections.title: Voluntary group
bullets: This describes a collection of policyholders for whom membership of a PMI scheme is voluntary.
It may include affinity groups or employment groups.
See Affinity group.

subsections.title: Waiting period
bullets: This is a feature adopted by friendly societies under which sickness benefits will not be paid for a specified period after the member first joins the society.
This waiting period may also be applied to any additional benefit from the date that the member buys the additional units of cover.
It might also be called a no-claim period.
See also Deferred period.

subsections.title: Waiver of premiums
bullets: This refers to the practice whereby the premium for an IP or CI policy is covered in addition to the main benefit provided by the policy in the event of disability.
It is possible that waiver of premium can work in different ways.

For example: 1.        companies that refund premiums proportionately for the period during which a claim is paid, or 2.        premium payments that fall due whilst the benefit is being paid could be waived.

subsections.title: Wearable technology
bullets: Wearable technology is often in the form of devices worn by a health and care insurance company’s policyholders, which allows the insurer to monitor the policyholder’s physical activity.
For example, some devices allow the tracking of the number of steps taken by an individual on a given day.
Some health and care insurers offer incentives and premium discounts for consumers that wear this type of technology.

subsections.title: World Health Organisation (WHO)
bullets: This is an autonomous health organisation set up in 1948, with the aim of assisting the population in the attainment of the highest possible level of health.
WHO proposes conventions, agreements, regulations and makes recommendations about international nomenclature of diseases, causes of death and public health practices.
It also develops, establishes, and promotes international standards concerning foods and biological, pharmaceutical and similar substances

subsections.title: Written premiums
bullets: In the case of regular premium business, written premiums are defined as the annualised amount of premiums for all policies commencing or renewing in a given period.
Single premiums are deemed wholly written.
